topics.title: Machining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Machining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MACHINING (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Machining is a controlled, subtractive manufacturing process in which a wedge-shaped cutting tool, possessing a definite geometry and made of a material harder than the workpiece, removes a predetermined volume of material in the form of chips from a workpiece to obtain the desired geometry, dimensional accuracy, and surface finish.*

The process relies on **relative motion** between the tool and the workpiece, generated by the machine tool. According to KTU Module 1 nomenclature, this relative motion comprises of:
1. **Primary Motion (Cutting Velocity, $v_c$)** – The main motion provided by the machine (usually rotary in turning, linear in shaping).
2. **Secondary Motion (Feed, $f$)** – The motion that advances the tool relative to the workpiece.
3. **Tertiary Motion (Depth of Cut, $d$ or $a_p$)** – The motion that provides the necessary thickness of material to be removed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The *Theory of Metal Cutting* forms the spine of Module 1. Every numerical problem in the KTU University Exam revolves around the Merchant's circle, chip thickness ratio, cutting forces, power, and machining time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Carpenter's Plane

Imagine a carpenter smoothing a wooden plank with a hand plane. The carpenter's wrist provides a **fast push** (primary motion), the plane advances a small distance per push (**feed**), and the blade protrudes a small height into the wood (**depth of cut**). A thin shaving (the *chip*) curls out, and the wood becomes flat.

In machining:
- The **carpenter** is the **machine spindle motor** (provides cutting velocity).
- The **plane** is the **cutting tool** (single-point or multi-point).
- The **wooden plank** is the **workpiece**.
- The **shaving** is the **chip**.
- The plank becomes the **finished product**.

This analogy helps clarify why machining is **subtractive** (material is removed) and **forced** (the tool must be harder than the workpiece, otherwise the workpiece would simply deform the tool).

## 1.3 Classification of Machining Processes

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 1 Domain:** Traditional / Conventional Machining processes. Non-conventional processes (EDM, AJM, USM, LBM) are typically covered in Module 4 or higher semesters. However, a brief classification is essential for context.

| **Criterion** | **Category A** | **Category B** | **Category C** |
|---------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| Energy Source | Mechanical | Chemical / Electrochemical | Thermal (EDM, LBM) |
| Tool Contact | Tool touches WP | Tool touches WP (ECM) | No tool contact (AJM) |
| Material Removal | Continuous chips | Ions / chemical etch | Melting / vaporization |
| KTU Example | Turning, Milling, Drilling | Chemical Milling | Wire-EDM, Plasma |

In the **2024 Scheme B.Tech syllabus**, Module 1 of *Machine Tools and Metrology* specifically lays emphasis on:
- **Turning** (Lathe) — most heavily tested.
- **Drilling** — geometry and time calculations.
- **Milling** — up-milling vs. down-milling, indexing.
- **Shaping / Planing / Slotting** — quick return mechanisms.

**Key Material Constants Used Throughout KTU Problems:**

- **Unit Power, $P_u$** (varies: **0.4–1.2 W·s/mm³** for steels, **0.15–0.4 W·s/mm³** for aluminium).
- **Specific Cutting Energy, $U_s$** (J/mm³).
- **Taylor's Tool Life Exponent, $n$** ≈ **0.20–0.25** (carbides), **0.3–0.5** (HSS).
- **Shear Angle Range, $\phi$** = **0° to 90°**, with practical optimum near **30°–45°**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Chip Formation

When a cutting tool engages the workpiece, the material ahead of the tool is subjected to intense compressive stress. The material fails along a **shear plane** (also called the **primary shear zone**), and the chip slides up the tool's **rake face**. This is the essence of **Ernst & Merchant's Theory of Orthogonal Cutting**.

### 2.1.1 The Step-by-Step Chip Birth Logic

1. **Tool Engagement:** The tool tip (cutting edge) first touches the uncut chip thickness $t_1$ of material.
2. **Plastic Deformation:** As the tool advances, the material in the shear zone yields plastically (strain hardening region).
3. **Shear Failure:** The material separates along a plane inclined at angle $\phi$ (the shear angle) to the cutting direction.
4. **Chip Sliding:** The separated material (chip) flows up the **rake face** of the tool, causing secondary deformation and friction.
5. **Continuous Chip Birth:** For ductile materials at recommended cutting speeds, this becomes a smooth, continuous ribbon.
6. **BUE (Built-Up Edge) Risk:** At low speeds, the chip welds to the rake face → degraded surface finish.

### 2.1.2 The 'Why' Behind the Shear Angle

> [!NOTE]
> **Merchant's Hypothesis (KTU Favourite):** The shear angle $\phi$ is such that the **work of plastic deformation in the shear plane is minimized**. This single hypothesis unlocks 70% of all theory questions in KTU university exams on this topic.

## 2.2 Chip Geometry Relationships — The Backbone of KTU Problems

Let:
- $t_1$ = Uncut chip thickness (mm)
- $t_2$ = Chip thickness after cut (mm)
- $r$ = **Chip thickness ratio** (also called *cutting ratio*)
- $\phi$ = Shear angle (°)
- $\alpha$ = Rake angle (°)
- $\beta$ = Friction angle (°)

The fundamental geometric identity (derivable from a simple velocity triangle):

$$
r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Chip Thickness Ratio `r` is the single most-tested parameter in KTU Module 1 numericals.** Always remember: $0 < r \le 1$. A higher `r` means thinner chips, indicating a sharp tool and efficient cutting.

## 2.3 Cutting Forces — The Merchant Circle

In **Orthogonal Cutting** (2-D model, single-point tool, cutting edge perpendicular to cutting velocity), three force systems act:

1. **Cutting Force, $F_c$** — in the direction of cutting velocity. *This is the largest force* and is used for **power calculation**.
2. **Thrust / Feed Force, $F_t$** — perpendicular to cutting velocity, in the plane of feed.
3. **Resultant Force, $R$** — combines $F_c$ and $F_t$.

**Force Decomposition along the shear plane:**

$$
F_s = F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi
$$

$$
N = F_c \sin\phi + F_t \cos\phi
$$

Where:
- $F_s$ = Shear force (along the shear plane)
- $N$ = Normal force on the shear plane

**Merchant's Relationship (derive-able but must be memorized):**

$$
\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}
$$

Where $\beta = \tan^{-1}\mu$ and $\mu$ is the **coefficient of friction** at the tool-chip interface.

## 2.4 Velocity Relationships (Merchants Triangle)

| **Velocity Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Vector Direction** |
|---------------------|-------------|----------------------|
| $V$ | Cutting velocity | Tool relative to WP (horizontal) |
| $V_s$ | Shear velocity | Along the shear plane |
| $V_{chip}$ | Chip sliding velocity | Along the rake face (upward) |

The vector triangle (which you must be able to draw blindfolded for the KTU exam) gives:

$$
V_s = \frac{V \cos\alpha}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

## 2.5 Power, MRR, and Unit Power

**Material Removal Rate (MRR), $Z_w$:**

For turning (most common KTU application):
$$
\text{MRR} = v_c \cdot f \cdot d \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]
$$

Where $v_c$ is in mm/s, $f$ in mm/rev, and $d$ (depth of cut) in mm.

**Power Consumed, $P_c$:**

$$
P_c = F_c \cdot v_c \quad [\text{W or J/s}]
$$

**Specific Cutting Energy (Unit Power), $K_c$ or $P_u$:**

$$
K_c = \frac{P_c}{\text{MRR}} \quad [\text{N/mm}^2 \text{ or W·s/mm}^3]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** $K_c$ is *not* a true material constant — it decreases with increasing $t_1$ (uncut chip thickness). This empirical relationship is often given as:
> $$K_c = K_{c1} \cdot t_1^{-m}$$
> Where $m$ is the **Kienzle exponent** ($0.18 < m < 0.30$ for steels).

## 2.6 Taylor's Tool Life Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite:** Appears at least once every KTU University Exam, either as a 3-mark definition or 14-mark derivation.

Frederick W. Taylor (1907) proposed that the relationship between cutting speed and tool life is:

$$
V \cdot T^n = C
$$

Where:
- $V$ = Cutting speed (m/min)
- $T$ = Tool life (min)
- $n$ = Taylor's tool life exponent
- $C$ = Taylor's constant (depends on tool, work, and cutting conditions)

Taking logarithms (for easy plotting):
$$
\log V = \log C - n \log T
$$

This gives a straight line on a log-log plot with **slope = $-n$**.

**Extended Taylor's Equation (with feed and depth of cut):**

$$
V \cdot T^n \cdot f^a \cdot d^b = C'
$$

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these 14 formulas cover >90% of Module 1 KTU numerical problems.**

| # | **Formula** | **Symbol Legend** | **Application** |
|---|-------------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | $r = t_1/t_2 = \dfrac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$ | $r$ = chip ratio, $\phi$ = shear angle, $\alpha$ = rake angle | Chip thickness |
| 2 | $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$ | $\beta = \tan^{-1}\mu$, friction angle | Merchant's relation |
| 3 | $V_s = V\cos\alpha / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$ | $V$ = cutting velocity | Velocity triangle |
| 4 | $F_s = F_c\cos\phi - F_t\sin\phi$ | $F_s$ = shear force | Force balance |
| 5 | $N = F_c\sin\phi + F_t\cos\phi$ | $N$ = normal force | Force balance |
| 6 | $P_c = F_c \cdot v_c$ | $P_c$ in Watts | Power |
| 7 | $\text{MRR} = v_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | $v_c$ in mm/s | Material removal |
| 8 | $K_c = P_c / \text{MRR}$ | $K_c$ in N/mm² | Unit power |
| 9 | $V \cdot T^n = C$ | $T$ = tool life (min) | Taylor's tool life |
| 10 | $n = \dfrac{\log(C/V_1) - \log(C/V_2)}{\log T_2 - \log T_1}$ | For two test conditions | Solve for `n` |
| 11 | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N} = \dfrac{L + l}{f \cdot N}$ | $L$ = length, $l$ = approach, $N$ = rpm | Machining time (turning) |
| 12 | $v_c = \pi D N / 1000$ | $D$ = diameter (mm), $N$ = rpm | Cutting speed |
| 13 | $t_1 = f \cdot \sin\kappa_r$ | $\kappa_r$ = side cutting edge angle | Effective chip thickness |
| 14 | $\cos\theta = \mu(\cos\alpha - \sin\alpha\cot\phi)$ | $\theta$ = chip-flow angle | 3-D cutting |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Industry 4.0 factories, the above relationships are encoded into **CNC controllers**:
- The **cutting force** $F_c$ predicted by Merchant's equations determines the **spindle motor torque** sizing.
- The **MRR** drives the **feedrate optimization** algorithms in adaptive control.
- The **Taylor's equation** powers the **tool condition monitoring** software in modern CAM packages.
- **Specific cutting energy** is fed to the **virtual machining simulators** (e.g., VERICUT) to predict cycle time and energy bills.

Thus, Module 1 isn't theoretical trivia — it's the *mathematical DNA* of every CNC code running in factories today.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Chip Thickness Ratio from the Geometry of Cutting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked (14-mark sub-question):** Derive the relationship $r = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$.

### **Setup**
Consider orthogonal cutting where the tool has rake angle $\alpha$ and the shear plane is inclined at angle $\phi$ to the cutting direction. The tool advances by uncut chip thickness $t_1$ to produce a chip of thickness $t_2$.

**Step 1 — Define the uncut and cut chip areas.**

Let the width of cut be $w$ (perpendicular to the page). Then:
- Uncut chip cross-section area: $A_1 = t_1 \cdot w$
- Cut chip cross-section area: $A_2 = t_2 \cdot w$

**Step 2 — Apply volumetric constancy (conservation of material).**

By plastic deformation, no material is created or destroyed (neglecting the small density change). Hence:
$$
A_1 = A_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_1 \cdot w = t_2 \cdot w
$$

Wait — strictly, the volume is conserved:
$$
t_1 \cdot w \cdot V = t_2 \cdot w \cdot V_{chip}
$$

So the **chip thickness ratio** is also equal to the velocity ratio:
$$
r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{V_{chip}}{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Draw the velocity triangle.**

From the Merchant's velocity diagram:
- $V$ is horizontal (cutting velocity).
- $V_{chip}$ is inclined at angle $(\phi - \alpha)$ from horizontal.
- $V_s$ closes the triangle, making angle $\phi$ with $V$.

**Step 4 — Apply trigonometry to the velocity triangle.**

$$
r = \frac{V_{chip}}{V} = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

$$
\boxed{r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *exact* expression most students are expected to derive in KTU ESE. Once derived, you can rearrange to find $\phi$ in terms of $r$ and $\alpha$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Merchant's Shear Angle Relationship

**Objective:** Show that $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$.

**Step 1 — Begin with the chip ratio equation.**

From Derivation 1:
$$
r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos\phi\cos\alpha + \sin\phi\sin\alpha}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $\cos\phi$:
$$
r = \frac{\tan\phi}{\cos\alpha + \tan\phi\sin\alpha}
$$

**Step 2 — Invert and isolate $\tan\phi$.**

$$
\frac{1}{r} = \cos\alpha \cot\phi + \sin\alpha
$$

$$
\cot\phi = \frac{1/r - \sin\alpha}{\cos\alpha} = \frac{1 - r\sin\alpha}{r\cos\alpha}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the Friction Angle definition.**

The friction angle $\beta$ relates to chip ratio through:
$$
r = \cos\alpha \left(1 - \tan\beta \tan\phi\right)^{-1}
$$

After substitution and simplification (the full algebraic manipulation is standard textbook material; see standard references on Merchant's analysis):

$$
2\phi = 90° + \alpha - \beta
$$

$$
\boxed{\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This elegant result tells us: *Increase the rake angle* $\alpha$ → **larger** $\phi$ → **thinner chip** → **less force**. *Increase the friction* $\beta$ → **smaller** $\phi$ → **thicker chip** → **more force*. This single equation guides tool designers worldwide.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Machining Time for a Turning Operation

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

- Workpiece length to be turned: $L$ (mm)
- Tool approach + overtravel: $l$ (mm) → total tool travel = $L + l$
- Spindle speed: $N$ (rev/min)
- Feed: $f$ (mm/rev)

**Step 2 — Feed per minute.**

The tool advances $f$ mm for every revolution. In one minute, at $N$ rev/min:
$$
\text{Feed per minute} = f \cdot N \quad [\text{mm/min}]
$$

**Step 3 — Total time = Distance / Feed rate.**

$$
T_m = \frac{L + l}{f \cdot N} \quad [\text{min}]
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of cutting velocity (often required).**

Using $v_c = \pi D N / 1000$, we get $N = 1000 v_c / (\pi D)$:
$$
\boxed{T_m = \frac{(L + l) \cdot \pi D}{1000 \cdot v_c \cdot f} \quad [\text{min}]}
$$

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A cylindrical workpiece of diameter 60 mm is being turned on a lathe. The depth of cut is 2 mm, feed is 0.25 mm/rev, and the cutting velocity is 150 m/min. Find (a) the spindle speed, (b) the material removal rate, and (c) the machining time for a 200 mm long workpiece (assume tool approach = 5 mm). Given specific cutting energy $K_c$ = 2000 N/mm², find (d) the power consumed.

**Part (a) — Spindle Speed $N$:**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot v_c}{\pi D} = \frac{1000 \times 150}{\pi \times 60}
$$

$$
N = \frac{150000}{188.496} \approx 795.77 \text{ rpm}
$$

**Part (b) — Material Removal Rate:**

Convert $v_c$ to mm/s: $v_c = 150$ m/min $= 150 \times 1000 / 60 = 2500$ mm/s.
$$
\text{MRR} = v_c \cdot f \cdot d = 2500 \times 0.25 \times 2 = 1250 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Part (c) — Machining Time:**

$$
T_m = \frac{L + l}{f \cdot N} = \frac{200 + 5}{0.25 \times 795.77} = \frac{205}{198.94} \approx 1.031 \text{ min}
$$

**Part (d) — Power Consumed:**

$$
P_c = K_c \cdot \text{MRR} = 2000 \times 1250 = 2{,}500{,}000 \text{ N·mm/s} = 2.5 \text{ kW}
$$

Equivalently, in horsepower: $P_c = 2.5 / 0.746 \approx 3.35$ HP.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: KTU Problem-Solving Strategy

| **Given in Problem** | **What to Find** | **Equation Cluster to Use** | **Common Pitfall** |
|----------------------|------------------|------------------------------|--------------------|
| $\phi$, $\alpha$ | Chip ratio $r$ | Eq. 1 | Forgetting to convert $\alpha$ to radians |
| $r$, $\alpha$ | $\phi$ | Rearranged Eq. 1 | Algebra sign error |
| $F_c$, $F_t$, $\phi$ | Shear force $F_s$, Normal force $N$ | Eqs. 4 & 5 | Forgetting $\phi$ in radians |
| $V$, $\phi$, $\alpha$ | Shear velocity $V_s$ | Eq. 3 | Mixing m/s and mm/s |
| $v_c$, $f$, $d$ | MRR | Eq. 7 | Units mismatch (m/min vs mm/s) |
| $P_c$, MRR | $K_c$ or $P_u$ | Eq. 8 | Confusing N/mm² with W·s/mm³ |
| $V_1, T_1, V_2, T_2$ | Taylor's `n` or `C` | Eq. 10 | Log base confusion |
| $D$, $v_c$ | $N$ | Eq. 12 | Forgetting the 1000 factor |
| $L$, $f$, $N$ | $T_m$ | Eq. 11 | Forgetting tool approach `l` |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Machining Process

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for the student:** Mermaid cannot realistically render a 2D physical schematic of an orthogonal cutting setup. The diagram below represents the **functional architecture** of the process — what flows into what, and what governs what. For the physical cutting diagram, refer to your KTU prescribed textbook.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Workpiece and Tool Geometry] --> B[Process: Relative Motion Activated]
    B --> C[Tool Engages Workpiece]
    C --> D[Plastic Deformation in Shear Zone]
    D --> E[Shear Plane Forms at Angle phi]
    E --> F[Chip Slides Along Rake Face]
    F --> G[Secondary Deformation and Friction]
    G --> H[Output: Continuous or Discontinuous Chip]
    H --> I[Energy: Heat Generated]
    H --> J[Output: Cutting Force F_c and Thrust Force F_t]
    H --> K[Output: New Workpiece Surface]
    I --> L[Coolant Application Loop]
    L --> C
    J --> M[Spindle Motor and Slide Drives]
    M --> B
    K --> N[Quality: Surface Finish and Dimensional Accuracy]
    N --> O{Acceptable?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Final Component]
    O -->|No| Q[Feedback to Cutting Parameters]
    Q --> A
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Orthogonal Cutting Model (Mermaid Block-Form)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Schematic Description (read aloud to visualize):** Imagine a horizontal bar (workpiece) moving leftward. A wedge-shaped tool is held stationary on top, with its **rake face** inclined at angle $\alpha$ from vertical. The tool tip just penetrates the top surface by depth $t_1$. A thin ribbon of metal — the chip — curls upward along the rake face, while the workpiece below the tool is now a fresh machined surface. The shear plane is an inclined line going from the cutting edge downward into the workpiece, making angle $\phi$ with the cutting direction.

The functional schematic of this in Mermaid form:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cutting Velocity V] --> B[Velocity Triangle]
    C[Shear Velocity V_s] --> B
    D[Chip Velocity V_chip] --> B
    B --> E[Resultant Vector R]
    F[Cutting Force F_c] --> G[Force Triangle]
    H[Thrust Force F_t] --> G
    I[Shear Force F_s] --> G
    J[Normal Force N] --> G
    G --> K[Resultant Force]
    style B fill:#e6f3ff
    style G fill:#fff0e6
```

## 4.3 Process-Control Loop for CNC Machining

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model] --> B[CAM Software]
    B --> C[G-Code Program]
    C --> D[CNC Controller]
    D --> E[Servo Drives and Spindle Motor]
    E --> F[Tool Engagement on Workpiece]
    F --> G[Sensor: Cutting Force and Vibration]
    G --> H[Adaptive Control Unit]
    H -->|Feedback Signal| D
    F --> I[Finished Component]
    style H fill:#ffe6e6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** Notice how the **Merchant-derived cutting force model** from Section 3 sits in the **Adaptive Control Unit (H)**. Real-time comparison of predicted vs. actual forces triggers automatic adjustment of $v_c$ and $f$ to maintain constant tool life and prevent breakage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the following terms used in machining with a suitable diagram:**
(a) Rake angle
(b) Shear angle
(c) Cutting velocity

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Rake Angle ($\alpha$):** The angle between the **rake face** of the tool and a plane perpendicular to the workpiece surface (or the reference plane), measured in the cutting plane.
>
> **(b) Shear Angle ($\phi$):** The angle between the **shear plane** (where the chip separates from the workpiece) and the **cutting velocity direction**. It is the single most important parameter in metal cutting analysis.
>
> **(c) Cutting Velocity ($v_c$):** The instantaneous relative velocity between the cutting tool and the workpiece surface, measured at the point of cut. For turning: $v_c = \pi D N / 1000$ (m/min).

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State Taylor's tool life equation. Explain the significance of the tool life exponent `n`.**

**Model Answer:**

> Taylor's tool life equation is:
> $$V \cdot T^n = C$$
> where $V$ is cutting speed (m/min), $T$ is tool life (min), $n$ is Taylor's exponent, and $C$ is a constant.
>
> **Significance of `n`:** The exponent `n` represents the **sensitivity of tool life to cutting speed**. A **small `n`** (≈ 0.2, for carbide tools) means tool life is *very* sensitive to speed — a small increase in speed drastically reduces tool life. A **larger `n`** (≈ 0.5, for HSS) means a more gradual decline. For maximum productivity, tools with low `n` are preferred when speed changes are minimized (CNC environments).

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module 1 of PCMET402 carries a 14-mark question in the ESE. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Derive the expression for chip thickness ratio `r` in terms of shear angle $\phi$ and rake angle $\alpha$ in orthogonal cutting. A cutting tool with a rake angle of 10° produces a chip thickness of 0.40 mm when the uncut chip thickness is 0.25 mm. Determine:**
(i) The shear angle $\phi$
(ii) The coefficient of friction $\mu$, if the friction angle $\beta = 30° + \phi/2$

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (as in Section 3.1):**
$$
r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}
$$

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $\alpha = 10°$, $t_1 = 0.25$ mm, $t_2 = 0.40$ mm.

**Step 1 — Compute chip thickness ratio:**
$$
r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.25}{0.40} = 0.625
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the geometric relationship:**

$$
0.625 = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - 10°)} = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos\phi\cos10° + \sin\phi\sin10°}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $\cos\phi$:
$$
0.625 = \frac{\tan\phi}{\cos10° + \tan\phi\sin10°} = \frac{\tan\phi}{0.9848 + 0.1736\tan\phi}
$$

Cross-multiply:
$$
0.625 \times 0.9848 + 0.625 \times 0.1736 \tan\phi = \tan\phi
$$

$$
0.6155 + 0.1085\tan\phi = \tan\phi
$$

$$
0.6155 = \tan\phi (1 - 0.1085) = 0.8915\tan\phi
$$

$$
\tan\phi = \frac{0.6155}{0.8915} = 0.6904
$$

$$
\boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.6904) \approx 34.62°}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute friction angle and $\mu$:**

$$
\beta = 30° + \phi/2 = 30° + 17.31° = 47.31°
$$

$$
\mu = \tan\beta = \tan(47.31°) \approx 1.083
$$

**Result:** $\phi \approx 34.62°$, $\mu \approx 1.083$.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A mild steel workpiece of 80 mm diameter is being turned on a lathe with the following data:** Spindle speed = 500 rpm, feed = 0.3 mm/rev, depth of cut = 2.5 mm, length of cut = 300 mm, tool approach = 10 mm, specific cutting energy = 2200 N/mm². **Find:**
(i) Cutting velocity in m/min
(ii) Material removal rate (MRR)
(iii) Machining time
(iv) Power consumed

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cutting velocity:**
$$
v_c = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 80 \times 500}{1000} = \frac{125664}{1000} \approx 125.66 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Step 2 — Material Removal Rate:**

Convert $v_c$ to mm/s: $v_c = 125.66 \times 1000 / 60 = 2094.4$ mm/s.
$$
\text{MRR} = v_c \cdot f \cdot d = 2094.4 \times 0.3 \times 2.5 = 1570.8 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Machining Time:**
$$
T_m = \frac{L + l}{f \cdot N} = \frac{300 + 10}{0.3 \times 500} = \frac{310}{150} \approx 2.067 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 — Power Consumed:**
$$
P_c = K_c \cdot \text{MRR} = 2200 \times 1570.8 = 3{,}455{,}760 \text{ N·mm/s} = 3.456 \text{ kW}
$$

In HP: $P_c = 3.456 / 0.746 \approx 4.63$ HP.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the chip formation process in metal cutting with a neat sketch. Discuss the different types of chips produced during machining and the conditions that favor each type.**

**Model Answer Outline:**

**Chip Formation Process (3 marks):**
1. As the tool advances, the material in front of the cutting edge undergoes **plastic deformation**.
2. When shear stress exceeds the shear strength, a **shear plane** is formed (inclined at angle $\phi$).
3. The chip slides up the **rake face**, experiencing **secondary deformation** and friction.
4. The result is a chip with thickness $t_2 > t_1$ (since material is compressed sideways as well).

**Types of Chips (4 marks):**

> **Type 1 — Continuous Chip:**
> - Forms in ductile materials (mild steel, aluminium) at high cutting speeds and proper rake angles.
> - Surface: Smooth, ribbon-like, no segmentation.
> - Indicates **good cutting conditions**.

> **Type 2 — Discontinuous Chip:**
> - Forms in brittle materials (cast iron, bronze) or at low speeds with high feed.
> - Surface: Segmented, saw-tooth.
> - Indicates material is fracturing rather than flowing.

> **Type 3 — Continuous Chip with Built-Up Edge (BUE):**
> - Forms at low cutting speeds in ductile materials.
> - A portion of the chip welds to the rake face → unstable → poor surface finish.
> - Solution: Increase speed, use coolant, or apply coatings (TiN, Al₂O₃) on the tool.

**Conditions Favoring Each:**
- Continuous: High $v_c$, positive rake, sharp tool, ductile material.
- Discontinuous: Low $v_c$, negative rake, brittle material, low temperature.
- BUE: Low $v_c$, inadequate coolant, high friction at the rake face.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**In an orthogonal cutting operation, the following data are observed:** Tool rake angle = 15°, cutting force = 1500 N, thrust force = 900 N, chip thickness ratio = 0.5. **Determine:**
(i) The shear angle
(ii) The shear and normal forces on the shear plane
(iii) The coefficient of friction at the tool-chip interface

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Shear angle from chip ratio:**

$$
r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.5 = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - 15°)}
$$

Following the algebra (as in Question A part a):
$$
\tan\phi = \frac{0.5 \cos15°}{1 - 0.5\sin15°} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.9659}{1 - 0.5 \times 0.2588} = \frac{0.4829}{0.8706} = 0.5547
$$

$$
\boxed{\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.5547) \approx 29.0°}
$$

**Step 2 — Shear and normal forces:**

Using $\phi$ in radians: $\phi_{rad} = 29.0 \times \pi / 180 = 0.5061$ rad.

$$
F_s = F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi = 1500 \cos29° - 900 \sin29°
$$

$$
F_s = 1500 \times 0.8746 - 900 \times 0.4848 = 1311.9 - 436.3 = 875.6 \text{ N}
$$

$$
N = F_c \sin\phi + F_t \cos\phi = 1500 \sin29° + 900 \cos29°
$$

$$
N = 1500 \times 0.4848 + 900 \times 0.8746 = 727.2 + 787.1 = 1514.3 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Friction angle and coefficient of friction:**

By definition, the friction force along the rake face is $F = F_c \sin\alpha + F_t \cos\alpha$:
$$
F = 1500 \sin15° + 900 \cos15° = 1500 \times 0.2588 + 900 \times 0.9659 = 388.2 + 869.3 = 1257.5 \text{ N}
$$

The normal force on the rake face is $N_r = F_c \cos\alpha - F_t \sin\alpha$:
$$
N_r = 1500 \cos15° - 900 \sin15° = 1500 \times 0.9659 - 900 \times 0.2588 = 1448.9 - 232.9 = 1216.0 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\mu = \frac{F}{N_r} = \frac{1257.5}{1216.0} \approx 1.034
$$

$$
\beta = \tan^{-1}(\mu) = \tan^{-1}(1.034) \approx 45.95°
$$

**Result:** $\phi \approx 29.0°$, $F_s \approx 875.6$ N, $N \approx 1514.3$ N, $\mu \approx 1.034$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign errors in friction/normal force signs** — students often forget that $N_r$ can be negative (tool digging into workpiece). Always re-check physical meaning.
> 2. **Radian vs. degree confusion** — in shear force equations, $\phi$ MUST be in radians only when used inside `sin`/`cos` calls of calculator programs. Most engineering calculators accept degrees, but if using Python/Octave, convert first.
> 3. **Forgetting tool approach (`l`) in machining time** — KTU examiners specifically test this. Loss: ~1 mark.
> 4. **Mixing up chip thickness ratio direction** — $r = t_1/t_2$ (uncut over cut), NOT $t_2/t_1$. Loss: ~1 mark.
> 5. **Unit mismatch in MRR and $K_c$** — $v_c$ in m/min gives wrong MRR by a factor of 60. Always convert to **mm/s** before multiplication.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Machining (use this in the last 10 minutes before the exam).**

- **Machining is subtractive** — material is removed as chips; energy is consumed as power; heat is generated.
- **Three motions** of machining: Primary (cutting velocity), Secondary (feed), Tertiary (depth of cut).
- **Chip thickness ratio** $r = t_1 / t_2 = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$; range is 0 to 1.
- **Merchant's relation** $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$ is the most-tested equation.
- **Cutting force $F_c$** is along velocity; **Thrust force $F_t$** is perpendicular. $F_c$ is always larger.
- **Shear force** $F_s = F_c\cos\phi - F_t\sin\phi$; **Normal force** $N = F_c\sin\phi + F_t\cos\phi$.
- **Power** $P_c = F_c \cdot v_c$ (use SI: $v_c$ in m/s, $F_c$ in N → $P_c$ in W).
- **MRR for turning** $= v_c \cdot f \cdot d$ — the "big three" of machining parameters.
- **Specific cutting energy** $K_c$ ranges from **1500 N/mm² (aluminium)** to **4000 N/mm² (titanium)**.
- **Taylor's equation** $VT^n = C$ — convert to log form to find `n` from two test conditions.
- **Tool life exponent `n`** is small for carbides (~0.2), large for HSS (~0.5).
- **Machining time** $T_m = (L+l) / (fN)$ — never forget the `l` (approach + overtravel).
- **BUE (Built-Up Edge)** forms at low speeds → poor surface finish → remedy: increase $v_c$, use coolant, coat tool.
- **Friction angle** $\beta = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$; high $\mu$ → high $F_t$ → tool dig-in → poor finish.
- **Increase positive rake** → larger $\phi$ → thinner chip → less force, less power, better finish.
- **Coolants** reduce tool temperature, increase tool life, and improve surface finish — but never use coolants on cast iron (fire risk + dust).

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Machining (PCMET402, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Machine Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Machine Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Machine Tool?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Machine Tool** is a power-driven machine, used to shape or machine metal or other materials by the controlled removal of unwanted material (chip formation), through a non-abrasive or abrasive process, in order to obtain a desired shape, size, and surface finish.

In the language of **NEP 2020 / OBE**, machine tools are the *backbone devices* of every manufacturing industry. Without them, mass production of accurate, repeatable components is impossible.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Carpenter's Workshop"

Think of a machine tool as a **smart carpenter's workstation**:

- A traditional carpenter uses a hand saw, chisel, and lathe wheel — slow, tiring, and inconsistent.
- A *machine tool* is the **motorized, precision-engineered, automated version** of those very tools.
- Just like a carpenter fixes wood in a vice and pushes a tool across it, a machine tool holds either the **workpiece** (e.g., in a lathe chuck) or the **tool** (e.g., in a milling spindle) and produces chips through relative motion.

| Manual Workshop | Machine Tool Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hand Saw | Power Hacksaw / Bandsaw |
| Hand Drilling | Pillar / Radial Drilling Machine |
| Hand Filing / Scraping | Milling Machine / Surface Grinder |
| Hand Lathe (wood) | Centre Lathe / CNC Turning Centre |

## 1.3 Classification of Machine Tools (Broad)

> [!NOTE]
> Classification helps engineers **select** the right tool for the job — a foundational decision in **Process Planning** (Module 2 / Module 3 of this course).

The two universal classifications adopted in KTU textbooks are:

**A. Based on the nature of machining process**
- **Conventional (Traditional) Machine Tools** — operated manually, e.g., Centre Lathe, Pillar Drill, Shaper.
- **Non-Conventional Machine Tools** — use energy other than mechanical chip cutting, e.g., EDM, Laser, Water-jet, USM.

**B. Based on type of motion / chip removal**
- **Turning machines** (lathe) — workpiece rotates, tool moves linearly.
- **Drilling & Boring** — tool rotates with axial feed.
- **Milling** — multi-point rotary cutter, workpiece or tool feeds.
- **Shaping & Planing** — straight-line reciprocating motion.
- **Grinding** — abrasive wheel removes micro-chips.
- **Sawing** — interrupted cutting by toothed blade.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Every KTU Student MUST Know

| Term | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Workpiece** | The raw material part to be machined | — |
| **Cutting Tool** | Tool with one or more cutting edges | — |
| **Chip** | The unwanted material removed | — |
| **Feed (f)** | Distance moved by tool per revolution/stroke | mm/rev or mm/min |
| **Depth of Cut (d)** | Thickness of material removed in one pass | mm |
| **Cutting Speed (V)** | Surface speed of workpiece at cutting zone | m/min |
| **Spindle Speed (N)** | Rotational speed of spindle | rpm |
| **MRR** | Material Removal Rate | mm³/min |
| **Machining Time (tₘ)** | Time required to complete one operation | min |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Basic Machine Tools — Working Principle, Kinematics & Operations

### A. Lathe (Turning Machine)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Centre Lathe** is the *mother of all machine tools*. Almost every other machine tool concept can be traced back to lathe kinematics.

**Kinematic principle:**
- The **workpiece rotates** about its axis (primary motion = rotation).
- The **tool moves linearly** along (longitudinal feed) and across (cross/transverse feed) the axis.
- Chip is formed by shear at the tool nose.

**Basic operations performed on a lathe:**
1. **Turning** — producing cylindrical surfaces.
2. **Facing** — producing flat surfaces perpendicular to axis.
3. **Taper turning** — by form tool, compound slide, or tailstock offset.
4. **Thread cutting** — using a single-point threading tool with synchronized feed.
5. **Drilling / Boring / Reaming** — using tools held in tailstock.
6. **Knurling** — producing a diamond/patterned grip surface.
7. **Parting / Grooving** — using a parting tool.

### B. Drilling Machine

**Kinematic principle:**
- The **drill rotates** (primary motion).
- The **drill feeds axially** into the stationary (or rotating) workpiece.

**Types:**
- **Sensitive / Bench drill** — small work, hand feed.
- **Pillar drill** — vertical column, hand feed, used in workshops.
- **Radial drill** — radial arm carries drill head; used for heavy/large jobs.
- **Gang / Multi-spindle drill** — multiple holes simultaneously.
- **Deep hole drill** — specialized for L/D > 10.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, **radial drilling machine** is the most-asked drilling variant — remember its distinguishing feature: **the drill head can be moved along a radial arm**, not fixed on a single column.

### C. Milling Machine

**Kinematic principle:**
- The **cutter rotates** (multi-tooth rotary cutting).
- The **workpiece is fed** linearly (or the table moves while the cutter rotates).

**Types:**
- **Column-and-knee type** (vertical / horizontal) — most common.
- **Bed-type / Fixed-bed type** — for heavy production.
- **Planer-type** — large workpieces.
- **CNC milling** — automated 3-axis / 5-axis versions.

**Milling operations:**
- **Plain (slab) milling** — surface parallel to cutter axis.
- **Face milling** — surface perpendicular to cutter axis.
- **End milling** — slots, pockets, profiles.
- **Side milling** — two peripheral cutting surfaces.
- **Straddle / Gang milling** — multiple cutters on one arbor.
- **Form milling** — contour using a shaped cutter.

### D. Shaper and Planer

Both use **reciprocating straight-line motion** of either tool or table.

| Feature | Shaper | Planer |
|---|---|---|
| Motion | Tool reciprocates; **workpiece stationary** | Table reciprocates; **tool stationary** |
| Size of job | Small / medium | **Large** workpieces |
| Stroke length | Short (typically < 1000 mm) | Long (up to several metres) |
| Cutting action | Only on forward stroke | Only on forward stroke |

**Quick-return mechanism (Whitworth / crank-and-slot)** ensures faster return stroke, saving time.

### E. Grinding Machine

- Uses an **abrasive wheel** as the cutting tool.
- Each abrasive grain acts as a microscopic single-point tool.
- Produces **very fine surface finish** and **high dimensional accuracy**.
- Types: **Surface grinder, Cylindrical grinder, Centreless grinder, Tool-and-cutter grinder, Internal grinder.**

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU, remember: **Grinding = precision finishing operation**, not bulk material removal. MRR is low, accuracy is high.

### F. Boring Machine

Used to **enlarge an already drilled hole** with high accuracy and fine finish.
- **Horizontal boring machine (HBM)** — spindle horizontal, table has X-Y movement.
- **Vertical boring machine (VBM)** — workpiece rotates on a large table; spindle vertical — used for large diameter work.

## 2.2 The Three Cardinal Speeds & Feeds

Every cutting operation is governed by **three inter-related parameters** which are universally tested in KTU numericals:

### (i) Cutting Speed (V)

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{m/min}$$

- $D$ = diameter of workpiece (or cutter) in **mm**
- $N$ = spindle speed in **rpm**
- The factor **1000** converts mm to m.

### (ii) Feed (f)

**Per revolution feed:** $f$ in mm/rev

**Feed rate (linear feed per minute):**

$$f_r = f \times N \quad \text{mm/min}$$

### (iii) Depth of Cut (d)

- In turning: radial depth (in mm).
- In milling: usually along the cutter axis (axial depth) or radial depth.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these. Numericals from this sheet appear every KTU exam cycle.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Variables Explained |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting Speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ = diameter in mm, $N$ = rpm |
| 2 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$ | rpm | solved from above |
| 3 | Feed Rate | $f_r = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f$ in mm/rev |
| 4 | MRR (Turning) | $\text{MRR} = \pi D \cdot d \cdot f_r$ | mm³/min | cylindrical removal |
| 5 | MRR (Milling) | $\text{MRR} = w \cdot d \cdot f_r$ | mm³/min | $w$ = width, $d$ = depth |
| 6 | MRR (Drilling) | $\text{MRR} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot f_r$ | mm³/min | hole cross-section |
| 7 | Power | $P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \cdot K_c}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW | $K_c$ = specific cutting energy J/mm³ |
| 8 | Machining Time | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r}$ | min | $L$ = tool travel length |
| 9 | Cutting Force | $F_c = K_c \cdot A$ | N | $A$ = chip cross-section |
| 10 | Average Chip Thickness | $t_c = f \cdot \sin\phi$ | mm | $\phi$ = approach angle (milling) |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU answer scripts, **always write the formula first, then substitute values, then units.** Skipping units costs 0.5–1 mark per sub-question.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Machine Tool | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| Lathe | Shafts, bolts, fasteners, automotive axles |
| Drill | Engine blocks, PCB holes, structural fabrication |
| Mill | Gears, dies, moulds, aerospace brackets |
| Shaper / Planer | Slideways, machine tool beds, large flat surfaces |
| Grinder | Bearings, cutting tools (HSS, carbide), finishing |
| Borer | Engine cylinders, large gear hubs |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Cutting Speed (V), Spindle Speed (N) and Diameter (D)

**Concept:** As the workpiece (or cutter) of diameter $D$ rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, a point on its surface travels along the circumference in one revolution.

**Step 1 — Circumference of a circle:**

$$C = \pi \cdot D \quad \text{[mm per revolution]}$$

**Step 2 — Distance travelled in one minute (at N rpm):**

$$\text{Distance per minute} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{[mm/min]}$$

**Step 3 — Convert mm to metres (divide by 1000):**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{[m/min]}$$

This is the universally accepted **KTU formula** for cutting speed.

**Step 4 — Rearranging for Spindle Speed $N$:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$

This form is what you use when the question gives you $V$ and $D$, and asks for $N$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Turning

**Concept:** In straight cylindrical turning, the tool removes an annular ring-shaped chip every revolution.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of chip (length = $\pi D$, thickness = $d$, width = $f$):**

$$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \quad \text{[mm²]}$$

**Step 2 — Volume removed per revolution:**

$$V_{rev} = A \cdot f = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f \quad \text{[mm³/rev]}$$

**Step 3 — Volume removed per minute (multiply by N):**

$$\text{MRR} = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f_r \quad \text{[mm³/min]}$$

since $f_r = f \cdot N$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Machining Time in Plain Turning

**Concept:** The tool travels along the workpiece length $L$ at a feed rate $f_r$.

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{[min]}$$

Where:
- $L$ = length of cut in mm (sometimes $L$ + tool approach + overtravel).
- $f_r$ = feed rate in mm/min.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU problems, students often forget to **add the tool approach length (≈ 2–5 mm)**. Examiners love this trap!

## 3.4 Worked Numerical 1 — KTU-Style (Turning)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** A mild steel workpiece of **60 mm diameter** is being turned on a lathe at a **cutting speed of 30 m/min, feed 0.2 mm/rev and depth of cut 2 mm.** Find: (i) Spindle speed (ii) Feed rate (iii) MRR.

**Given Data:**
- $D = 60$ mm
- $V = 30$ m/min
- $f = 0.2$ mm/rev
- $d = 2$ mm

### Solution — Part (i): Spindle Speed

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$$

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 60}$$

$$N = \frac{30000}{188.4956}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 159.15 \text{ rpm}}$$

### Solution — Part (ii): Feed Rate

$$f_r = f \cdot N = 0.2 \times 159.15$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 31.83 \text{ mm/min}}$$

### Solution — Part (iii): Material Removal Rate

$$\text{MRR} = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f_r$$

$$\text{MRR} = \pi \times 60 \times 2 \times 31.83$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 12000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \;\; (12 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min})}$$

## 3.5 Worked Numerical 2 — KTU-Style (Drilling)

> **Problem:** A **20 mm diameter drill** operates at **cutting speed 25 m/min** and **feed 0.15 mm/rev**. Find: (i) Spindle speed (ii) Feed rate (iii) MRR (iv) Machining time for a **40 mm deep hole**.

### Solution — Part (i): Spindle Speed

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 20} = \frac{25000}{62.8319}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 397.89 \text{ rpm}}$$

### Solution — Part (ii): Feed Rate

$$f_r = f \cdot N = 0.15 \times 397.89$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 59.68 \text{ mm/min}}$$

### Solution — Part (iii): Material Removal Rate (Drilling)

For drilling, the cross-sectional area of the hole is $\pi D^2 / 4$.

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot f_r = \frac{\pi \times 20^2}{4} \times 59.68$$

$$\text{MRR} = 314.159 \times 59.68$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 18747.6 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

### Solution — Part (iv): Machining Time

For drilling, the tool must travel the depth + drill point length. The drill point angle is typically **118°**, so extra length needed:

$$\text{Extra length} = \frac{D}{2} \cdot \cot(59°) = \frac{20}{2} \times 0.5543 \approx 5.54 \text{ mm}$$

So total travel $L = 40 + 5.54 = 45.54$ mm.

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{45.54}{59.68}$$

$$\boxed{t_m \approx 0.763 \text{ min} \approx 45.8 \text{ seconds}}$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical 3 — KTU-Style (Milling)

> **Problem:** A slab milling operation uses a **80 mm diameter cutter** with **8 teeth**, $V = 40$ m/min, $f = 0.1$ mm/tooth, $w = 60$ mm (width), $d = 3$ mm. Find MRR and table feed.

### Solution

Spindle speed first:

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 40}{\pi \times 80} = 159.15 \text{ rpm}$$

Feed per revolution:

$$f_{rev} = f_{tooth} \times z = 0.1 \times 8 = 0.8 \text{ mm/rev}$$

Feed rate:

$$f_r = f_{rev} \times N = 0.8 \times 159.15 = 127.32 \text{ mm/min}$$

MRR (milling):

$$\text{MRR} = w \cdot d \cdot f_r = 60 \times 3 \times 127.32$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 22917.6 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Basic Machine Tools

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Machine Tools] --> B1[Conventional]
    A1 --> B2[Non Conventional]
    B1 --> C1[Turning]
    B1 --> C2[Drilling and Boring]
    B1 --> C3[Milling]
    B1 --> C4[Shaping and Planing]
    B1 --> C5[Grinding]
    B1 --> C6[Sawing]
    C1 --> D1[Centre Lathe]
    C1 --> D2[Capstan and Turret Lathe]
    C1 --> D3[Automatic Lathe]
    C2 --> D4[Pillar Drill]
    C2 --> D5[Radial Drill]
    C2 --> D6[Horizontal Boring]
    C2 --> D7[Vertical Boring]
    C3 --> D8[Column and Knee Type]
    C3 --> D9[Bed Type]
    C3 --> D10[Planer Type]
    C4 --> D11[Shaper]
    C4 --> D12[Planer]
    C4 --> D13[Slotter]
    C5 --> D14[Surface Grinder]
    C5 --> D15[Cylindrical Grinder]
    C5 --> D16[Centreless Grinder]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Centre Lathe

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Main Power Supply 3 Phase AC] --> P2[Main Motor]
    P2 --> P3[Headstock Gearbox]
    P3 --> P4[Spindle and Chuck]
    P4 --> P5[Workpiece held in 3 jaw chuck]
    P6[Feed Rod and Lead Screw] --> P7[Carriage Assembly]
    P7 --> P8[Cross Slide and Compound Rest]
    P8 --> P9[Tool Post and Cutting Tool]
    P5 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Chip Formation at Tool Nose]
    P10 --> P11[Finished Cylindrical Surface]
    P12[Tailstock with Dead Centre] --> P5
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow of a Radial Drilling Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Base mounted to floor] --> M2[Vertical Column]
    M2 --> M3[Radial Arm swings 360 deg]
    M3 --> M4[Drill Head slides horizontally on arm]
    M4 --> M5[Spindle Motor and Quill]
    M5 --> M6[Drill Tool rotates]
    M6 --> M7[Quill feeds axially]
    M7 --> M8[Hole produced in workpiece]
    W1[Heavy / Large Workpiece] --> P1[Clamped on base table]
    P1 --> M8
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Milling Machine Kinematics

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Main Motor] --> A2[Spindle Drive]
    A2 --> A3[Cutter Rotates about Z axis]
    B1[Feed Motor X] --> B2[Table moves along X]
    C1[Feed Motor Y] --> C3[Table moves along Y]
    D1[Feed Motor Z] --> D4[Quill moves along Z]
    A3 --> OUT[Chip Formation on Workpiece]
    B2 --> OUT
    C3 --> OUT
    D4 --> OUT
    OUT --> FIN[Finished Profile Slot Pocket]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Kinematics Diagram — Turning vs Milling vs Drilling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[Turning Lathe]
        T1A[Workpiece rotates] --> T1B[Tool moves linearly]
    end
    subgraph T2[Milling]
        T2A[Cutter rotates] --> T2B[Workpiece or Table moves linearly]
    end
    subgraph T3[Drilling]
        T3A[Drill rotates] --> T3B[Drill feeds axially downward]
    end
    subgraph T4[Shaper]
        T4A[Tool reciprocates] --> T4B[Workpiece clamped stationary]
    end
    subgraph T5[Planer]
        T5A[Workpiece reciprocates on table] --> T5B[Tool stationary]
    end
```

## 4.6 Process Flow — From Raw Stock to Finished Part

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Raw Stock Rod or Billet] --> S2[Facing on Lathe]
    S2 --> S3[Rough Turning]
    S3 --> S4[Finish Turning]
    S4 --> S5[Drilling on Lathe Tailstock]
    S5 --> S6[Taper Turning]
    S6 --> S7[Thread Cutting]
    S7 --> S8[Grinding for Precision]
    S8 --> FIN[Finished Shaft Component]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a machine tool. List any four basic machine tools used in a mechanical workshop.**

**Model Answer:**
A machine tool is a power-driven machine used to shape metal or other rigid materials by controlled removal of chips to obtain desired geometry and surface finish.

Four basic machine tools:
1. **Centre Lathe** — for turning, facing, threading.
2. **Drilling Machine** — for producing holes.
3. **Milling Machine** — for flat surfaces, slots, contours.
4. **Grinding Machine** — for precision finishing.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners award full 3 marks only when the **definition includes chip-removal**, plus the **list with one-line use** of each tool.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between a shaping machine and a planing machine (any four points).**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. | Feature | Shaper | Planer |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reciprocating member | Tool | Workpiece (table) |
| 2 | Job size | Small to medium | Large and heavy |
| 3 | Stroke length | Short (< 1000 mm) | Long (> 1000 mm) |
| 4 | Application | Slots, keyways | Machine beds, slideways |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block-level architecture diagram, explain the **principal parts of a centre lathe** and state the function of each part.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mild steel shaft of **initial diameter 80 mm** is reduced to **76 mm** in one pass on a lathe. The **cutting speed is 35 m/min** and the **feed is 0.25 mm/rev**. Calculate:
(i) Spindle speed in rpm
(ii) Feed rate in mm/min
(iii) Material removal rate in mm³/min
(iv) Machining time for a cut length of 250 mm.

---

### 📝 Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Principal Parts of a Centre Lathe

The main parts and their functions are:

1. **Bed** — Heavy base casting with precision-ground V-ways (ways = sliding tracks) that support and align headstock, carriage, and tailstock.
2. **Headstock** — Contains the main spindle, chuck, and gear train; rotates the workpiece.
3. **Spindle** — Hollow shaft that holds the chuck; transmits rotary motion to the workpiece.
4. **Chuck (3-jaw / 4-jaw self-centering)** — Holds and grips the workpiece.
5. **Tailstock** — Supports the long end of the workpiece; carries dead centre or drill bit; can slide along bed.
6. **Carriage** — Moves longitudinally along bed ways; carries cross-slide, compound rest, and tool post.
7. **Cross-slide** — Mounted on carriage; moves tool perpendicular to spindle axis.
8. **Compound Rest** — Swivels to any angle for taper turning.
9. **Tool Post** — Holds the single-point cutting tool.
10. **Lead Screw & Feed Rod** — Transmit motion to carriage for threading and feed.
11. **Apron** — Houses feed mechanisms, half-nuts, and levers to engage/disengage feeds.
12. **Legs / Cabinet** — Support the bed and house the motor, coolant pump, and gearbox.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners expect at least **6–8 parts** with functions for full 7 marks. A neat labelled diagram (block-level) fetches **2 extra marks**.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $D = 80$ mm (initial diameter — used for cutting speed calculation as per convention)
- Final diameter = 76 mm
- $V = 35$ m/min
- $f = 0.25$ mm/rev
- Length $L = 250$ mm
- Depth of cut $d = (80 - 76)/2 = 2$ mm

**Step 1: Spindle Speed**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D} = \frac{1000 \times 35}{\pi \times 80}$$

$$N = \frac{35000}{251.327}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 139.26 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 2: Feed Rate**

$$f_r = f \times N = 0.25 \times 139.26$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 34.82 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**Step 3: MRR**

$$\text{MRR} = \pi \times D \times d \times f_r$$

$$\text{MRR} = \pi \times 80 \times 2 \times 34.82$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 17500.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**Step 4: Machining Time**

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{250}{34.82}$$

$$\boxed{t_m \approx 7.18 \text{ min}}$$

---

### 🔹 Question B (Internal Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a functional diagram the working principle of a **radial drilling machine**. Why is it preferred over a pillar drill for heavy workpieces?

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a **plain milling operation**, the cutter diameter is **100 mm**, has **8 teeth**, runs at a **cutting speed of 30 m/min**, with **feed per tooth = 0.12 mm/tooth**, **width of cut = 50 mm**, **depth of cut = 4 mm**. Compute:
(i) Cutter rpm
(ii) Table feed in mm/min
(iii) MRR in mm³/min

---

### 📝 Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Radial Drilling Machine

**Working Principle:**
The radial drilling machine has a **vertical column** mounted on a heavy base. A **horizontal radial arm** extends out from this column and can be **rotated 360° about the column** and **raised or lowered to suit the workpiece height**. A **drill head** carrying the spindle and motor is mounted on this arm and can be moved **horizontally along the arm**.

**Functional Flow:**
1. Workpiece (heavy/large) clamped on base table or floor plate.
2. Drill head positioned along radial arm directly over the hole location.
3. Spindle rotates the drill (primary motion).
4. Quill feeds downward (secondary motion) to produce the hole.
5. After one hole, arm swings to next hole position without moving heavy workpiece.

**Advantages over Pillar Drill:**
- **No need to reposition the heavy workpiece** for multiple holes — saves time and effort.
- Can drill holes **outside the column circle** (large jobs).
- Suitable for **large, heavy, awkward** components.
- Drill head can be moved in **3 planes** (radial, vertical, axial feed).

#### Part (b) — Milling Numerical

**Given:**
- $D = 100$ mm, $z = 8$ teeth
- $V = 30$ m/min, $f_z = 0.12$ mm/tooth
- $w = 50$ mm, $d = 4$ mm

**Step 1: Cutter rpm**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 100} = \frac{30000}{314.159}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 95.49 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 2: Feed rate**

Feed per revolution $= f_z \times z = 0.12 \times 8 = 0.96$ mm/rev

$$f_r = 0.96 \times 95.49$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 91.67 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**Step 3: MRR**

$$\text{MRR} = w \times d \times f_r = 50 \times 4 \times 91.67$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 18334 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Wrong diameter in MRR formula** — For turning, many students use the *final* diameter instead of the *initial* diameter. Always use the **diameter at which cutting speed is referenced** (usually the initial one).
> 2. **Unit conversion missing** — Always write mm³/min or cm³/min explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the drill point allowance** in drilling machining time — loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing shaper and planer** — examiners specifically test this.
> 5. **Forgetting to draw a labelled diagram** in part (a) questions — at least **2 marks are reserved for the figure**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — tick these before entering the exam hall.**

- ✅ A **machine tool** is a *power-driven chip-forming device* — definition is worth at least 2 marks in any Part B.
- ✅ **Three universal machining parameters** = $V$ (cutting speed), $f$ (feed), $d$ (depth of cut).
- ✅ Master formula $V = \pi D N / 1000$ and its three rearrangements.
- ✅ **MRR differs by operation:**
  - Turning → $\pi D d f_r$
  - Drilling → $(\pi D^2 / 4) \cdot f_r$
  - Milling → $w \cdot d \cdot f_r$
- ✅ **Lathe** = mother of all machine tools; **workpiece rotates**.
- ✅ **Drill** = tool rotates and feeds axially.
- ✅ **Mill** = multi-tooth cutter, table feeds.
- ✅ **Shaper vs Planer** = tool reciprocates vs table reciprocates.
- ✅ **Grinder** = abrasive wheel = precision finishing (not bulk removal).
- ✅ **Radial drill** = arm rotates 360° + head slides along arm; used for large/heavy jobs.
- ✅ **Specific energy ($K_c$)** = energy required to remove 1 mm³ of material — given in tables; remember units **N·mm/mm³ = J/mm³**.
- ✅ **Whitworth quick-return mechanism** = standard for shaper/planer.
- ✅ Always include **units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- ✅ In part (a) descriptive questions, **draw a labelled diagram** — it is worth 1.5–2 marks even if your text is average.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single and Multi-Point Cutting Tools, Tool Geometry and Materials, Mechanics of Chip Formation: Orthogonal and Oblique Cutting, Shear Angle, Velocity Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single and Multi-Point Cutting Tools, Tool Geometry and Materials, Mechanics of Chip Formation: Orthogonal and Oblique Cutting, Shear Angle, Velocity Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Single-Point Cutting Tool (SPCT)

A **Single-Point Cutting Tool (SPCT)** is a machining tool with **one effective cutting edge** that engages the workpiece at a time, removing material in the form of a continuous chip. Examples include the tool used on a lathe, boring bar, shaping machine, and planer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** "A cutting tool having only one cutting edge that performs the material removal action is called a Single-Point Cutting Tool. The geometry of this tool is the foundation for analyzing all other machining operations."

### Conceptual Analogy — The Carpenter's Chisel
Imagine a carpenter using a chisel to shave wood. The chisel has **one sharp edge** doing all the cutting — this is exactly how a lathe tool works. As the workpiece rotates and the tool moves sideways (feed), a thin ribbon of wood (chip) peels off, similar to how a potato peeler removes a continuous strip. The lathe tool is the metal-cutting cousin of the chisel.

---

## 1.2 Multi-Point Cutting Tool (MPCT)

A **Multi-Point Cutting Tool (MPCT)** has **two or more cutting edges** that engage the workpiece simultaneously. Each cutting edge removes a small portion of material (chip) during one revolution.

### Common Examples
- **Drill bit** — two cutting edges (lips)
- **Milling cutter** — multiple teeth around its periphery
- **Reamer** — multiple straight or helical flutes
- **Broach** — progressively rising teeth

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In MPCT, each tooth enters and exits the workpiece — this is called **interrupted cutting**, which imposes cyclic mechanical and thermal loads on the tool.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Pizza Cutter vs. the Chef's Knife
A single chef's knife makes one continuous slice (SPCT). A pizza cutter, however, has a rolling disc that has many "edges" effectively cutting as it rolls (similar to MPCT). Each rotation of a milling cutter is like a tiny pizza-cutter action happening many times per second.

---

## 1.3 Cutting Tool Geometry — The ASA Nomenclature (7 Elements)

The **American Standards Association (ASA)** system identifies a single-point tool by **seven basic elements** that completely describe its geometry:

| # | Element | Symbol | Description |
|---|---------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | **Shank** | — | The body of the tool held in the holder |
| 2 | **Nose** | — | Tip where major and minor edges meet |
| 3 | **Point** | — | The corner of the tool (often the nose itself) |
| 4 | **Face (Rake Face)** | — | Surface over which the chip flows |
| 5 | **Flank (Clearance Face)** | — | Surface facing the machined surface |
| 6 | **Base** | — | Bottom of the shank |
| 7 | **Cutting Edge** | — | Intersection of face and flank |

---

## 1.4 Tool Signature (Reference Tool and ASA System)

To define tool angles unambiguously, **three reference planes** are used:
- **Reference Plane** (horizontal plane through the tool point, parallel to the shank base)
- **Cutting Edge Plane** (tangent to the cutting edge at the tool point)
- **Working Rake Plane** (passes through the cutting edge and is perpendicular to the cutting edge plane)

### ASA Tool Signature Format
$$\alpha - \beta - \gamma - \phi - \lambda - \text{(nose radius)}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = Side clearance angle
- $\beta$ = Side rake angle (or back rake)
- $\gamma$ = Front clearance angle
- $\phi$ = Side cutting edge angle
- $\lambda$ = Inclination angle
- The sixth element = **nose radius** (e.g., $1/16$ inch)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always draw the tool reference system (three planes) before writing the signature — valuation rewards clear diagrams.

---

## 1.5 Orthogonal Cutting vs. Oblique Cutting

### Orthogonal Cutting (2-D Cutting)
- The **cutting edge is perpendicular to the direction of cutting velocity**.
- Tool lies in a vertical plane, workpiece velocity is horizontal.
- Only **two forces** are considered: $F_c$ (cutting force) and $F_t$ (thrust force).
- Used purely for **theoretical analysis** (Merchant's analysis).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine slicing cheese with a knife held perfectly vertical — only the side view matters. The "depth" of the cheese doesn't affect the slicing mechanics.

### Oblique Cutting (3-D Cutting)
- The **cutting edge is inclined** at an angle (inclination angle $\lambda \neq 0$) to the velocity vector.
- **Three force components** arise: $F_c$, $F_t$, $F_r$ (radial).
- All **practical machining operations** (turning, drilling, milling) are oblique.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Take the same cheese knife and tilt it 30° while slicing — now you feel a sideways push too. That sideways component is the **radial force** that did not exist in orthogonal cutting.

---

## 1.6 Mechanics of Chip Formation — Overview

When a tool advances into a workpiece, the material in front of the tool is subjected to intense **compressive stress**. This stress exceeds the yield strength of the material, causing **plastic deformation** along a narrow region called the **primary shear zone** (or shear plane).

The chip formed flows up the tool face, where **secondary deformation** occurs due to friction — this is the **secondary shear zone**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The chip formation process is fundamentally plastic deformation under extreme strain rates** ($\dot{\epsilon}$ of the order of $10^3$ to $10^6 \ \text{s}^{-1}$), which justifies using **flow stress ($\sigma_f$)** rather than ultimate tensile strength in chip-formation analysis.

---

## 1.7 Types of Chips Produced

| Chip Type | Description | Application |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Continuous Chip** | Long, ribbon-like, no break | Ductile materials, sharp tool, high speed |
| **Discontinuous Chip** | Segmented, separate pieces | Brittle materials (cast iron), low speed |
| **Continuous with Built-Up Edge (BUE)** | Weld-like deposit on tool | Sticking conditions, low speed, ductile metals |
| **Serrated (Semi-Continuous)** | Saw-tooth edges | High cutting speed, titanium alloys |

> [!WARNING]
> **BUE Exam Trap:** Many students confuse BUE with a "sharpened" tool. In reality, **BUE degrades surface finish and changes effective rake angle**, leading to unpredictable cutting forces. KTU valuation penalizes ignoring BUE in chip-type identification.

---

## 1.8 Cutting Tool Materials — Classification

The ideal tool material must be **hard, tough, wear-resistant, and chemically stable** at elevated temperatures.

| Material | Hot Hardness | Toughness | Cost | Application |
|----------|--------------|-----------|------|-------------|
| **High-Speed Steel (HSS)** | Low (~600°C) | High | Low | Drills, taps, low-speed tools |
| **Cemented Carbides (WC-Co)** | Medium (~900°C) | Medium | Medium | General turning, milling |
| **Ceramics (Al₂O₃, Si₃N₄)** | High (~1200°C) | Low | High | High-speed finishing of cast iron |
| **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)** | Very High (~1300°C) | Medium | Very High | Hardened steels (>50 HRC) |
| **Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD)** | Excellent | Low | Highest | Non-ferrous, composites, Al alloys |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Carbides dominate 70% of industrial cutting operations. Always mention **ISO classification (P, M, K)** for carbides in exam answers — it shows industrial awareness and earns easy marks.

- **P-series (Blue):** Steel
- **M-series (Yellow):** Stainless steel, austenitic
- **K-series (Red):** Cast iron, non-ferrous

---

## 1.9 Key Terminology Summary (Quick Glossary)

- **Feed (f):** Distance the tool advances per revolution (mm/rev)
- **Depth of Cut (d or ap):** Radial penetration (mm)
- **Cutting Speed (V):** Surface velocity at the cutting point (m/min)
- **Chip Thickness ($t_1$):** Uncut chip thickness = feed (for turning)
- **Chip Thickness ($t_2$):** Actual chip thickness after deformation
- **Chip Thickness Ratio (r):** $r = t_1 / t_2$
- **Shear Angle ($\phi$):** Angle of shear plane with cutting velocity
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 1 — SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Tool Geometry — Detailed Dissection

### 2.1.1 Rake Angle (Most Studied Angle)

The **rake angle** is the angle between the **rake face** and a reference plane (horizontal in ASA).

$$\text{Positive Rake} \Rightarrow \text{Sharp tool, less force, thin chip}$$

$$\text{Negative Rake} \Rightarrow \text{Strong edge, used for hard materials}$$

### Effects of Rake Angle

| Parameter | Increase Rake (+) | Decrease Rake (−) |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Cutting Force | Decreases | Increases |
| Chip Thickness | Decreases | Increases |
| Tool Strength | Decreases | Increases |
| Heat at Tool Tip | Decreases | Increases |
| Surface Finish | Improves | Worsens |

---

### 2.1.2 Clearance Angle

A small angle (typically $6°$–$15°$) that prevents the **flank** from rubbing against the machined surface.

$$\alpha + \beta = 90° \quad \text{(only for single-point tool in ASA)}$$

Where $\alpha$ = clearance, $\beta$ = rake (both measured from same reference).

---

### 2.1.3 Cutting Edge Angles

- **Cutting Edge Angle ($\phi$):** Angle between cutting edge and reference plane (lateral).
- **Lip Angle / Cutting Angle:** $\psi = 90° - \beta + \phi$
- **Nose Radius ($r$):** Roundness at the tool tip; larger nose = better finish, less stress concentration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** CNC turning inserts are graded by **rake (positive/negative), relief angle, chip-breaker geometry, and nose radius**. The selection determines chip control, tool life, and surface finish.

---

## 2.2 Mechanics of Chip Formation — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 The Primary Shear Zone

As the tool advances by an uncut chip thickness $t_1$, the material in front gets compressed. The chip forms by **plastic shear deformation** along a plane called the **shear plane**, inclined at angle $\phi$ (shear angle) to the cutting velocity direction.

### Chip Thickness Relationship

$$\boxed{r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \beta)}}$$

This is the **fundamental chip-thickness-ratio equation** derived from geometric analysis.

Where:
- $r$ = chip thickness ratio
- $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness (feed)
- $t_2$ = actual chip thickness
- $\phi$ = shear angle
- $\beta$ = rake angle

---

### 2.2.2 Shear Strain in the Shear Zone

$$\gamma = \cot \phi + \tan(\phi - \beta)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation shows that **higher rake angles and larger shear angles reduce shear strain**, lowering cutting force and energy. This is why positive rake is preferred when the tool material is strong enough.

---

### 2.2.3 Velocity Relationships in the Shear Zone

Using **velocity vectors** in Merchant's circle analysis:

$$\boxed{V = V_s \cos \phi + V_c \cos \beta \cdot \tan \phi \quad \text{(merging velocities)}}$$

The chip velocity $V$ along the tool face can be decomposed using the **incoming cutting velocity $V$** and **chip flow velocity $V_c$**:

$$V_s = \frac{V \sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \beta)} \quad \text{(velocity along shear plane)}$$

$$V_c = \frac{V \sin \beta}{\cos(\phi - \beta)} \quad \text{(chip velocity along tool face)}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| # | Formula | Meaning | Key Use |
|---|---------|---------|---------|
| 1 | $r = t_1 / t_2$ | Chip thickness ratio | Chip compression measurement |
| 2 | $r = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \beta)$ | Chip ratio via angles | Solve for $\phi$ |
| 3 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left[\frac{r \cos\beta}{1 - r \sin\beta}\right]$ | Shear angle from r | Inverse problem |
| 4 | $\gamma = \cot\phi + \tan(\phi - \beta)$ | Shear strain | Strain calculation |
| 5 | $\tau = \sigma_s \cos\beta / \sin\phi$ (approx.) | Shear stress | Force analysis |
| 6 | $F_s = \tau \cdot A_s$ | Shear force | $A_s$ = shear area |
| 7 | $A_s = t_1 \cdot w / \sin\phi$ | Shear area (w=width) | Geometric |
| 8 | $F_c = F \cos\beta - N \sin\beta$ | Horizontal force | Force decomposition |
| 9 | $F_t = F \sin\beta + N \cos\beta$ | Vertical thrust | Force decomposition |
| 10 | $V_s = V \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \beta)$ | Shear velocity | Velocity analysis |
| 11 | $V_c = V \sin\beta / \cos(\phi - \beta)$ | Chip velocity | Velocity analysis |
| 12 | $\eta = F_s V_s / (F_c V)$ | Cutting efficiency | Energy utilization |
| 13 | $P_c = F_c \cdot V$ | Cutting power (W) | Power estimation |
| 14 | $MRR = V \cdot f \cdot d$ | Metal removal rate | Productivity |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL:** In all the above, $w$ denotes **width of cut**, not the absolute value notation. Never use the symbol $\vert$ in formula tables — use parentheses or descriptive context instead.

---

## 2.4 Merchant's Circle Analysis — The Force Diagram

The four forces acting in orthogonal cutting, related through Merchant's circle, are:

| Force | Symbol | Acting On | Direction |
|-------|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Cutting force | $F_c$ | Tool | Along $V$ (horizontal) |
| Thrust force | $F_t$ | Tool | Perpendicular to $V$ |
| Shear force | $F_s$ | Shear plane | Along shear plane |
| Normal force on shear plane | $F_n$ | Shear plane | Normal to shear plane |
| Friction force | $F$ | Tool face | Along rake face |
| Normal force on tool face | $N$ | Tool face | Normal to rake face |

### Force Equilibrium Relations

$$\boxed{F = F_c \sin\beta + F_t \cos\beta}$$

$$\boxed{N = F_c \cos\beta - F_t \sin\beta}$$

$$F_s = F \cos(\phi + \beta) - N \sin(\phi + \beta)$$

$$F_n = F \sin(\phi + \beta) + N \cos(\phi + \beta)$$

---

## 2.5 Merchant's Equation for Shear Angle

By minimizing the cutting force $F_c$ with respect to $\phi$, Merchant derived:

$$\boxed{2\phi + \beta - \tau = 90°}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\phi = 45° + \frac{\beta}{2} - \frac{\tau}{2}$$

Where $\tau$ is the **friction angle** at the tool-chip interface.

$$\mu = \tan\tau \quad \text{(coefficient of friction)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Favorite:** Merchant's equation is the **most tested** topic in chip formation. The "minimum energy" principle is the conceptual basis. Always draw Merchant's circle and label all four forces for full marks.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

| Engineering Scenario | Formula(s) Used |
|----------------------|------------------|
| **CNC Tool Selection** | Merchant's equation → optimum rake |
| **Cutting Force Estimation** | $F_c$ decomposition |
| **Spindle Power Sizing** | $P_c = F_c \cdot V$ |
| **Coolant Flow Design** | Heat dissipation from shear zone |
| **CAM Toolpath Planning** | Feed & depth based on MRR |
| **Surface Finish Prediction** | Nose radius, feed, BUE modeling |

---

## 2.7 Cutting Tool Materials — Properties Deep Dive

### 2.7.1 High-Speed Steel (HSS)
- Composition: Fe + W, Mo, Cr, V (e.g., M2, M42)
- Hardness: **HRC 62–65**
- Hot Hardness: Retains hardness up to **600°C**
- Toughness: **Highest among tool materials**

### 2.7.2 Cemented Carbides (WC-Co)
- Tungsten Carbide grains bonded with Cobalt matrix
- ISO classification: **P (steel), M (stainless), K (cast iron)**
- Hardness: **HRA 89–94**
- Hot Hardness: Up to **900°C**
- Coating: **TiN, TiAlN, AlCrN** (CVD/PVD) for 3–5× tool life

### 2.7.3 Ceramics
- Al₂O₃-based: high temperature, wear-resistant, brittle
- Si₃N₄-based: tougher, for high-speed roughing
- **Cannot be used on steel** (chemical affinity causes crater wear)

### 2.7.4 Superhard Materials

| Property | CBN | Diamond (PCD) |
|----------|-----|---------------|
| Knoop Hardness | 4500 | 7000 |
| Chemical Stability with Fe | Excellent | Poor (reacts with Fe) |
| Cost | Very High | Highest |
| Best for | Hardened Steel (>50 HRC) | Al, Cu, composites |
| Thermal Conductivity | Moderate | Very High |

> [!WARNING]
> **Diamond cannot machine steel** — carbon dissolves in iron at high temperatures, causing rapid wear. This is a frequent KTU conceptual question.

---

## 2.8 The Effect of Cutting Parameters on Tool Life

### Taylor's Tool Life Equation

$$\boxed{V \cdot T^n = C}$$

Where:
- $V$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $T$ = tool life (min)
- $n$ = Taylor's exponent (typically 0.2–0.4 for HSS, 0.3–0.5 for carbide)
- $C$ = constant depending on tool, work, and conditions

$$\boxed{V_1^{n} \cdot T_1 = V_2^{n} \cdot T_2 \quad \text{(comparison form)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** Used in CAM software and process planning to estimate economic cutting speeds and tool-change intervals.
