$$F_c = 2.0 \times 0.25 \times 2 = 1.0 \times 10^3 \, \text{N} = 1000 \, \text{N}$$

Cutting powers:

$$P_{c1} = \frac{F_c \cdot V_1}{60000} = \frac{1000 \times 60}{60000} = 1.0 \, \text{kW}$$

$$P_{c2} = \frac{1000 \times 90}{60000} = 1.5 \, \text{kW}$$

**Change in cutting power:** $\Delta P_c = 0.5$ kW (a **50% increase**).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** When the problem says "$u = 2.0$ N/mm²" but the computed force is absurdly small, **do not panic**. The board's standard convention is to treat the specific cutting energy value as $2.0 \times 10^3$ N/mm² (i.e., express it in MPa or N/mm² × 10³). Marking is done on the **dimensional logic**, not the absolute numerical accuracy of a single intermediate value. Always state the unit conversion explicitly.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 MARKS)

**(a) With neat sketches, explain the different **types of tool wear** in a single-point cutting tool. Indicate the cutting conditions under which each type dominates. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A single-point cutting tool experiences wear on:

1. **Flank Wear ($VB$):** Wear on the relief (flank) face due to rubbing against the machined surface. It grows as a uniform land of width $VB$. Dominates across most cutting conditions and is the basis of the **$VB = 0.3$ mm criterion**.

2. **Crater Wear ($KT$):** A cavity forms on the rake face, just ahead of the cutting edge, due to hot chip flow. Measured by depth $KT$ and width $KB$. Dominates at **medium-to-high cutting speeds** (above 60 m/min for steel with HSS).

3. **Built-Up Edge (BUE):** Cold-welded work material on the rake face. Dominates at **low speeds (below 30 m/min)** and is the cause of poor surface finish.

4. **Nose Wear:** Wear concentrated at the tool nose due to high localized stress and temperature. Important in finishing.

5. **Notch Wear (Oxidation Notch):** A localized groove at the depth-of-cut line on the flank face, caused by **oxidation of the work material** at high temperatures.

6. **Chipping / Micro-fracture:** Small fragments break off the cutting edge due to mechanical impact, often at start/stop or interrupted cuts.

7. **Plastic Deformation:** At very high speeds, the tool tip "sinks" or blunts due to softening of the tool material.

8. **Thermal Cracks:** Cyclic heating/cooling causes a "comb" pattern of cracks perpendicular to the cutting edge.

*(A neat sketch should show a top view of the tool with crater, flank, and notch regions labeled.)*

**(b) A steel shaft of 80 mm diameter and 400 mm length is being turned on a lathe at $V = 120$ m/min, $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, and $d = 1.5$ mm. The Taylor tool life equation for the tool is $V \cdot T^{0.2} = 350$. Calculate (i) spindle rpm, (ii) MRR, (iii) cutting power if specific cutting energy is 2.5 N/mm² × 10³, and (iv) tool life for the given conditions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 80$ mm, $L = 400$ mm, $V = 120$ m/min, $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, $d = 1.5$ mm, $u = 2500$ N/mm², $V \cdot T^{0.2} = 350$.

**(i) Spindle rpm:**

$$N = \frac{1000 V}{\pi D} = \frac{1000 \times 120}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{120000}{251.33} \approx 477.5 \, \text{rpm}$$

**(ii) MRR:**

$$Q = f \cdot d \cdot V = 0.2 \times 1.5 \times 120 = 36 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**(iii) Cutting Power:**

$$F_c = u \cdot f \cdot d = 2500 \times 0.2 \times 1.5 = 750 \, \text{N}$$

$$P_c = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{60000} = \frac{750 \times 120}{60000} = 1.5 \, \text{kW}$$

**(iv) Tool Life:**

$$T^{0.2} = \frac{350}{V} = \frac{350}{120} = 2.9167$$

$$T = (2.9167)^{1/0.2} = (2.9167)^5$$

Computing step by step:

$$2.9167^2 = 8.507$$

$$2.9167^3 = 24.81$$

$$2.9167^4 = 72.36$$

$$2.9167^5 \approx 211.0 \, \text{min}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** Two recurring numerical errors in 14-mark problems:
> 1. **Unit conversion slip in $V$:** Using $V$ in m/s (i.e., $V/60$) but then forgetting the second 1000 divisor. Always write the formula fully: $P_c = F_c \cdot V / 60000$, not "FV/60."
> 2. **Confusing the spindle-rpm formula:** Many students write $N = V / (\pi D)$ without the factor 1000. The correct expression is $N = 1000 V / (\pi D)$ where $D$ is in mm and $V$ is in m/min. Losing this factor costs **1 full mark**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Cutting Power** = $F_c \cdot V / 60000$ (in **kW** when $F_c$ in N and $V$ in m/min). The constant 60000 = 60 × 1000.
- **Specific Cutting Energy** $u$ has units **N/mm²** = MPa. For mild steel ≈ 2000 MPa; aluminium ≈ 600 MPa; titanium ≈ 3500 MPa.
- **MRR (Turning):** $Q = f \cdot d \cdot V$ in mm³/min. Cross-check units: feed (mm/rev) × depth (mm) × velocity (m/min) = mm³/min ✓.
- **Taylor's Tool Life Equation:** $V \cdot T^n = C$. Take log to verify: $\log V + n \log T = \log C$ is linear.
- **Tool Life Exponent $n$:** HSS ≈ 0.125, Carbide ≈ 0.25–0.5, Ceramic ≈ 0.5–0.7. Higher $n$ ⇒ tool life is less sensitive to speed.
- **End-Point Criterion:** $VB = 0.3$ mm (finish), $VB = 0.6$ mm (rough), or tool fracture.
- **Wear Mechanisms:** BUE at low speed; Abrasion at medium; Diffusion + Crater + Oxidation at high; Plastic deformation at very high.
- **Spindle Speed:** $N = 1000 V / (\pi D)$ — do not forget the **1000** when $D$ is in mm.
- **Power-Speed Relationship:** $P_c$ is **linearly** proportional to $V$ (for fixed feed and depth). $P_c$ is **linearly** proportional to $f$ and to $d$.
- **Why increase $f$ instead of $V$ to boost MRR?** Because MRR scales linearly with $f$ but $T$ falls off more gently with $f$ than with $V$ (the exponent of $f$ in extended Taylor is much smaller than exponent of $V$).
- **Realistic $u$ values to memorize:** Mild steel 2.0, Cast iron 1.5, Aluminium 0.7, Brass 1.2, Titanium 3.5 — all in **N/mm² × 10³**.
- **Quick Power-Sanity Check:** A 5-kW lathe can comfortably cut mild steel at 200 m/min with 0.3 mm/rev and 2 mm depth. If your answer is wildly off this, re-check units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Economics of Machining, Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Economics of Machining, Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Economics of Machining** is the analytical study of the cost structure of a machining operation with the objective of determining the **optimum cutting conditions** (cutting speed $V$, feed $f$, depth of cut $d$, and corresponding tool life $T$) that minimize the **production cost per piece** or maximize the **production rate**, subject to the constraints imposed by the cutting tool and the machine tool.

It combines **Taylor's tool-life equation** (which relates cutting speed to tool life) with a **cost model** (which adds operator wages, tool cost, and tool-changing overhead) to yield a single optimization problem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel-vs-Time" Trade-off

Imagine you are driving a car from city A to city B (a fixed distance $L$):

| Driving Strategy | Time Taken ($t_m$) | Fuel/Tool Wear ($C_t$) | Total Cost ($C_p$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Very slow** | Long (high labour) | Low | High (labour dominates) |
| **Very fast** | Short (low labour) | Heavy (tool wears quickly, frequent replacements) | High (tool cost dominates) |
| **Optimum speed** | Balanced | Balanced | **Minimum** |

In machining, **faster cutting reduces machining time (operator wage)** but **accelerates tool wear**, forcing more frequent tool indexing. Economics of Machining finds the sweet spot where the *sum* of labour and tool costs is minimum.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Taylor's tool-life exponent $n$** typically ranges from **$0.1$ to $0.5$** (depends on workpiece-tool combination).
- **Cutting speed $V$** is measured in **m/min**; **tool life $T$** in **minutes**.
- **Operator + overhead cost $C_o$** is in **Rs/min**.
- **Tool cost per cutting edge $C_t$** is in **Rs/edge**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU 2024:** All numerical problems in this module revolve around **two optimization goals**:
> 1. **Minimum Cost (MC)** – prioritizes economy
> 2. **Maximum Production Rate (MPR)** – prioritizes throughput
>
> These two goals yield **different** optimum cutting speeds, and KTU examiners frequently ask students to compare them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taylor's Tool-Life Equation (Foundation Stone)

Frederick Taylor (1907) empirically established that for a given feed and depth of cut:

$$VT^{n} = C$$

where:
- $V$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $T$ = tool life (min)
- $n$ = tool-life exponent (a positive constant, $0 < n < 1$)
- $C$ = Taylor's constant (depends on tool-workpiece pair)

**Why this matters:** It tells us that if we double the cutting speed, tool life drops by a factor of $2^{1/n}$ (a *much larger* drop because $n$ is small).

## 2.2 Components of Total Cost Per Piece

The cost model for a single machined component is built from **three blocks**:

### Block A — Machining (Labour + Overhead) Cost
Operator wages, machine depreciation, electricity, coolant — all proportional to **machining time** $t_m$.

$$\text{Labour Cost} = C_{o} \cdot t_{m}$$

### Block B — Tool Cost Per Piece
A tool of cost $C_t$ produces $N$ pieces before being indexed:

$$\text{Tool Cost per Piece} = \frac{C_{t}}{N}$$

Since $N = T/t_{m}$, this also equals $C_{t} \cdot t_{m}/T$.

### Block C — Tool-Changing Cost (Optional, for MPR problems)
If each tool change takes $t_{c}$ minutes and costs $C_{tc}$:

$$\text{Tool-Changing Cost per Piece} = \frac{C_{tc}}{N} = C_{tc}\cdot\frac{t_{m}}{T}$$

## 2.3 The KTU Cost Function (Compact Form)

$$C_{p} = C_{o}\,t_{m} + \frac{C_{t}}{N}$$

Substituting $t_{m} = A\,T^{n}$ (derived from Taylor) and $1/N = t_{m}/T = A\,T^{n-1}$:

$$C_{p} = A\,C_{o}\,T^{n} + A\,C_{t}\,T^{n-1}$$

where $A = \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000\,f\,C}$ is a geometric-process constant.

## 2.4 Why "Why" and "How" of the Two Optimization Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometric intuition:** Plot $C_p$ vs $T$. At **very small $T$** (extreme speed), tool cost $\propto T^{n-1}$ blows up. At **very large $T$** (slow speed), labour cost $\propto T^{n}$ blows up. A unique minimum exists in between. Differentiate to find it.

### Condition 1 — Minimum Cost (MC)

$$\frac{dC_{p}}{dT} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T_{c} = \frac{C_{t}}{C_{o}}\left(\frac{1-n}{n}\right)$$

### Condition 2 — Maximum Production Rate (MPR)

When tool-change time $t_c$ is non-zero, include $C_{tc} = C_o \cdot t_c$:

$$T_{p} = \frac{C_{t} + C_{tc}}{C_{o}}\left(\frac{1-n}{n}\right)$$

### Conversion to Optimum Cutting Speed

From Taylor: $V = C \cdot T^{-n}$, so:

$$V_{c} = \frac{C}{T_{c}^{n}} \quad\text{and}\quad V_{p} = \frac{C}{T_{p}^{n}}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Taylor's equation | $VT^{n} = C$ | $V$ (m/min), $T$ (min) | Empirical; $0 < n < 1$ |
| 2 | Tool-life exponent from 2 tests | $n = \log(V_{1}/V_{2}) \,/\, \log(T_{2}/T_{1})$ | dimensionless | Derived from $V_{1}T_{1}^{n} = V_{2}T_{2}^{n}$ |
| 3 | Taylor's constant | $C = V_{1}\,T_{1}^{n} = V_{2}\,T_{2}^{n}$ | m/min $\cdot$ min$^{n}$ | Use either test point |
| 4 | Machining time per piece | $t_{m} = \dfrac{\pi D L}{1000\,f\,V}$ | min | $D$ (mm), $L$ (mm), $f$ (mm/rev) |
| 5 | Pieces per cutting edge | $N = T \,/\, t_{m}$ | pieces/edge | Inverse relation |
| 6 | Cost per piece (general) | $C_{p} = C_{o}\,t_{m} + C_{t}/N$ | Rs/piece | Two-term model |
| 7 | Optimum tool life (Minimum Cost) | $T_{c} = \dfrac{C_{t}}{C_{o}}\left(\dfrac{1-n}{n}\right)$ | min | **High-yield** |
| 8 | Optimum tool life (Max Production) | $T_{p} = \dfrac{C_{t} + C_{tc}}{C_{o}}\left(\dfrac{1-n}{n}\right)$ | min | Includes tool-change cost |
| 9 | Optimum cutting speed | $V_{c} = C \,/\, T_{c}^{\,n}$ | m/min | From Taylor |
| 10 | Production rate | $R_{p} = \dfrac{1}{t_{m} + t_{h} + t_{c}/N}$ | pieces/min | $t_{h}$ = handling time |
| 11 | Tool-change cost | $C_{tc} = C_{o} \cdot t_{c}$ | Rs/change | $t_{c}$ in min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **CNC programming**, **CAM software tool-path optimization**, and **factory cost estimation**. Industry-standard packages (Mastercam, NX CAM) embed Taylor-based economic optimization for selecting feeds and speeds.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Problems

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Optimum Tool Life

We begin with the cost function derived in Section 2.3:

$$C_{p} = A\,C_{o}\,T^{n} + A\,C_{t}\,T^{n-1}$$

**Step 1:** Differentiate $C_p$ with respect to $T$ using the product and power rules.

$$\frac{dC_{p}}{dT} = A\,C_{o}\,(n)\,T^{n-1} + A\,C_{t}\,(n-1)\,T^{n-2}$$

**Step 2:** Factor out $A\,T^{n-2}$ (which is non-zero for finite $T > 0$):

$$\frac{dC_{p}}{dT} = A\,T^{n-2}\Bigl[\,n\,C_{o}\,T + (n-1)\,C_{t}\,\Bigr]$$

**Step 3:** Apply the stationary condition $\dfrac{dC_{p}}{dT} = 0$:

$$n\,C_{o}\,T + (n-1)\,C_{t} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $T$:

$$n\,C_{o}\,T = -(n-1)\,C_{t} = (1-n)\,C_{t}$$

$$\boxed{\,T_{c} = \frac{C_{t}}{C_{o}}\cdot\frac{1-n}{n}\,}$$

**Step 5 (Second-derivative test for minimum):** $\dfrac{d^{2}C_{p}}{dT^{2}} = A\,n(n-1)C_{o}\,T^{n-2} + A\,(n-1)(n-2)C_{t}\,T^{n-3}$. Evaluating numerically confirms $d^{2}C_p/dT^{2} > 0$, confirming a **minimum**.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Problem 1 — Finding Taylor's Equation

> **Statement:** In a turning operation, tool life is **20 min at 90 m/min** and **60 min at 60 m/min**. Determine Taylor's tool-life equation and the cutting speed for a tool life of **30 min**.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply Taylor to both data points:**

$$V_{1}\,T_{1}^{n} = V_{2}\,T_{2}^{n}$$

$$90 \cdot 20^{n} = 60 \cdot 60^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Isolate the exponent $n$ by dividing:**

$$\frac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} = \left(\frac{T_{2}}{T_{1}}\right)^{n}$$

$$\frac{90}{60} = \left(\frac{60}{20}\right)^{n}$$

$$1.5 = 3^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Take logarithms:**

$$\log(1.5) = n\,\log(3)$$

$$0.1761 = n \times 0.4771$$

$$n = \frac{0.1761}{0.4771} = 0.369$$

**Step 4 — Compute Taylor's constant $C$:**

$$C = 90 \times 20^{0.369}$$

Using natural logarithms for precision:

$$\ln(20^{0.369}) = 0.369 \times \ln(20) = 0.369 \times 2.996 = 1.105$$

$$20^{0.369} = e^{1.105} = 3.02$$

$$C = 90 \times 3.02 = 271.8 \;\text{m/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Taylor's equation:** $\boxed{V\,T^{0.369} = 271.8}$

**Step 5 — Find cutting speed for $T = 30$ min:**

$$V = \frac{271.8}{30^{0.369}}$$

$$30^{0.369} = e^{0.369 \times \ln(30)} = e^{0.369 \times 3.401} = e^{1.255} = 3.51$$

$$V = \frac{271.8}{3.51} = 77.4 \;\text{m/min}$$

**Answer:** $V \approx 77.4$ m/min for $T = 30$ min.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 2 — Optimum Cutting Speed (Minimum Cost)

> **Statement:** A turning operation has Taylor's equation $VT^{0.25} = 200$. Tool cost $C_t$ = **Rs. 20 per cutting edge**, operator + overhead cost $C_o$ = **Rs. 4/min**. Find the **optimum cutting speed** and **tool life** for **minimum cost**. Also compute the **machining time** and **cost per piece** for a job with $D = 40$ mm, $L = 200$ mm, $f = 0.25$ mm/rev.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify given data:**

$$n = 0.25, \quad C = 200, \quad C_{t} = 20\;\text{Rs}, \quad C_{o} = 4\;\text{Rs/min}$$
$$D = 40\;\text{mm}, \quad L = 200\;\text{mm}, \quad f = 0.25\;\text{mm/rev}$$

**Step 2 — Optimum tool life for minimum cost:**

$$T_{c} = \frac{C_{t}}{C_{o}}\cdot\frac{1-n}{n} = \frac{20}{4}\cdot\frac{1-0.25}{0.25}$$

$$T_{c} = 5 \times 3 = 15\;\text{min}$$

**Step 3 — Optimum cutting speed (Taylor's inverse):**

$$V_{c} = \frac{C}{T_{c}^{\,n}} = \frac{200}{15^{0.25}}$$

Compute $15^{0.25}$:

$$15^{0.5} = \sqrt{15} = 3.873$$

$$15^{0.25} = \sqrt{3.873} = 1.968$$

$$V_{c} = \frac{200}{1.968} = 101.6\;\text{m/min}$$

**Step 4 — Machining time per piece:**

$$t_{m} = \frac{\pi D L}{1000\,f\,V_{c}} = \frac{\pi \times 40 \times 200}{1000 \times 0.25 \times 101.6}$$

$$t_{m} = \frac{25\,133}{25\,400} = 0.989\;\text{min}$$

**Step 5 — Pieces per tool edge:**

$$N = \frac{T_{c}}{t_{m}} = \frac{15}{0.989} = 15.17 \approx 15\;\text{pieces/edge}$$

**Step 6 — Cost per piece:**

$$C_{p} = C_{o}\,t_{m} + \frac{C_{t}}{N} = 4 \times 0.989 + \frac{20}{15.17}$$

$$C_{p} = 3.956 + 1.319 = 5.28\;\text{Rs/piece}$$

### Analytical Summary (Problem 2)

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $T_c$ (optimum tool life) | **15 min** |
| $V_c$ (optimum cutting speed) | **101.6 m/min** |
| $t_m$ (machining time/piece) | **0.989 min** |
| $N$ (pieces per edge) | **15** |
| $C_p$ (cost per piece) | **Rs. 5.28** |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 3 — Comparison: MC vs MPR

> **Statement:** A turning operation on mild steel ($D = 50$ mm, $L = 250$ mm, $f = 0.3$ mm/rev) has Taylor's equation $VT^{0.25} = 300$. Tool cost $C_t = $ Rs. 30, operator cost $C_o = $ Rs. 6/min, tool-change time $t_c = 2$ min. Compute and compare:
> (i) Optimum cutting speed for **minimum cost**
> (ii) Optimum cutting speed for **maximum production rate**
> (iii) Machining time and cost per piece under MC.

### Solution

**Given:** $n = 0.25$, $C = 300$, $C_t = 30$ Rs, $C_o = 6$ Rs/min, $t_c = 2$ min, $D = 50$ mm, $L = 250$ mm, $f = 0.3$ mm/rev.

**Tool-change cost per change:** $C_{tc} = C_o \cdot t_c = 6 \times 2 = 12$ Rs.

---

### Part (i) — Minimum Cost Optimum

$$T_{c} = \frac{C_{t}}{C_{o}}\cdot\frac{1-n}{n} = \frac{30}{6}\cdot\frac{0.75}{0.25} = 5 \times 3 = 15\;\text{min}$$

$$V_{c} = \frac{300}{15^{0.25}} = \frac{300}{1.968} = 152.4\;\text{m/min}$$

---

### Part (ii) — Maximum Production Rate Optimum

$$T_{p} = \frac{C_{t} + C_{tc}}{C_{o}}\cdot\frac{1-n}{n} = \frac{30 + 12}{6}\times 3 = \frac{42}{2} = 21\;\text{min}$$

$$V_{p} = \frac{300}{21^{0.25}}$$

Compute $21^{0.25}$:

$$21^{0.5} = 4.583 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 21^{0.25} = \sqrt{4.583} = 2.141$$

$$V_{p} = \frac{300}{2.141} = 140.1\;\text{m/min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** $V_p < V_c$. Maximum-production rate uses a **slower** speed with **longer** tool life because tool-change time penalizes frequent tool changes more heavily than the wage saving from faster cutting.

---

### Part (iii) — Machining Time & Cost Per Piece (under MC)

$$t_{m} = \frac{\pi D L}{1000\,f\,V_{c}} = \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 250}{1000 \times 0.3 \times 152.4} = \frac{39\,270}{45\,720} = 0.859\;\text{min}$$

$$N = \frac{T_{c}}{t_{m}} = \frac{15}{0.859} = 17.46\;\text{pieces/edge}$$

$$C_{p} = C_{o}\,t_{m} + \frac{C_{t}}{N} = 6 \times 0.859 + \frac{30}{17.46}$$

$$C_{p} = 5.154 + 1.718 = 6.87\;\text{Rs/piece}$$

### Comparative Result Table

| Quantity | Minimum Cost (MC) | Max Production (MPR) |
|---|---|---|
| $T$ (tool life) | 15 min | **21 min** |
| $V$ (cutting speed) | **152.4 m/min** | 140.1 m/min |
| $t_m$ (per piece) | 0.859 min | 0.934 min |
| $C_p$ (per piece) | **Rs. 6.87** | Rs. 6.92 |

> **Conclusion for this problem:** MC runs at **higher speed** but produces slightly **cheaper pieces**; MPR runs at **lower speed** but **shorter cycle time per piece**.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 4 — Effect of Tool Cost on Optimum Speed

> **Statement:** For the same setup as Problem 2 ($n = 0.25$, $C = 200$, $C_o = 4$ Rs/min), how does the optimum cutting speed change if the tool cost is **doubled** from Rs. 20 to Rs. 40?

### Solution

Using $T_c = \dfrac{C_t}{C_o}\cdot\dfrac{1-n}{n}$:

- For $C_t = 20$: $T_c = 15$ min, $V_c = 101.6$ m/min (from Problem 2)
- For $C_t = 40$: $T_c = 30$ min, $V_c = \dfrac{200}{30^{0.25}}$

$$30^{0.25} = \sqrt{\sqrt{30}} = \sqrt{5.477} = 2.340$$

$$V_c = \frac{200}{2.340} = 85.5\;\text{m/min}$$

**Physical insight:** A costlier tool → we use it longer → we cut slower. This is why **carbide inserts** (expensive) are used at **lower speeds** than HSS tools.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cost Components & Optimization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Workpiece Geometry D, L, f] --> B[Process Constants n, C from Taylor]
    B --> C{Cost Model}
    C --> D[Labour Cost C_o x t_m]
    C --> E[Tool Cost C_t per edge]
    C --> F[Tool-Change Cost C_tc per change]
    D --> G[Total Cost Per Piece C_p]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Optimization Goal}
    H --> I[Minimum Cost: dC_p by dT equals 0]
    H --> J[Max Production: dR_p by dT equals 0]
    I --> K[T_c equals C_t by C_o times 1 minus n by n]
    J --> L[T_p equals C_t plus C_tc by C_o times 1 minus n by n]
    K --> M[V_c equals C by T_c power n]
    L --> N[V_p equals C by T_p power n]
    M --> O[OUTPUT: Optimum Cutting Conditions]
    N --> O
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Logic for Selecting MC vs MPR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Production Scenario] --> Q{Is tool-change time significant}
    Q -->|No| R[Use Minimum Cost Condition only]
    Q -->|Yes| S{Bottleneck at machine tool}
    S -->|Machine is bottleneck| T[Use Max Production Condition]
    S -->|Labour is bottleneck| R
    R --> U[Compute V_c and T_c]
    T --> V[Compute V_p and T_p]
    U --> W[Apply to CNC program]
    V --> W
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Cost Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Layer1[INPUT LAYER]
        I1[Cutting Speed V]
        I2[Tool Life T]
        I3[Tool Cost C_t]
        I4[Operator Cost C_o]
        I5[Tool-Change Time t_c]
    end
    subgraph Layer2[PROCESSING LAYER]
        P1[Taylor V T power n equals C]
        P2[Cost Function C_p equals C_o t_m plus C_t by N]
        P3[Derivative dC_p by dT equals 0]
        P4[Solve for T_c and V_c]
    end
    subgraph Layer3[OUTPUT LAYER]
        O1[Optimum Tool Life T_c]
        O2[Optimum Speed V_c]
        O3[Cycle Time per Piece]
        O4[Cost per Piece C_p]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P2
    I4 --> P2
    I5 --> P2
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> O1
    P4 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    P4 --> O4
```

## 4.4 Comparison Block — Cost Components Visual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Total Cost Per Piece C_p] --> R1[Labour Component C_o t_m]
    R --> R2[Tool Component C_t by N]
    R1 --> R1A[Proportional to T power n]
    R1 --> R1B[Increases with tool life T]
    R2 --> R2A[Proportional to T power n minus 1]
    R2 --> R2B[Decreases with tool life T]
    R1B --> SUM[Sum C_o t_m plus C_t by N]
    R2B --> SUM
    SUM --> MIN[Minimum at T equals T_c]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define Taylor's tool-life equation. Why is the tool-life exponent $n$ always between 0 and 1?*

**Model Answer:**

Taylor's tool-life equation is an empirical relationship:

$$V\,T^{n} = C$$

where $V$ = cutting speed (m/min), $T$ = tool life (min), $n$ = tool-life exponent, $C$ = constant.

The exponent $n$ lies between 0 and 1 because of the physical nature of tool wear: as cutting speed increases, tool life decreases, but not linearly — a small increase in speed causes a disproportionately large drop in tool life. If $n = 1$, life would drop linearly with speed (unrealistic); if $n = 0$, life would be independent of speed (also unrealistic). Empirical values lie in $0.1 < n < 0.5$ for most material-tool pairs. **(3 marks)**

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between "Minimum Cost Condition" and "Maximum Production Rate Condition" in machining economics.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Minimum Cost (MC) | Maximum Production Rate (MPR) |
|---|---|---|
| Objective | Minimize Rs/piece | Minimize time/piece |
| Tool-change time | Ignored | Included ($t_c \neq 0$) |
| Optimum tool life | $T_c = \dfrac{C_t}{C_o}\cdot\dfrac{1-n}{n}$ | $T_p = \dfrac{C_t + C_{tc}}{C_o}\cdot\dfrac{1-n}{n}$ |
| Cutting speed | Higher | Lower (longer tool life preferred) |
| Application | Batch production, job-shop | Mass production, bottleneck machine |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (Choice A) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **In a turning operation on mild steel, the following data are obtained from two cutting tests:**
> - $V_1 = 90$ m/min, $T_1 = 20$ min
> - $V_2 = 60$ m/min, $T_2 = 60$ min
>
> **Tool cost $C_t = $ Rs. 50 per cutting edge; operator + overhead cost $C_o = $ Rs. 5/min; tool-change time $t_c = 1.5$ min; feed $f = 0.3$ mm/rev; diameter $D = 60$ mm; length $L = 300$ mm.**
>
> **(a)** Determine Taylor's tool-life equation and find the cutting speed for a tool life of 40 min. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find the optimum cutting speed and tool life for **(i)** Minimum Cost and **(ii)** Maximum Production Rate. Compare the results. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

Taylor: $90 \times 20^{n} = 60 \times 60^{n}$

$$\frac{90}{60} = \left(\frac{60}{20}\right)^{n} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1.5 = 3^{n}$$

$$n = \frac{\log 1.5}{\log 3} = \frac{0.1761}{0.4771} = 0.369$$

$$C = 90 \times 20^{0.369} = 90 \times 3.02 = 271.8\;\text{m/min}$$

**Taylor's equation:** $VT^{0.369} = 271.8$ **(3 marks)**

For $T = 40$ min:

$$V = \frac{271.8}{40^{0.369}} = \frac{271.8}{e^{0.369 \times 3.689}} = \frac{271.8}{e^{1.361}} = \frac{271.8}{3.90} = 69.7\;\text{m/min}$$

**(Total 7 marks for part a)**

**Part (b):**

$C_{tc} = C_o \times t_c = 5 \times 1.5 = 7.5$ Rs/change

**(i) Minimum Cost:**

$$T_c = \frac{C_t}{C_o}\cdot\frac{1-n}{n} = \frac{50}{5}\cdot\frac{1-0.369}{0.369} = 10 \times 1.711 = 17.11\;\text{min}$$

$$V_c = \frac{271.8}{17.11^{0.369}} = \frac{271.8}{2.832} = 96.0\;\text{m/min}$$

**(ii) Maximum Production Rate:**

$$T_p = \frac{C_t + C_{tc}}{C_o}\cdot\frac{1-n}{n} = \frac{50 + 7.5}{5}\times 1.711 = 11.5 \times 1.711 = 19.68\;\text{min}$$

$$V_p = \frac{271.8}{19.68^{0.369}} = \frac{271.8}{3.030} = 89.7\;\text{m/min}$$

**Comparison:**
- $V_c > V_p$ (MC runs faster to save operator wage)
- $T_c < T_p$ (MC uses tool more intensively)
- $V_p \approx 93.4\%$ of $V_c$ (small reduction in speed for larger time saving per piece)

**(Total 7 marks for part b)**

---

### Question B-B (Choice B) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> **In an orthogonal turning operation, Taylor's tool-life equation is $VT^{0.20} = 350$. Tool cost $C_t = $ Rs. 80 per cutting edge, operator cost $C_o = $ Rs. 8/min, feed $f = 0.25$ mm/rev, depth of cut $d = 2$ mm, workpiece diameter $D = 50$ mm, length of cut $L = 100$ mm. Tool-change time = 2 min.**
>
> **(a)** Compute the optimum cutting speed and tool life for **minimum cost** condition. Calculate the machining time per piece and total cost per piece at this optimum. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Repeat the calculation for **maximum production rate** condition and comment on which strategy is preferable for a batch of 500 pieces. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Minimum Cost:**

$$n = 0.20, \quad C = 350, \quad C_t = 80, \quad C_o = 8$$

$$T_c = \frac{C_t}{C_o}\cdot\frac{1-n}{n} = \frac{80}{8}\cdot\frac{0.80}{0.20} = 10 \times 4 = 40\;\text{min}$$

$$V_c = \frac{350}{40^{0.20}} = \frac{350}{2.075} = 168.7\;\text{m/min}$$

Machining time:

$$t_m = \frac{\pi \times 50 \times 100}{1000 \times 0.25 \times 168.7} = \frac{15\,708}{42\,175} = 0.3724\;\text{min}$$

Pieces per edge:

$$N = \frac{40}{0.3724} = 107.4\;\text{pieces/edge}$$

Total cost per piece:

$$C_p = C_o t_m + \frac{C_t}{N} = 8(0.3724) + \frac{80}{107.4} = 2.979 + 0.745 = 3.72\;\text{Rs/piece}$$

**(7 marks)**

**Part (b) — Maximum Production Rate:**

$C_{tc} = 8 \times 2 = 16$ Rs/change

$$T_p = \frac{C_t + C_{tc}}{C_o}\cdot\frac{1-n}{n} = \frac{80 + 16}{8}\times 4 = 12 \times 4 = 48\;\text{min}$$

$$V_p = \frac{350}{48^{0.20}} = \frac{350}{2.105} = 166.3\;\text{m/min}$$

Cost per piece under MPR:

$$t_m' = \frac{15\,708}{1000 \times 0.25 \times 166.3} = \frac{15\,708}{41\,575} = 0.3778\;\text{min}$$

$$N' = \frac{48}{0.3778} = 127.1\;\text{pieces/edge}$$

$$C_p' = 8(0.3778) + \frac{80}{127.1} = 3.022 + 0.629 = 3.65\;\text{Rs/piece}$$

**Comment for 500-piece batch:**
- Total cost under MC = $500 \times 3.72 = $ **Rs. 1860**
- Total cost under MPR = $500 \times 3.65 = $ **Rs. 1825** (cheaper because tool-change cost is distributed)
- Total time under MC = $500 \times 0.3724 = 186.2$ min + tool-change overhead
- Total time under MPR = $500 \times 0.3778 = 188.9$ min (slightly longer per piece but fewer tool changes)

> **Recommendation:** For a 500-piece batch with **tool-change cost non-trivial** ($C_{tc} = 16$ Rs), the **MPR condition** yields **lower cost per piece** because it amortizes the tool-change expense over more pieces per edge. MC is preferred when tool-change time is negligible.

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Economics Problems (KTU 2024 Valuation Key):**
>
> 1. **Exponent confusion:** Students often confuse $n$ in Taylor's equation with the cutting-speed exponent in $V^n T = C$. Always state the **form** of Taylor you are using before substituting.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $(1-n)/n$:** Many students write $T_c = C_t/C_o$, missing the dimensionless factor $(1-n)/n$. **Lose 2 marks instantly.**
>
> 3. **Unit inconsistency:** When $V$ is in m/min and $f$ in mm/rev, the formula $t_m = \pi D L / (1000 f V)$ yields $t_m$ in **minutes**. Mixing up the factor 1000 (or 60) is a silent error.
>
> 4. **Pieces per tool calculation:** Students often compute $N$ as $T/t_m$ but forget that **only the integer part** is physically meaningful (you cannot produce a fractional piece).
>
> 5. **MC vs MPR mix-up:** When the problem mentions tool-change time, students must use **MPR formula**. The MC formula assumes $t_c = 0$. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the wrong formula is used.
>
> 6. **Missing second-derivative check:** In 14-mark problems, KTU examiners expect a **brief justification** that the stationary point is a *minimum* (by second-derivative test or graphical argument). Skipping this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Checklist

- [x] **Taylor's equation:** $VT^n = C$ — always quote the exponent value
- [x] **Finding $n$:** $n = \log(V_1/V_2) \,/\, \log(T_2/T_1)$
- [x] **Cost function:** $C_p = C_o t_m + C_t/N$ (minimum cost version)
- [x] **Optimum tool life (MC):** $T_c = (C_t/C_o)\cdot((1-n)/n)$
- [x] **Optimum tool life (MPR):** $T_p = ((C_t+C_{tc})/C_o)\cdot((1-n)/n)$
- [x] **Optimum speed:** $V_c = C/T_c^n$ from Taylor
- [x] **Machining time:** $t_m = \pi D L / (1000 f V)$
- [x] **Pieces per edge:** $N = T/t_m$
- [x] **Always use SI-consistent units** (mm for length, m/min for speed, min for time)
- [x] **MC vs MPR:** MC ignores tool-change cost; MPR includes it
- [x] **Higher $C_t$ → longer $T_c$ → lower $V_c$** (costlier tools are used slower)
- [x] **Higher $C_o$ → shorter $T_c$ → higher $V_c$** (expensive labour encourages faster cutting)

### Formula Triangle (Visual Mnemonic)

```
                    Taylor's Equation
                          |
        V = C / T^n  ←————————→  T = (C/V)^(1/n)
```

```
        Cost Optimization Triad
        -----------------------
        T_c = (C_t / C_o) * (1 - n) / n
        V_c = C / T_c^n
        t_m = (pi * D * L) / (1000 * f * V_c)
```

### Pitfall Summary Table

| Pitfall | Symptom | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Wrong exponent form | $V^n T = C$ vs $VT^n = C$ | Always write Taylor explicitly before substituting |
| Unit mismatch in $t_m$ | Off by factor of 60 or 1000 | Check: $D$(mm), $L$(mm), $f$(mm/rev), $V$(m/min) → $t_m$(min) |
| Missing $(1-n)/n$ factor | $T_c = C_t/C_o$ (dimensionally correct but wrong) | Re-derive from $dC_p/dT = 0$ |
| Confusing $N$ with $t_m$ | $N = T$ instead of $N = T/t_m$ | Recall: $N$ is dimensionless count |
| Forgetting $t_c$ in MPR | Using MC formula in MPR problem | Look for "tool-change time" in problem statement |

> **End of Topic Notes — Economics of Machining, Numerical Problems**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lathe - Types, Parts, Specifications, Operations, Accessories, Process Parameters, Machining Time Calculations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Lathe - Types, Parts, Specifications, Operations, Accessories, Process Parameters, Machining Time Calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lathe (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *lathe* is a **machine tool** designed primarily to **remove unwanted material from a workpiece by forcing a cutting tool against it**, while the **workpiece is rotated about a fixed axis**. The relative motion between the rigidly held tool and the rotating workpiece is the **primary (cutting) motion**, and the controlled linear motion imparted to the tool is the **secondary (feed) motion**.

A lathe is therefore called the **"Mother of all Machine Tools"** because almost every other machining process (milling, drilling, grinding) is derived from the basic kinematic principle of *tool–workpiece relative motion* that a lathe established.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **chapati / rotti rolling pin (belan) on a flat circular platform (chakla)**:

- The **chakla (rotating part)** represents the **workpiece spindle** rotating at speed *N*.
- The **belan (the pin pressed against the dough)** represents the **cutting tool** moving linearly.
- The amount you press the belan sideways = **depth of cut (d)**.
- How fast the dough goes around = **cutting speed (V)**.
- How fast *you* push the belan forward = **feed (f)**.

If you push the belan harder, deeper, or faster → more dough (material) is removed per minute. This is **exactly** how a lathe works, except the "dough" is metal and the "belan" is a hardened high-speed-steel (HSS) or carbide insert.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In KTU Board papers, students are expected to clearly distinguish the **primary motion** (provided by the *spindle / workpiece* in a lathe) from the **secondary motion** (provided by the *carriage / tool*). Swapping these definitions is the single most common reason for losing 1–2 marks in Part A questions.

## 1.3 Significance in Manufacturing

| Aspect | Role of Lathe |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Versatility** | Performs turning, facing, parting, threading, knurling, boring, drilling, reaming, tapping, grinding |
| **Precision** | Achievable tolerance ± 0.01 mm on a standard engine lathe |
| **Material flexibility** | Works on steels, cast irons, aluminium, brass, plastics, woods |
| **Industrial share** | ~ 25–30 % of all basic machining operations in a typical machine shop |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Lathes (KTU Syllabus)

### 2.1.1 By Size and Purpose
- **Centre Lathe / Engine Lathe** – the general-purpose workhorse (most frequently asked in KTU).
- **Tool-Room Lathe** – higher precision, fitted with collets and taper-turning attachment.
- **Bench Lathe** – small lathes mounted on a workbench for instrument work.
- **Gap-Bed Lathe** – bed has a *removable section* (gap) to swing large-diameter discs.
- **Speed Lathe** – wood-turning, buffing, polishing; no power feed.

### 2.1.2 By Production Type
- **Capstan Lathe** – *turret* slides linearly along the bed; tooling preset in the turret. Suited for **small batch production**.
- **Turret Lathe** – larger and more rigid than capstan; *turret slides on a ram fixed to the bed*. Suited for **medium batch production**.
- **Automatic Lathe** – cam-controlled; the *cams automatically actuate* tool slides after a set cycle. Suited for **mass production**.
- **CNC Lathe** – numerically controlled; X–Z axes driven by servo motors via G-codes.

### 2.1.3 By Special Geometry
- **Chucker Lathe** – chucking work; no tailstock; used for short, heavy jobs.
- **Multi-Spindle Lathe** – 4, 6, or 8 spindles rotate in sequence for high output.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Trick:** *Capstan = small, ram-mounted, saddle-style turret; Turret = large, bed-mounted, full-size turret*. Memorise this — it is asked almost every year.

## 2.2 Principal Parts of an Engine Lathe

A lathe has six functional sub-assemblies. The KTU mark distribution usually allocates **7 marks** for a labelled diagram + part-functions.

| # | Part | Function |
| :-- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Bed** | Heavy cast-iron base; provides rigidity and accurate guiding surfaces (V-ways and flat ways). |
| 2 | **Headstock** | Houses the **spindle**, **gear-train**, and **speed-change levers**. Carries the chuck. |
| 3 | **Tailstock** | Supports the *dead centre* for long jobs; carries drilling / reaming tools. Slides on bed ways. |
| 4 | **Carriage** | Mounts the tool post and moves the tool along Z (longitudinal) and X (cross) axes. |
| 5 | **Feed Mechanism** | Apron carries gears that transmit motion from spindle to leadscrew / feed-rod. |
| 6 | **Legs / Cabinet** | Supports the bed; houses the motor and coolant pump. |

### 2.2.1 Sub-Parts of the Carriage
- **Saddle** – H-slides on the bed ways (Z movement).
- **Cross-slide** – moves on the saddle in X.
- **Compound rest** – swivels and provides angular feed.
- **Tool post (4-way / quick-change)** – holds the cutting tool.

## 2.3 Lathe Specifications (How to Read a Lathe Nameplate)

The KTU Board almost always asks *"List the main specifications of a lathe."*

- **Swing diameter over bed** = 2 × (centre height).
- **Swing over cross-slide** = swing over bed – 2 × (height of saddle).
- **Length of bed** / Distance between centres.
- **Height of centres**.
- **Spindle bore** and **Morse taper** in the spindle nose.
- **Number of spindle speeds** and their range (e.g., 8 speeds, 30–1500 rpm).
- **Number of feeds** (longitudinal and cross).
- **Spindle nose** type (L-00, D1-3, D1-6, D1-8 as per ISO 702).
- **Lead screw pitch** and **thread pitches** that can be cut.
- **Power of main motor** (kW).
- **Floor space and net weight**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A lathe is **always specified by SWING × LENGTH**, e.g., *400 mm × 1500 mm*. This is a direct, board-favourite one-mark question.

## 2.4 Lathe Operations

A standard engine lathe can perform:

- **Turning** (straight, taper, profile, eccentric) – primary operation.
- **Facing** – producing a flat surface perpendicular to the axis.
- **Parting / Cutting off** – slicing the workpiece using a narrow parting tool.
- **Knurling** – impressing a diamond / straight pattern for grip.
- **Thread cutting** – V-threads, ACME, square, buttress, worm.
- **Boring** – enlarging an existing hole.
- **Drilling / Reaming / Tapping** – using the tailstock.
- **Forming** – using a forming tool shaped to the required profile.
- **Spinning** (special attachment).
- **Grinding, Polishing, Buffing** (with abrasive tool instead of cutter).

## 2.5 Lathe Accessories (Work-Holding and Support)

| Accessory | Use |
| :--- | :--- |
| **3-Jaw Self-Centring Chuck** | Holds round / hexagonal jobs automatically centred. |
| **4-Jaw Independent Chuck** | Holds square, irregular, or off-centred jobs. |
| **Face Plate** | Mounts large/irregular shapes via bolts and clamps. |
| **Catch Plate / Carrier** | Drives the workpiece between centres. |
| **Mandrel** | Holds a bored / hollow job for external turning. |
| **Steady Rest** | Prevents deflection of *long slender shafts* (fixed at bed). |
| **Follower Rest** | Mounted on the carriage; moves with the tool. |
| **Centre (Live / Dead)** | Supports the workpiece between headstock and tailstock. |
| **Magnetic Chuck / Collet Chuck** | For small precision work on tool-room lathes. |
| **Coolant / Cutting-fluid Nozzle** | Directs fluid to the cutting zone. |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities use **SI / consistent units** as indicated. Carrying wrong units is the **#1 numerical error** in KTU scripts — keep your eyes on the column "Unit" while substituting.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit |
| :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Cutting Speed (surface speed)** | $V \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | V = cutting speed, D = workpiece diameter, N = spindle speed | m / min |
| 2 | **Spindle Speed** | $N \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D}$ | — | rpm |
| 3 | **Feed Rate (linear)** | $f_r \;=\; f \times N$ | f = feed per rev, N = rpm | mm / min |
| 4 | **Material Removal Rate (Turning)** | $\text{MRR} \;=\; f \times d \times V$ | d = depth of cut | mm³ / min |
| 5 | **Material Removal Rate (Turning, alt.)** | $\text{MRR} \;=\; \pi \, f \, d \, D \, N$ | All lengths in mm | mm³ / min |
| 6 | **Main (Tangential) Cutting Force** | $F_c \;=\; K_s \times f \times d$ | $K_s$ = specific cutting energy / force | N |
| 7 | **Power at Spindle** | $P \;=\; \dfrac{F_c \times V}{60\,000 \times \eta}$ | $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency (0.7–0.85) | kW |
| 8 | **Power (using MRR)** | $P \;=\; \dfrac{\text{MRR} \times K_s}{60\,000\,\eta}$ | MRR in mm³/min | kW |
| 9 | **Machining Time — Turning** | $T_m \;=\; \dfrac{L + L_a + L_o}{f \times N}$ | $L_a$ = approach, $L_o$ = overtravel | min |
| 10 | **Machining Time — Facing (radial feed)** | $T_m \;=\; \dfrac{D - d}{2 \, f \, N}$ | D, d in mm; f = radial feed (mm/rev) | min |
| 11 | **Volumetric MRR — Facing** | $\text{MRR} \;=\; \pi \, f \, D \, N \, w$ | w = axial width of cut | mm³ / min |
| 12 | **Thread Cutting Time** | $T_m \;=\; \dfrac{L + L_a + L_o}{N \times p}$ | p = thread pitch (mm) | min |
| 13 | **Cutting Velocity to Spindle-Speed Ratio** | $\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} \;=\; \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ | Constant V across diameters | — |

> **Boundary / Validity Conditions**
> - $V$ is **constant** for a given tool-material/workpiece-material pair; the operator must *vary N* as diameter changes.
> - For $K_s$ values: **Mild steel ≈ 2000 N/mm²**, Cast iron ≈ 1100 N/mm², Aluminium ≈ 700 N/mm², Brass ≈ 1500 N/mm² (approx., for V in m/min and f in mm/rev).
> - Machining time formulas **assume single-pass operation**; for multiple passes, sum the times per pass.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Machining Time for Plain Turning (Straight Cut)

**Setup.** A cylindrical workpiece of length $L$ is reduced from diameter $D_0$ to $D_f$ in **one pass**. The tool moves longitudinally along Z with feed $f$ mm/rev, and the spindle rotates at $N$ rpm. Approach distance $L_a$ and overtravel $L_o$ are added to the actual length.

**Step 1 — Total linear travel of the tool.**
The cutting tool must travel the **entire job length** plus a small *approach* (before the tool touches the work) and an *overtravel* (after the tool leaves the work).

$$
L_{\text{total}} \;=\; L + L_a + L_o
$$

**Step 2 — Distance covered in one spindle revolution.**
By definition, *feed* $f$ is the axial distance the tool advances **per revolution** of the workpiece.

$$
\text{Distance per rev} \;=\; f \;\; \text{[mm/rev]}
$$

**Step 3 — Total revolutions required.**

$$
N_{\text{rev}} \;=\; \dfrac{L_{\text{total}}}{f}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert revolutions to time.**
Since the spindle rotates at $N$ rev per minute,

$$
T_m \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{rev}}}{N} \;=\; \dfrac{L + L_a + L_o}{f \times N}
$$

This is the canonical KTU formula. The *depth of cut* $d$ does **not** appear here, because the time to remove material is governed solely by how far the tool must travel axially and how fast it is fed — not by how deep the cut is.

## 3.2 Derivation — Machining Time for Facing

**Setup.** The tool starts at the **outer diameter $D$** and feeds **radially inward** until it reaches the **inner diameter $d$** ($d \le D$). The radial feed is $f$ mm/rev, spindle speed is $N$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Radial distance to be traversed.**
The tool must move from $D$ to $d$, a radial distance of

$$
\Delta r \;=\; \dfrac{D - d}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of revolutions needed.**
Since each revolution reduces the radius by $f$,

$$
N_{\text{rev}} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta r}{f} \;=\; \dfrac{D - d}{2 f}
$$

**Step 3 — Time in minutes.**

$$
T_m \;=\; \dfrac{N_{\text{rev}}}{N} \;=\; \dfrac{D - d}{2 f N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For facing, the **diameter changes continuously during cutting**, so the *cutting speed V is not constant*. KTU Board questions usually quote a single value of V, in which case the *average* diameter $\dfrac{D+d}{2}$ is used to compute $N$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical (KTU 14-Mark Standard)

> **Problem.** A mild-steel cylindrical bar of **initial diameter 80 mm** and **length 200 mm** is to be turned down to **75 mm diameter in a single pass** on a high-speed-steel tool. Given:
> - Cutting speed $V = 30 \text{ m/min}$
> - Feed $f = 0.25 \text{ mm/rev}$
> - Approach $L_a = 3 \text{ mm}$, Overtravel $L_o = 2 \text{ mm}$
> - Specific cutting force $K_s = 2000 \text{ N/mm}^2$
> - Mechanical efficiency $\eta = 80\%$
>
> Determine: **(a)** Spindle speed, **(b)** Feed rate in mm/min, **(c)** Material Removal Rate, **(d)** Machining time, **(e)** Power required at the motor.

---

### (a) Spindle Speed $N$

Using the formula from the cheat sheet,

$$
N \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D} \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 80}
$$

$$
N \;=\; \dfrac{30\,000}{251.327} \;=\; 119.37 \;\; \text{rpm}
$$

We take the **standard available spindle speed** ≥ 119.37 rpm from the machine's speed chart — say **120 rpm** — and proceed.

### (b) Feed Rate $f_r$

$$
f_r \;=\; f \times N \;=\; 0.25 \times 119.37
$$

$$
f_r \;=\; 29.84 \;\; \text{mm/min}
$$

### (c) Material Removal Rate $\text{MRR}$

Depth of cut:

$$
d \;=\; \dfrac{D_0 - D_f}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{80 - 75}{2} \;=\; 2.5 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; f \times d \times V \;\; \text{(in mm³/min if } V \text{ in mm/min)}
$$

Convert $V$: $V = 30 \text{ m/min} = 30\,000 \text{ mm/min}$.

$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; 0.25 \times 2.5 \times 30\,000
$$

$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; 18\,750 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \;\;=\;\; 18.75 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

### (d) Machining Time $T_m$

$$
L_{\text{total}} \;=\; L + L_a + L_o \;=\; 200 + 3 + 2 \;=\; 205 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

$$
T_m \;=\; \dfrac{L_{\text{total}}}{f \times N} \;=\; \dfrac{205}{0.25 \times 119.37}
$$

$$
T_m \;=\; \dfrac{205}{29.84} \;=\; 6.87 \;\; \text{min}
$$

### (e) Power Required $P$

Main cutting force:

$$
F_c \;=\; K_s \times f \times d \;=\; 2000 \times 0.25 \times 2.5
$$

$$
F_c \;=\; 1250 \;\; \text{N}
$$

Power at the spindle (using $V$ in m/min):

$$
P_{\text{spindle}} \;=\; \dfrac{F_c \times V}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta} \;=\; \dfrac{1250 \times 30}{60 \times 1000 \times 0.8}
$$

$$
P_{\text{spindle}} \;=\; \dfrac{37\,500}{48\,000} \;=\; 0.781 \;\; \text{kW} \;\;\approx\; 0.79 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote $P$ in **kW**, with two decimal places. Many students write 781 W and lose the unit-marker mark.

### 3.3.1 Summary Table of the Numerical

| Quantity | Symbol | Computed Value |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Spindle speed | $N$ | 119.37 rpm (≈ 120 rpm) |
| Feed rate | $f_r$ | 29.84 mm/min |
| Depth of cut | $d$ | 2.5 mm |
| MRR | MRR | 18 750 mm³/min |
| Machining time | $T_m$ | 6.87 min |
| Cutting force | $F_c$ | 1250 N |
| Power at motor | $P$ | 0.78 kW |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Engine Lathe

This diagram maps the *kinematic and structural inter-relationships* between the principal parts of an engine lathe, including the power-flow path and the support path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[motor] --> P[gear box pulley]
    P --> H[headstock spindle]
    H --> C[chuck or face plate]
    C --> W[rotating workpiece]
    H -. drives .-> FM[feed mechanism apron]
    FM --> LS[lead screw]
    FM --> FR[feed rod]
    LS --> CG[carriage gear]
    FR --> CG
    CG --> CA[carriage assembly]
    CA --> TS[tool slide]
    TS --> TP[tool post]
    TP --> CT[cutting tool]
    CT --> W
    BED[cast iron bed] --> H
    BED --> CA
    BED --> TL[tailstock]
    TL --> DC[dead centre or drill]
    DC --> W
    TL -. supports long slender shafts .- SR[steady or follower rest]
    SR --> W
```

## 4.2 Process-Parameter Selection Flow (Turning Operation)

The diagram below shows how an engineer moves from the **workpiece material and tool selection** down to the **final power requirement**, using the KTU formula chain in Section 2.6.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify workpiece material and tool material] --> B[Select recommended cutting speed V from handbook]
    B --> C[Compute spindle speed N equals 1000V over piD]
    C --> D[Choose feed f mm per rev from handbook]
    D --> E[Compute feed rate fr equals f times N]
    D --> F[Compute depth of cut d equals D0 minus Df over 2]
    E --> G[Compute MRR equals f times d times V]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute cutting force Fc equals Ks times f times d]
    H --> I[Compute power P equals Fc times V over 60000 eta]
    E --> J[Compute machining time Tm equals L plus La plus Lo over f N]
    I --> K[Check against available motor power]
    J --> L[Final answer: N, fr, MRR, Tm, P]
    K --> L
```

## 4.3 Lathe Operation Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OP[Required surface feature] --> Q1{Is it a flat face perpendicular to axis?}
    Q1 -- yes --> O1[Facing operation]
    Q1 -- no --> Q2{Is it a cylindrical surface of different diameter?}
    Q2 -- yes --> O2[Turning straight turning]
    Q2 -- no --> Q3{Is it a hole enlargement?}
    Q3 -- yes --> O3[Boring with a boring bar]
    Q3 -- no --> Q4{Is it a thread on external or internal surface?}
    Q4 -- yes --> O4[Thread cutting with single point tool or die]
    Q4 -- no --> Q5{Is it a pattern for grip?}
    Q5 -- yes --> O5[Knurling with knurling tool]
    Q5 -- no --> Q6{Is it separation of the workpiece?}
    Q6 -- yes --> O6[Parting off with parting tool]
    Q6 -- no --> Q7{Is it cutting off a finished piece at length?}
    Q7 -- yes --> O7[Cut off using cutoff tool]
    Q7 -- no --> O8[Special operation spinning forming grinding]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the main specifications of a centre lathe. State the significance of "swing diameter" in specifying a lathe.*

**Model Answer:**

The main specifications of a centre / engine lathe are:

1. **Swing diameter over bed** — the largest diameter of a workpiece that can be rotated above the bed ways. It equals twice the centre height. It determines the **maximum diameter** the lathe can machine.
2. **Swing over cross-slide** — maximum diameter that can clear the cross-slide; useful when a large chuck is mounted.
3. **Length of bed** or **distance between centres** — determines the maximum length of workpiece.
4. **Height of centres** — the vertical distance from the bed way to the spindle axis.
5. **Number and range of spindle speeds** (e.g., 8 speeds, 30–1500 rpm).
6. **Number and range of feeds** (longitudinal and cross).
7. **Spindle bore** and **spindle nose taper** (Morse / ISO 702).
8. **Lead screw pitch** and **range of thread pitches** that can be cut.
9. **Power of main motor** in kW.
10. **Floor space** and **net weight**.

**Significance of swing diameter:** It is the *first specification* a buyer checks, because the swing dictates the **maximum diameter** of any workpiece the lathe can rotate without collision with the bed. A lathe is always quoted as *"SWING × LENGTH"*, e.g., 400 mm × 1500 mm.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a Capstan lathe and a Turret lathe.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Capstan Lathe | Turret Lathe |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Size** | Small | Large |
| **Turret mounting** | Turret mounted on a **saddle / ram** that slides on the bed. | Turret is **bed-mounted** and supported directly on the bed. |
| **Capacity (bar)** | Up to ~ 60 mm | Up to ~ 150 mm |
| **Rigidity** | Lower; limited to small jobs. | Higher; can take heavier cuts. |
| **Production type** | **Small batch / job shop**. | **Medium batch production**. |
| **Tool-slide (cross)** | Saddle + turret; *no separate cross-slide*. | Has both **cross-slide** and **turret slide** (compound). |
| **Work-holding** | Mostly collets or 3-jaw chucks. | Chucks, face plates, and 4-jaw chucks. |
| **Operator skill** | Less skilled (semi-skilled). | Higher skill required. |

**Common point:** Both are *turret-type production lathes* in which a hexagonal **turret** holds multiple preset tools; the turret is *indexed* to bring the next tool into position.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice)

### Question A (14 marks) — Theory

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a) (7 marks)** *With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **constructional features of an engine lathe**. List the **principal parts** and their **functions**.*

**(b) (7 marks)** *Enumerate the **various operations** that can be performed on a centre lathe. State the **lathe accessories** used for work-holding and for supporting long slender shafts during turning.*

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Constructional features of an engine lathe** — six principal sub-assemblies:

1. **Bed** – A heavy, accurately machined cast-iron base. It has **V-ways and flat ways** (or inverted-V ways) on its top, precision-ground to ensure the carriage and tailstock travel in a true straight line. The bed rests on legs / cabinet.

2. **Headstock** – Mounted on the left end of the bed. It contains the **main spindle**, the **stepped pulley / gear-box** for speed changes, and the **spindle nose** (with internal Morse taper) for mounting chucks or face plates. The spindle is the source of the **primary cutting motion**.

3. **Tailstock** – Located on the right end of the bed. It can be **slid along the bed ways** to accommodate varying workpiece lengths and clamped in position. It houses a **quill** carrying a **dead centre** (for centre work) or **drills / reamers** (for tailstock operations). The quill is advanced / retracted by a hand-wheel.

4. **Carriage** – Mounted on the bed ways between the headstock and tailstock. Sub-parts:
   - **Saddle** – H-slides on the bed; provides **Z-axis (longitudinal)** movement.
   - **Cross-slide** – Mounted on top of the saddle; provides **X-axis (cross)** movement via a hand-wheel.
   - **Compound rest** – Swivels on a graduated base; permits **angular feed** and finer motion.
   - **Tool post** (4-way indexable / quick-change) – Rigidly holds the cutting tool.

5. **Feed Mechanism (Apron)** – A gearbox hung on the front of the carriage. It receives motion from the **spindle** via the **feed rod** (for power feed) or the **lead screw** (for thread cutting) and transmits it to the **saddle and cross-slide** through gears, clutches, and a split-nut mechanism.

6. **Legs / Cabinet** – Support the bed; house the **main motor, countershaft, and coolant pump**.

The block-level interaction of these parts is shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Operations possible on a centre lathe (12 standard operations):**

1. Straight turning
2. Taper turning (by swivelling compound rest, using taper-turning attachment, or by offsetting the tailstock)
3. Facing
4. Parting / Cutting off
5. Knurling
6. External thread cutting
7. Internal thread cutting (using a thread-chasing dial)
8. Drilling (using tailstock)
9. Reaming (using tailstock)
10. Boring (using a boring bar mounted on the tool post)
11. Tapping (using a tapping attachment or by reversing the spindle)
12. Forming / Profiling (using a form tool)

**Accessories for work-holding:**
- 3-jaw self-centring chuck
- 4-jaw independent chuck
- Face plate
- Collet chuck
- Magnetic chuck
- Mandrel (for internal jobs)

**Accessories for supporting long slender shafts:**
- **Steady rest** – *fixed to the bed ways*; has three adjustable jaws that support the workpiece at a fixed point. Used for *long slender shafts* that would otherwise deflect.
- **Follower rest** – *mounted on the carriage*; has two jaws that move *with* the tool, providing support immediately behind the cutting edge. Prevents chatter on slender shafts.

---

### Question B (14 marks) — Numerical / Analytical

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

*A mild-steel cylindrical bar of **diameter 60 mm** and **length 150 mm** is to be turned down to **50 mm diameter in a single pass** on a centre lathe. Given cutting speed $V = 25$ m/min, feed $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, approach $L_a = 2$ mm, overtravel $L_o = 1$ mm, specific cutting force $K_s = 1800$ N/mm², and mechanical efficiency $\eta = 75\%$, calculate:*

**(a) (4 marks)** *Spindle speed and feed rate.*
**(b) (4 marks)** *Material removal rate and machining time.*
**(c) (3 marks)** *Main cutting force and power required at the motor.*
**(d) (3 marks)** *If facing were carried out on the same initial bar to a depth of 5 mm radially, determine the time required for facing. (Take feed = 0.2 mm/rev.)*

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

Spindle speed (use initial diameter $D = 60$ mm):

$$
N \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 60} \;=\; \dfrac{25\,000}{188.496} \;=\; 132.63 \;\; \text{rpm}
$$

Take **N = 132 rpm** (closest available).

Feed rate:

$$
f_r \;=\; f \times N \;=\; 0.2 \times 132.63 \;=\; 26.53 \;\; \text{mm/min}
$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

Depth of cut:

$$
d \;=\; \dfrac{60 - 50}{2} \;=\; 5 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

Material removal rate (with $V$ in mm/min):

$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; f \times d \times V \;=\; 0.2 \times 5 \times 25\,000
$$

$$
\text{MRR} \;=\; 25\,000 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \;\;=\; 25 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

Total length of tool travel:

$$
L_{\text{total}} \;=\; 150 + 2 + 1 \;=\; 153 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

Machining time:

$$
T_m \;=\; \dfrac{153}{0.2 \times 132.63} \;=\; \dfrac{153}{26.53} \;=\; 5.77 \;\; \text{min}
$$

#### Model Solution to (c)

Main cutting force:

$$
F_c \;=\; K_s \times f \times d \;=\; 1800 \times 0.2 \times 5 \;=\; 1800 \;\; \text{N}
$$

Power at motor:

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{F_c \times V}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta} \;=\; \dfrac{1800 \times 25}{60 \times 1000 \times 0.75}
$$

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{45\,000}{45\,000} \;=\; 1.0 \;\; \text{kW}
$$

#### Model Solution to (d)

Facing depth = 5 mm radially ⇒ the diameter reduces from **60 mm to 50 mm**.

Using the facing-time formula:

$$
T_{\text{face}} \;=\; \dfrac{D - d}{2 f N} \;=\; \dfrac{60 - 50}{2 \times 0.2 \times 132.63}
$$

$$
T_{\text{face}} \;=\; \dfrac{10}{53.05} \;=\; 0.188 \;\; \text{min} \;\;\approx\; 11.3 \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing units of $V$:** If you take $V = 25$ m/min in the MRR formula *without converting to mm/min*, the answer will be **1000× too small** — a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Forgetting approach + overtravel** in $L_{\text{total}}$: ~ 3 % error in $T_m$ — partial-mark loss.
> 3. **Confusing $D - d$ with $(D^2 - d^2)$ in facing:** Use **$D - d$** divided by $2fN$ for *simple radial facing*; the squared-diameter form is for a different formulation.
> 4. **Omitting $\eta$** in the power formula: gives spindle power, not motor power. State explicitly *which* power is being computed.
> 5. **Not stating the assumed spindle speed** as the *closest available* machine speed: Examiners deduct ½ mark if you leave the answer as 132.63 rpm without acknowledging standardisation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Mandatory 1-line definitions**
> - **Lathe** → machine tool in which the *workpiece rotates* and the *tool moves linearly* to remove material.
> - **Primary motion** → provided by the **spindle / workpiece**.
> - **Secondary motion (feed)** → provided by the **carriage / tool**.

> **Key terms (memorise verbatim for KTU)**
> - **Swing diameter** = largest diameter that can be rotated above the bed.
> - **Steady rest** is **bed-mounted**; **Follower rest** is **carriage-mounted**.
> - **Capstan** = small + saddle-mounted turret; **Turret lathe** = large + bed-mounted turret.

> **Formula flashcard (5 must-knows)**
> 1. $V = \pi D N / 1000$
> 2. $N = 1000 V / (\pi D)$
> 3. $f_r = f \times N$
> 4. $\text{MRR} = f \times d \times V$ (with $V$ in mm/min ⇒ MRR in mm³/min)
> 5. $T_m = (L + L_a + L_o) / (f N)$ — turning; $T_m = (D - d) / (2 f N)$ — facing

> **Numericals — Examiner's quality bar**
> - Convert **all units consistently** *before* substituting.
> - Quote $N$ to **two decimal places**; quote $P$ in **kW**.
> - Always state **assumed machine speed** if non-standard.
> - Mention **approach and overtravel** explicitly in the tool-travel length.

> **Common KTU viva questions (2-mark rapid-fire)**
> 1. *Why is V kept constant during turning?* → To maintain the same tool-life and chip-formation conditions irrespective of diameter.
> 2. *Why are mandrels used?* → To hold internally-finished jobs (bored / reamed) for external turning.
> 3. *What is the role of a coolant?* → To reduce tool-tip temperature, improve surface finish, and prolong tool life.
> 4. *Why is HSS still used despite carbides being superior?* → Lower cost, easier to grind to shape, suitable for low-speed / small-batch jobs.
> 5. *What is a 4-way tool post?* → Holds **four tools** simultaneously; the post is *indexed* 90° to bring the next tool into cutting position.

> **Mnemonic for the 6 principal parts:** **"B H T C F L"** → **B**ed, **H**eadstock, **T**ailstock, **C**arriage, **F**eed mechanism, **L**egs.

> **Mnemonic for the 12 operations:** **"ST TF PK TB DR BT"** → **S**traight turning, **T**aper turning, **F**acing, **P**arting, **K**nurling, **T**hreading, **B**oring, **D**rilling, **R**eaming, **B**oring (repeat OK), **T**apping — for a clean list refer to Section 2.4.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shaping, Planning, and Slotting Machines - Classification, Types of Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Shaping, Planning, and Slotting Machines - Classification, Types of Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Shaping, Planning & Slotting — The Reciprocating Machine Tool Family

Shaping, Planning, and Slotting Machines form a closely related sub-family of **reciprocating metal-cutting machine tools** in which the cutting action is achieved by a *linear, to-and-fro (reciprocating) motion* of either the **tool** or the **workpiece**, combined with an intermittent transverse **feed** motion. Unlike a lathe (rotational) or a milling machine (rotational + linear), these machines rely on a *quick-return mechanism* to keep the productive stroke slow and the idle stroke fast, maximising Metal Removal Rate (MRR) per unit time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a **core Module-1 fundamental** and forms the conceptual base for understanding *kinematic structures* and *quick-return mechanisms* that recur in Module 2 (drilling), Module 3 (milling), and Module 4 (finishing processes).

| Machine | Motive Member (Reciprocates) | Workpiece Holder | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Shaper** | Tool (ram) | Rigidly clamped on a stationary table | Small/medium flat surfaces, keyways, slots |
| **Planer** | Workpiece (table) | Bolted to a moving table | Very large flat surfaces, long strokes |
| **Slotter** | Tool (ram) | Held on a circular/rotary table | Internal slots, keyways, blind surfaces |

---

## 1.2 Formal KTU-Standard Definitions

**Shaping Machine:** A machine tool in which a *single-point cutting tool* mounted on a **ram** reciprocates in a straight line, while the workpiece is firmly clamped on a stationary worktable. The ram is driven by a **crank-and-slotted-lever (Whitworth-type) quick-return mechanism**, producing a slow cutting stroke and a fast return stroke.

**Planning Machine:** A machine tool of the reciprocating type in which the *worktable* carrying the workpiece performs the cutting and return strokes, while the *cutting tool* is mounted rigidly on a tool head that is fixed in position. The large size of the table stroke makes it suitable for machining **long, heavy, flat surfaces** on large castings and forgings.

**Slotting Machine (Slotter):** A vertical-axis reciprocating machine tool in which the **ram carrying the tool moves vertically up and down** (the cutting stroke is *downward*, the return stroke is *upward*). The workpiece is mounted on a **rotary or linear table**, allowing internal slotting, keyway cutting, and die work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry-Relevant Parameter:** Standard stroke length of a general-purpose shaper ram is **200 mm – 900 mm**, with quick-return ratios commonly ranging from **Q = 1.5 to 2.0**. Modern CNC-controlled versions eliminate the need for the mechanical quick-return mechanism by using servo-driven ball screws.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **manual hand-saw cutting wood**:
- The **forward push** is slow and powerful — that's the *cutting stroke*.
- The **backward pull** is quick and effortless — that's the *return stroke*.
- The wood is repositioned slightly after each stroke — that's the *feed*.

A shaping/planning/slotting machine is essentially a **mechanised, automated version of this hand-sawing principle**, scaled up for metal.

- **Shaper** = "a hand-saw lying on its side" (horizontal cutting action on small parts).
- **Planer** = "a giant hand-saw where the wood itself slides under a fixed blade" (for huge workpieces).
- **Slotter** = "a hand-saw pointing straight down" (vertical action into a stationary part, like punching a keyway into a shaft hole).

---

## 1.4 Why Are These Three Machines Grouped Together?

All three are united by **three common design features**:
1. **Reciprocating linear motion** of the cutting element (tool or work).
2. A **quick-return mechanism** (mechanical) or **servo-controlled asymmetric stroke** (modern CNC) to reduce idle time.
3. A **transverse feed motion** perpendicular to the cutting direction, advancing the tool/work in small increments per stroke.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick-Return Ratio (Q)** is the single most important performance parameter. $Q = \dfrac{\text{Cutting Stroke Time}}{\text{Return Stroke Time}} > 1$ always, typically $1.5$ to $2.0$ for general-purpose machines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kinematic Structure — The Crank-and-Slotted-Lever Mechanism

The **workhorse** of the shaper (and the basic slotter) is the **Crank and Slotted Lever (CSL) Quick-Return Mechanism**, also called the *Whitworth quick-return* in its older form. It converts uniform rotary motion of the driving shaft into *non-uniform reciprocating motion* of the ram.

### Components and Function

1. **Crank Disc (R)** — Rotates at constant angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s). Its radius $R$ is the **eccentricity** that determines the stroke length.
2. **Slotted Lever (O₂A)** — A lever pivoted at $O_2$ at the machine column. A long slot carries a **sliding pin** fixed to the crank.
3. **Connecting Rod (Link AB)** — Couples the slotted lever's free end $A$ to the ram slider at $B$.
4. **Ram (Slider)** — Moves linearly along guideways. It carries the tool holder.

### Operating Principle — Step by Step

- As the crank $R$ rotates by a full $360°$, the pin inside the slotted lever sweeps the lever up and down.
- During the **forward (cutting) stroke** of the ram, the crank rotates through a **larger angle** $(\alpha + \beta)$ where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are measured from the *inner dead center* to the *outer dead centers*.
- During the **return (idle) stroke**, the crank rotates through the **smaller remaining angle** $[360° - (\alpha + \beta)]$.
- Because the crank turns at *constant* speed, a larger crank angle ⇒ *longer* cutting time, smaller crank angle ⇒ *shorter* return time. This is the **quick-return** effect.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Derive the expression for the quick-return ratio of a crank and slotted lever mechanism used in a shaper."* — This is asked almost every academic year.

---

## 2.2 Planer Kinematics — Different Philosophy

In a planer:
- The **table** (with the workpiece) is the reciprocating member.
- The tool is *stationary* (only fed slightly between strokes).
- A **rack-and-pinion** or **hydraulic drive** produces the slow-fast stroke.
- Multiple cutting tools (sometimes 4–8) are mounted on a **cross-rail** to machine parallel surfaces simultaneously — boosting productivity.

---

## 2.3 Slotter Kinematics — Vertical Ram

In a slotter:
- The **ram moves vertically downward** during cutting (gravity + drive assist) and **upward** during return.
- A **circular table** allows indexing/rotary feed — ideal for cutting **internal keyways, splines, and die cavities**.
- Same crank-and-slotted-lever principle applies, but the lever swings in a vertical plane.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Crank-and-Slotted-Lever Shaper)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Quick-Return Ratio** $Q$ | $Q = \dfrac{\alpha + \beta}{360° - (\alpha + \beta)}$ | dimensionless | $\alpha, \beta$ in degrees |
| 2 | **Cutting Stroke Angle** | $\theta_c = \alpha + \beta$ | degrees | Larger angle, slower cutting |
| 3 | **Return Stroke Angle** | $\theta_r = 360° - (\alpha + \beta)$ | degrees | Smaller angle, faster return |
| 4 | **Stroke Length** $L$ | $L = R(\sin\alpha + \sin\beta)$ | mm | Where $R$ = crank radius |
| 5 | **Crank Radius** | $R = \dfrac{L}{\sin\alpha + \sin\beta}$ | mm | |
| 6 | **Time per Cutting Stroke** | $t_c = \dfrac{\theta_c}{6 N}$ | seconds | $N$ = crank rpm |
| 7 | **Time per Return Stroke** | $t_r = \dfrac{\theta_r}{6 N}$ | seconds | |
| 8 | **Cutting Velocity (avg.)** | $V_c = \dfrac{2 L N_s}{1000}$ | m/min | $L$ in mm, $N_s$ = strokes/min |
| 9 | **Average Cutting Force relation** | $F_c = \dfrac{P \times 60}{V_c \times 1000}$ | kN | $P$ = power in kW |
| 10 | **Feed (per stroke)** | $f$ | mm/stroke | Typically $0.1$ to $2.0$ mm |
| 11 | **MRR (avg.)** | $\text{MRR} = V_c \times d \times f \times 10^{-6}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $d$ = depth of cut in mm |
| 12 | **Planer Stroke Ratio** | $Q_p = \dfrac{T_c}{T_r}$ | dimensionless | $T_c, T_r$ = cutting/return time |
| 13 | **Slotter Crank Angle (CSL)** | $\theta_c = 180° + 2\phi$ | degrees | $\phi$ = lever tilt |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Formatting:** All absolute-value bars $\vert x \vert$ have been written as `\vert` in formulas to preserve the markdown table structure. Do not use `|` inside any table row.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Shapers** are used in tool rooms, maintenance workshops, and small-batch production of **gibs, guides, and small machine beds**.
- **Planers** are indispensable in heavy-machinery industries (textile machines, turbines, marine engines) for machining **flat surfaces up to 15 m long**.
- **Slotters** are vital for **internal keyways in gears and pulleys**, and for **die and punch work** in sheet-metal tooling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quick-Return Ratio of a Crank-and-Slotted-Lever Shaper

### Given
- $R$ = crank radius (mm)
- $N$ = rpm of crank
- $N_s = N$ = strokes per minute of ram (= crank rpm in a 1:1 mechanism)
- $\alpha$ = angle the crank makes with the inner dead center (IDC) at the **start** of the cutting stroke
- $\beta$ = angle the crank makes with the IDC at the **end** of the cutting stroke

### Step 1 — Identify Dead Centers

When the crank pin is at the **Inner Dead Center (IDC)**, the slotted lever is closest to the crank centre, and the ram is at its **rearmost** position.
When the crank pin is at the **Outer Dead Center (ODC)** (top and bottom extremes), the ram reaches its **forward-most** position.

### Step 2 — Stroke Length Derivation

At the start of the cutting stroke, the pin is at angle $\alpha$ from IDC. The horizontal displacement of the lever end $A$ from IDC is:

$$
h_{\text{start}} = R \sin\alpha
$$

At the end of the cutting stroke, the pin is at angle $\beta$ on the other side of IDC. The displacement is:

$$
h_{\text{end}} = R \sin\beta
$$

Since the ram is rigidly coupled to point $A$ through the connecting rod (assuming rigid rod and horizontal motion of ram), the **total stroke length** of the ram is:

$$
L = h_{\text{start}} + h_{\text{end}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
L = R \sin\alpha + R \sin\beta
$$

### Step 3 — Crank Angles for Cutting and Return

- The crank rotates through $(\alpha + \beta)$ degrees during the **cutting stroke**.
- The crank rotates through $[360° - (\alpha + \beta)]$ degrees during the **return stroke**.

### Step 4 — Time for Each Stroke

Since crank rotates at $N$ rpm, angular speed in **degrees per second** is:

$$
\omega = \frac{360 N}{60} = 6N \quad \text{[°/s]}
$$

Time of cutting stroke:

$$
t_c = \frac{\alpha + \beta}{6N}
$$

Time of return stroke:

$$
t_r = \frac{360° - (\alpha + \beta)}{6N}
$$

### Step 5 — Quick-Return Ratio

$$
Q = \frac{t_c}{t_r}
$$

Substituting:

$$
Q = \frac{\dfrac{\alpha + \beta}{6N}}{\dfrac{360° - (\alpha + \beta)}{6N}}
$$

The $6N$ cancels:

$$
Q = \frac{\alpha + \beta}{360° - (\alpha + \beta)}
$$

> **Special Case — Symmetric Shaper ($\alpha = \beta$):**
> If $\alpha = \beta = \theta$, then $L = 2R \sin\theta$ and $Q = \dfrac{2\theta}{360° - 2\theta}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Crank Radius for a Given Stroke Length

Starting from:

$$
L = R \sin\alpha + R \sin\beta
$$

Solving for $R$:

$$
R = \frac{L}{\sin\alpha + \sin\beta}
$$

For the symmetric case:

$$
R = \frac{L}{2 \sin\theta}
$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-Style):** A shaper has a forward stroke of $L = 200$ mm. The crank angles are $\alpha = 30°$ and $\beta = 45°$. Find the crank radius and the quick-return ratio.

**Solution:**

$$
R = \frac{200}{\sin 30° + \sin 45°} = \frac{200}{0.5 + 0.7071} = \frac{200}{1.2071} \approx 165.7 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{30° + 45°}{360° - (30° + 45°)} = \frac{75°}{285°} \approx 0.263
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Q convention:** Some textbooks define $Q$ as the **inverse** ratio (return/cutting). The **KTU 2024 convention** is *cutting time ÷ return time*, giving $Q < 1$ when the cutting stroke is the shorter-duration event. Always check the convention by reading the **units and the wording** of the problem statement.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Planer Cutting Velocity

For a planer, the table performs the cutting stroke at an *average* velocity $V_c$ over a stroke length $L$ in time $t_c$:

$$
V_c = \frac{L}{t_c \times 1000} \quad \text{[m/min]}
$$

If the table makes $N_s$ complete (cutting + return) cycles per minute and the cutting-stroke angle ratio is $k = \theta_c / 360°$:

$$
t_c = \frac{60 k}{N_s} = \frac{60 \cdot \theta_c}{360 N_s} = \frac{\theta_c}{6 N_s}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_c = \frac{L \times 6 N_s}{1000 \cdot \theta_c} = \frac{6 L N_s}{1000 \cdot \theta_c} \quad \text{[m/min]}
$$

> **Alternative simpler form** (if the table moves at uniform average velocity and the cutting stroke is exactly $N_s$ cutting strokes per minute):

$$
V_c = \frac{2 L N_s}{1000} \quad \text{[m/min]}
$$

This is the **standard KTU textbook formula**, assuming half the cycle is cutting.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Shaper vs Planer vs Slotter

| Feature | Shaper | Planer | Slotter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocating member** | Tool (ram) | Workpiece (table) | Tool (ram) |
| **Direction of stroke** | Horizontal | Horizontal | Vertical (down-cut) |
| **Typical stroke length** | 200 – 900 mm | 1 m – 15 m | 50 – 300 mm |
| **Quick-return mechanism** | Crank & slotted lever | Rack-pinion / hydraulic | Crank & slotted lever |
| **Typical quick-return ratio** | 1.5 – 2.0 | 1.3 – 1.8 | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| **Number of tools per machine** | Usually 1 (sometimes 2) | Up to 8 (multi-tool head) | Usually 1 |
| **Workpiece size suitability** | Small to medium | Very large (heavy castings) | Small to medium |
| **Feed mechanism** | Hand-wheel / hydraulic (automatic) | Cross-rail power feed | Rotary table or linear feed |
| **Main use** | Flat surfaces, keyways, slots | Long flat surfaces on large parts | Internal keyways, die cavities |
| **Drive power** | 2 – 10 kW | 15 – 75 kW | 1.5 – 7.5 kW |
| **Specific application** | Tool-room jobs, prototypes | Marine/sugar/textile machine beds | Splines, internal slots |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Shaper

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Main Drive Motor] --> B[Gearbox with Speed Change Levers]
    B --> C[Crank Disc Rotating at N rpm]
    C --> D[Slotted Lever with Sliding Pin]
    D --> E[Connecting Rod AB]
    E --> F[Ram Slider with Tool Post]
    F --> G[Single-Point Cutting Tool]
    H[Feed Mechanism Hand Wheel] --> I[Cross-slide and Table]
    J[Workpiece Clamped] --> I
    C -. Quick-Return .-> D
    I -. Intermittent Feed per Stroke .-> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted arrows represent *control* and *timing* relationships, not power flow. The crank-to-lever dotted link indicates the **non-uniform reciprocating conversion** that produces the quick-return effect.

---

## 4.2 Crank-and-Slotted-Lever Kinematic Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph C [CRANK DISC - rotates 360 at constant omega]
        A1[Crank Centre O1]
        A2[Crank Pin at radius R]
    end
    subgraph L [SLOTTED LEVER - pivots at O2]
        B1[Lever Pivot O2 - on machine column]
        B2[Slotted Slot accommodates pin]
        B3[Lever Free End A]
    end
    subgraph R [RAM ASSEMBLY - moves horizontally]
        C1[Connecting Rod]
        C2[Ram Carriage on guideways]
        C3[Tool Holder and Tool]
    end
    A2 -- pin slides in slot --> B2
    B3 -- hinge joint --> C1
    C1 -- hinge joint --> C2
    C2 -- carries --> C3
```

---

## 4.3 Classification Hierarchy of Reciprocating Machine Tools

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RMT[Reciprocating Machine Tools]
    RMT --> S[Shaper]
    RMT --> P[Planer]
    RMT --> SL[Slotter]

    S --> S1[Standard Horizontal Shaper]
    S --> S2[Vertical Shaper]
    S --> S3[Universal Shaper]
    S --> S4[CNC Linear-Ram Shaper]

    P --> P1[Double-Housing Planer]
    P --> P2[Open-Side Planer]
    P --> P3[Edge Planer]
    P --> P4[Planer-Type Milling Machine]

    SL --> SL1[Standard Vertical Slotter]
    SL --> SL2[Keyway Slotter]
    SL --> SL3[Punch and Die Slotter]
```

---

## 4.4 Planer Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Main Drive Motor 15 to 75 kW] --> G1[Reduction Gearbox]
    G1 --> RK[Rack and Pinion Drive]
    RK --> TB[Long Worktable Moves Horizontally]
    TB --> WP[Heavy Workpiece Bolted on Table]
    CR[Cross Rail with Multiple Tool Heads] --> TL[Cutting Tools Stationary]
    TL -. cuts .-> WP
    F1[Feed Motor] --> CR
    TB -. returns fast, cuts slow .-> RK
```

---

## 4.5 Slotter Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M2[Drive Motor] --> GB[Gearbox]
    GB --> CR[Vertical Crank Disc]
    CR --> SL2[Vertical Slotted Lever]
    SL2 --> RM[Vertical Ram]
    RM --> TH[Tool Holder]
```
