```mermaid
    TH --> CUT[Cutting Tool moves down then up]
    WT[Circular Rotary Worktable] --> WP2[Workpiece]
    CUT -. cuts internal slot .-> WP2
    FM[Feed Motor] --> WT
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"List and briefly explain the three main types of reciprocating machine tools. State one distinguishing feature of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Shaping Machine** — A reciprocating machine tool in which a *single-point tool mounted on a ram* moves in a horizontal straight line over a *stationary workpiece*. Distinguishing feature: tool reciprocates, workpiece is held fixed; used for small to medium flat surfaces and keyways.

2. **Planning Machine** — A reciprocating machine tool in which the *worktable carrying the workpiece* moves in a straight line under a *stationary tool head*. Distinguishing feature: very long table strokes (1–15 m) to machine large castings.

3. **Slotting Machine** — A reciprocating machine tool in which the *tool moves vertically* (cutting stroke downward) over a workpiece mounted on a rotary table. Distinguishing feature: vertical orientation, ideal for internal slots and keyways.

*(1 mark for each correct machine + feature = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Define quick-return ratio. Why is it always greater than unity in conventional shapers?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **quick-return ratio (Q)** is defined as the ratio of the *time taken for the cutting stroke* to the *time taken for the return stroke* of the ram:

$$
Q = \frac{t_c}{t_r} = \frac{\alpha + \beta}{360° - (\alpha + \beta)}
$$

It is **greater than unity** because the crank rotates at *constant* angular velocity, and the crank angle subtended during the cutting stroke $(\alpha + \beta)$ is **deliberately made larger** than the angle during the return stroke $[360° - (\alpha + \beta)]$. Since time ∝ angle (at constant $\omega$), a larger cutting angle ⇒ longer cutting time ⇒ $t_c > t_r$ ⇒ $Q > 1$.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for "why greater than 1" = 3 marks)*

---

## 📗 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module-1 Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Module 1, KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a crank and slotted lever quick-return mechanism** used in a shaping machine. (7 marks)

**(b)** In a shaper using a crank and slotted lever mechanism, the crank radius is **75 mm** and the crank rotates at **30 rpm**. The angle of the crank at the beginning of the cutting stroke is **20°** and at the end is **50°** from the inner dead center. Calculate:
- (i) Length of the stroke
- (ii) Quick-return ratio
- (iii) Time taken for cutting and return strokes
- (iv) Average cutting speed in m/min
*(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**Construction:**
The mechanism consists of a **crank disc** of radius $R$ rotating about a fixed centre $O_1$, a **slotted lever** pivoted at $O_2$ on the machine column, a **sliding pin** attached to the crank and free to slide inside the slot of the lever, and a **connecting rod** that links the free end $A$ of the slotted lever to the ram slider at $B$. The ram reciprocates on horizontal guideways and carries the tool holder.

**Working:**
The crank rotates at a uniform angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ rad/s. The pin, being rigidly fixed to the crank at radius $R$, traces a circle. As it does, it forces the slotted lever to oscillate about $O_2$. This oscillation, when transmitted via the connecting rod to the ram, produces a to-and-fro (reciprocating) linear motion of the ram.

**Quick-return action:**
When the crank pin moves through the **upper** portion of its circle, the slotted lever tilts more rapidly, and the ram moves a long distance in a short time (return stroke). When the pin moves through the **lower** portion (where the lever has to swing through a larger arc), the ram travels at a slower average speed (cutting stroke). Thus, the cutting stroke takes a longer time and the return stroke is quick — hence *quick-return*.

**Sketch description (textual):**
- Crank $O_1P$ rotates clockwise.
- Lever $O_2A$ swings between two extreme positions.
- The angles $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are measured from the *inner dead centre* (IDC) — the position where the crank pin is closest to $O_2$ — to the start and end of the cutting stroke respectively.
- The total crank rotation during the cutting stroke is $(\alpha + \beta)$, and during the return stroke is $[360° - (\alpha + \beta)]$.

*(1 mark sketch, 3 marks construction, 2 marks working, 1 mark quick-return explanation = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:**
- Crank radius $R = 75$ mm
- Crank speed $N = 30$ rpm
- $\alpha = 20°$, $\beta = 50°$

**(i) Length of the Stroke:**

$$
L = R \sin\alpha + R \sin\beta
$$

$$
L = 75 \sin 20° + 75 \sin 50°
$$

$$
L = 75 (0.3420) + 75 (0.7660)
$$

$$
L = 25.65 + 57.45 = 83.10 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Quick-Return Ratio:**

$$
Q = \frac{\alpha + \beta}{360° - (\alpha + \beta)} = \frac{20° + 50°}{360° - 70°} = \frac{70°}{290°} \approx 0.241
$$

> **KTU Convention Check:** This $Q < 1$ because the *cutting stroke angle* is *smaller* than the *return stroke angle* in this case (rare but mathematically valid). In most industrial shapers the *cutting* subtends the *larger* angle. Always re-examine the wording: if "forward stroke = cutting", then usually $Q > 1$.

**(iii) Time for Cutting and Return Strokes:**

Angular velocity of crank in degrees per second:

$$
\omega = 6N = 6 \times 30 = 180 \text{ °/s}
$$

Time for cutting stroke:

$$
t_c = \frac{\alpha + \beta}{\omega} = \frac{70°}{180} = 0.389 \text{ s}
$$

Time for return stroke:

$$
t_r = \frac{360° - 70°}{180} = \frac{290°}{180} = 1.611 \text{ s}
$$

**(iv) Average Cutting Speed:**

$$
V_c = \frac{L}{t_c \times 1000} \times 60 = \frac{83.10 \text{ mm}}{0.389 \text{ s}} \times \frac{1 \text{ m}}{1000 \text{ mm}} \times 60 \text{ s/min}
$$

$$
V_c = \frac{83.10}{0.389} \times 0.06 \approx 213.6 \times 0.06 \approx 12.82 \text{ m/min}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $L = 83.1$ mm
- $Q = 0.241$ (or its inverse, depending on convention)
- $t_c = 0.389$ s, $t_r = 1.611$ s
- $V_c \approx 12.8$ m/min

*(1 mark each for parts i–iv + 1 mark for units & formula = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Module 1, Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** Compare the constructional and operational features of a **shaper and a planer** in a tabular form. State at least **six** points of difference. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of a slotting machine**. List **four typical operations** performed on it. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to B(a):**

| # | Feature | Shaper | Planer |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reciprocating member | Tool (ram) | Workpiece (table) |
| 2 | Stroke length | Short (200–900 mm) | Long (1–15 m) |
| 3 | Workpiece size | Small to medium | Very large and heavy |
| 4 | Tool head | Single-point on a moving ram | Multiple tools on stationary cross-rail |
| 5 | Quick-return mechanism | Crank & slotted lever | Rack-pinion / hydraulic |
| 6 | Productivity | Low (1 tool) | High (multi-tool simultaneous) |
| 7 | Cost | Lower | Higher |
| 8 | Floor space required | Less | More |

*(1 mark per correct row × 6 = 6 marks + 1 mark for tabular format and neatness = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to B(b):**

**Working Principle (Block Diagram in Words):**
A motor drives a gearbox that rotates a vertical crank disc. The crank pin engages a vertical slotted lever pivoted at the machine column. As the crank rotates, the slotted lever oscillates, and via a connecting rod, drives a **vertical ram** carrying the tool holder in a *downward cutting* and *upward return* motion. The workpiece is clamped on a **circular rotary worktable** which can be indexed for successive cuts (e.g., for cutting multiple keyways). An automatic feed mechanism advances the rotary table in small increments between strokes.

**Four Typical Operations:**
1. **Internal keyway cutting** in gears, pulleys, and shaft hubs.
2. **Internal spline** machining in hub-type components.
3. **Square or rectangular pocket cutting** in dies and fixtures.
4. **Cylindrical surface grooving** (using a circular table and profiled tool).

*(3 marks for block-diagram description, 2 marks for naming operations, 2 marks for correct principle = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up which member reciprocates** — remember: *shaper & slotter = tool reciprocates*, *planer = workpiece reciprocates*. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert angular velocity** — using $\omega$ in rad/s in a degree-based numerator is the #1 calculation error. Always write $\omega = 6N$ °/s when angles are in degrees.
> 3. **Mis-defining Quick-Return Ratio** — KTU has *two* conventions in its question bank: $Q = t_c/t_r$ and $Q = t_r/t_c$. **Always state the convention** at the start of your answer.
> 4. **Confusing crank radius with stroke length** — Stroke length is *not* $2R$; it depends on $\alpha$ and $\beta$. The full equation $L = R(\sin\alpha + \sin\beta)$ is mandatory.
> 5. **Ignoring units** — "$V_c$ in m/min" vs "m/s" vs "mm/s" — examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing/incorrect unit conversion.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Three reciprocating machines:** Shaper (tool reciprocates, horizontal), Planer (workpiece reciprocates, horizontal, very long), Slotter (tool reciprocates, vertical).
- **Common core feature:** Quick-return mechanism (mechanical CSL or hydraulic/servo).
- **Quick-Return Ratio** $Q = \dfrac{\alpha + \beta}{360° - (\alpha + \beta)}$ — always *state the convention*.
- **Stroke Length** $L = R(\sin\alpha + \sin\beta)$ — for crank-and-slotted-lever.
- **Symmetric shaper shortcut:** If $\alpha = \beta = \theta$, then $L = 2R \sin\theta$ and $Q = \dfrac{2\theta}{360° - 2\theta}$.
- **Angular velocity conversion:** $\omega$ in °/s = $6N$ where $N$ is in rpm.
- **Average cutting velocity:** $V_c = \dfrac{2 L N_s}{1000}$ m/min (general shaper formula).
- **Time per stroke** $t_c = \dfrac{\theta_c}{6N}$ and $t_r = \dfrac{\theta_r}{6N}$.
- **Planer distinction:** Multiple tool heads on a cross-rail → higher productivity.
- **Slotter distinction:** Vertical ram + rotary worktable → internal slots & keyways.
- **MRR for reciprocating machines:** $\text{MRR} = V_c \times d \times f \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s.
- **CNC evolution:** Modern CNC shapers/planers/slotters replace the mechanical CSL with **servo-driven ball screws**, giving programmable stroke lengths and Q ratios.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drilling Machines - Operations, Drill Bit Nomenclature, Process Parameters, Machining Time Calculations and Cutting Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Drilling Machines - Operations, Drill Bit Nomenclature, Process Parameters, Machining Time Calculations and Cutting Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Drilling Machine?

A **drilling machine** is a power-driven machine tool designed primarily to produce cylindrical holes in a workpiece by rotating a multi-point cutting tool (the *drill bit*) against a stationary (or slowly rotating) workpiece. The basic motion is a *rotary primary motion* provided to the drill and a *linear secondary motion* (axial feed) that drives the drill into the material.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, drilling is classified as a **non-conventional generative shaping process** where material is removed by a tool having **two cutting edges** that work simultaneously on both lips at the drill point.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Style):** "Drilling is a hole-making operation in which a multi-point tool (twist drill) is rotated and simultaneously fed axially to produce a round hole of definite diameter and depth."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a drilling machine as a **mechanized hole-punch**:

- Imagine pushing a sharp, spinning pencil into a sheet of paper. The pencil must *rotate* (otherwise it just tears the paper) and *push forward* (otherwise no hole is made). The drilling machine automates this exact twin action.
- The **twist drill** is the spinning pencil; the **workpiece** is the paper; the **chuck** holds the pencil; and the **spindle** drives it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Module:**
> - Standard drill point angle: **118°** (general purpose for mild steel)
> - Standard helix angle of twist drill: **20°–30°**
> - Specific cutting pressure **Kc** for mild steel: **~2000 N/mm²**
> - Standard machine efficiency **η**: **80% (0.8)** unless stated otherwise
> - 1 H.P. = **746 W**; 1 kW = **102 kgf·m/s**

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (For KTU Context)

| Type | Drive | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Bench Drilling Machine | Hand-feed | Light, small workpieces |
| Pillar / Upright Drilling | Hand or auto | Medium jobs, workshops |
| Radial Drilling Machine | Arm-mounted | Large/heavy workpieces |
| Gang / Multi-spindle | Multiple spindles | Mass production |
| CNC Drilling | Numerical control | Precision, automation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operations Performed on a Drilling Machine

A drilling machine is not just for *drilling* — it is a versatile hole-making center. KTU expects you to know the following operations and differentiate them clearly:

1. **Drilling** — Producing a new hole using a twist drill (most common).
2. **Reaming** — Enlarging and finishing a pre-drilled hole using a multi-tooth reamer for high accuracy (IT7) and fine surface finish.
3. **Boring** — Enlarging an existing hole using a single-point boring tool on a lathe or boring bar (not a drill).
4. **Tapping** — Cutting internal threads using a tap (requires reversal of spindle at bottom).
5. **Counterboring** — Producing a cylindrical recess at the entry of a hole to seat a bolt head using a counterbore tool.
6. **Countersinking** — Producing a conical recess at the hole entry to accommodate a flat-head screw using a countersink (typically 82° or 90°).
7. **Spot Facing** — Smoothing and squaring a rough cast/forged surface to provide a flat seat for a bolt head/washer.
8. **Trepaning** — Cutting an annular groove to produce a circular disk, leaving a solid cylindrical core.

## 2.2 Twist Drill Nomenclature (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the labelled parts of a twist drill** — KTU frequently asks students to draw and label a twist drill, or identify parts from a diagram.

Key parts:

- **Body / Shank** — The driving portion held in the chuck. Cylindrical (≤ 12.7 mm) or tapered (Morse Taper) for larger sizes.
- **Point / Drill Point** — The conical cutting end, ground to a **point angle** of 118° (standard), 135° (for hard materials), or 90° (for soft, non-ferrous).
- **Cutting Lips / Cutting Edges** — Two sharp straight edges that perform the actual cutting. They must be of *equal length* and at *equal angles* with the axis; otherwise the drill produces oversized or undersized holes.
- **Flutes** — Helical grooves that:
  (a) Provide chip clearance,
  (b) Allow cutting fluid to reach the cutting lips,
  (c) Form the cutting edge.
- **Margin / Land** — Narrow cylindrical strip along the flute that provides a bearing surface and guides the drill in the hole.
- **Neck** — The portion between the body and shank where the drill size is engraved.
- **Clearance / Lip Relief** — The angle behind the cutting lip (typically 8°–12°) to prevent rubbing.
- **Chisel Edge** — The straight edge at the very tip connecting the two cutting lips. It does **not cut** but **plunges/pushes** the material.
- **Heel** — The trailing edge of the cutting lip.

## 2.3 Process Parameters

The four governing process parameters for drilling (just like turning) are:

1. **Cutting Speed (V)** — Surface speed at the drill periphery, in m/min.
2. **Spindle Speed (N)** — Rotational speed of the drill, in rpm.
3. **Feed (f)** — Linear axial movement of the drill per minute, in mm/min.
4. **Feed per Revolution (f_r)** — Axial advance of the drill per revolution, in mm/rev.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam essentials. Memorize the table — it covers 90% of numerical problems on drilling.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | m/min | D in mm, N in rpm |
| 2 | Spindle speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$ | rpm | Direct re-arrangement |
| 3 | Linear feed | $f = f_r \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f_r$ = feed/rev |
| 4 | Feed per rev | $f_r = \dfrac{f}{N}$ | mm/rev | Often given directly |
| 5 | Feed per flute (chip thickness) | $f_z = \dfrac{f_r}{2}$ | mm/tooth | 2 flutes for twist drill |
| 6 | Material Removal Rate (blind) | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot f$ | mm³/min | Treats drill as solid cylinder |
| 7 | Material Removal Rate (through) | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{L}{T_m}$ | mm³/min | Used with machining time |
| 8 | Machining Time | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | min | L = effective drilled length |
| 9 | Effective drilled length | $L = l + \dfrac{D}{2} + l_o$ | mm | Approach + depth + overrun |
| 10 | Approach distance (118° point) | $l_a = \dfrac{D}{2} \cot 59° \approx 0.3 D$ | mm | Half cone height of drill point |
| 11 | Thrust force (empirical) | $F_T = K_c \cdot f_r \cdot D$ | N | For mild steel Kc ≈ 2000 N/mm² |
| 12 | Cutting force (per flute) | $F_z = K_c \cdot f_z \cdot D$ | N | $f_z$ = $f_r/2$ |
| 13 | Total cutting force (2 flutes) | $F_c = 2 F_z = K_c \cdot f_r \cdot D$ | N | Two cutting lips |
| 14 | Torque (drilling) | $T = F_c \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$ | N·mm | Couple arm = D/2 |
| 15 | Power (gross) | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | W | N in rpm, T in N·m |
| 16 | Power (kW from V, Fc) | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta}$ | kW | η = efficiency |
| 17 | Spindle power (H.P.) | $P_{HP} = \dfrac{P_W}{746}$ | H.P. | 1 HP = 746 W |
| 18 | Indexing time for tapping | $T_{tap} = \dfrac{L}{N_{tap} \cdot p}$ | min | p = thread pitch (mm/rev) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention for thrust force sign convention:** The axial force $F_T$ always acts *pushing on the drill* (i.e., along the drill axis) and is the load the feed mechanism must overcome. The tangential force $F_c$ acts at the cutting lip periphery and creates torque.

## 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

- **Choosing V and f correctly** controls tool life, surface finish, and productivity. A 10% increase in cutting speed roughly *halves* tool life (Taylor's tool-life equation).
- **Thrust force** determines the strength of the drill, the feed motor sizing, and the tendency of the workpiece to deflect.
- **Torque** sets the spindle power and chuck capacity.
- **Machining time** is the direct driver of cycle time and cost per part in production planning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cutting Speed and Spindle Speed

The drill, when rotating at N rpm with diameter D, traces a circle of circumference $\pi D$ per revolution.

Step 1 — Distance travelled at the periphery per minute:

$$\text{Linear distance per min} = N \times \pi D$$

where D is in **mm** and N is in **revolutions per minute**. The result is in **mm/min**.

Step 2 — Convert mm to m by dividing by 1000:

$$V = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} \;\;\; \text{m/min}$$

Step 3 — Re-arrange for N:

$$N = \frac{1000 \, V}{\pi D} \;\;\; \text{rpm}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Material Removal Rate in Drilling

In drilling, the cross-section being removed is equivalent to a solid cylinder of diameter D (the drill sweeps its full disc area, since it is a flat-end tool, not a side-milling cutter).

Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the hole:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

Step 2 — Volume removed per minute = Area × axial feed per minute:

$$MRR = A \cdot f = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \cdot f_r \cdot N$$

where $f = f_r N$ is the linear feed in mm/min.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Machining Time

The drill advances $f$ mm in one minute. To drill a hole of effective length L, the time required is:

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f} = \frac{L}{f_r \cdot N} \;\;\; \text{minutes}$$

The **effective length L** is *not* just the hole depth l. It includes:
- $l$ = specified hole depth (in mm)
- $l_a$ = approach distance = drill-point runout. For a 118° point, the cone height is $\frac{D}{2}\cot(59°)$ ≈ 0.3 D.
- $l_o$ = overrun / overtravel (typically 1–3 mm) to ensure clean breakthrough.

$$L = l + \frac{D}{2} \cot 59° + l_o \approx l + 0.3 D + l_o$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cutting Force, Thrust Force, and Torque

The twist drill has **two cutting lips** (two flutes, two cutting edges). Each lip removes a chip of:

- **Feed per lip (chip thickness):** $t_1 = \dfrac{f_r}{2}$
- **Chip width (= lip length ≈ D/2):** $b = \dfrac{D}{2}$

Step 1 — Empirical cutting force on ONE lip (tangential, at the periphery):

$$F_{z} = K_c \cdot t_1 \cdot b = K_c \cdot \frac{f_r}{2} \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{K_c \, f_r \, D}{4}$$

Step 2 — Total tangential force on drill (sum of two lips):

$$F_c = 2 F_{z} = \frac{K_c \, f_r \, D}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Many KTU textbooks (Pandey & Shan, Ghosh & Mallik) simplify and write the *tangential* force as $F_c \approx K_c f_r D$ and the *thrust* force (axial) as $F_T \approx 1.1 \, K_c \, f_r \, D$ or $F_T \approx K_c f_r D / 2$ depending on lip geometry. The coefficient depends on point angle, helix angle, and material.

Step 3 — Torque required to drive the drill:

The tangential force $F_c$ acts at radius $D/2$. Torque = Force × arm.

$$T = F_c \cdot \frac{D}{2} = \frac{K_c \, f_r \, D^{2}}{4} \;\;\; \text{N·mm}$$

Step 4 — Spindle Power:

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \;\;\; \text{W (if T in N·m)}$$

Step 5 — Account for machine efficiency η:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P}{\eta} = \frac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \cdot 1000 \cdot \eta} \;\;\; \text{kW}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 1 (Standard KTU Pattern)

> A 20 mm diameter hole is to be drilled in a mild-steel plate 40 mm thick. Cutting speed = 25 m/min, feed = 0.20 mm/rev. Specific cutting pressure $K_c = 2000$ N/mm². Drill point angle = 118°. Machine efficiency = 80%. Find:
> (a) Spindle speed N
> (b) Machining time $T_m$
> (c) Thrust and cutting forces
> (d) Torque
> (e) Spindle power

**Given:** D = 20 mm, l = 40 mm, V = 25 m/min, $f_r$ = 0.20 mm/rev, $K_c$ = 2000 N/mm², η = 0.8.

### (a) Spindle Speed

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 20}$$

$$\pi \times 20 = 62.832$$

$$N = \frac{25000}{62.832} = 397.9 \approx 398 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

### (b) Effective Length & Machining Time

Approach for 118° point: $\dfrac{D}{2}\cot 59° = 10 \times 0.6009 = 6.0$ mm (or use rule 0.3 D = 6 mm).

Overrun: assume $l_o = 2$ mm.

$$L = 40 + 6 + 2 = 48 \;\; \text{mm}$$

Linear feed $f = f_r N = 0.20 \times 398 = 79.6$ mm/min.

$$T_m = \frac{L}{f} = \frac{48}{79.6} = 0.603 \;\; \text{min} \approx 36.2 \;\; \text{seconds}$$

### (c) Cutting and Thrust Forces

$$F_c = K_c \cdot f_r \cdot D = 2000 \times 0.20 \times 20 = 8000 \;\; \text{N}$$

$$F_T \approx 1.1 \cdot F_c = 1.1 \times 8000 = 8800 \;\; \text{N (using common ratio)}$$

> Or using the textbook variant: $F_T = \dfrac{K_c f_r D}{2} = 4000$ N (lower-bound estimate).

### (d) Torque

$$T = F_c \cdot \frac{D}{2} = 8000 \times \frac{20}{2} = 8000 \times 10 = 80{,}000 \;\; \text{N·mm} = 80 \;\; \text{N·m}$$

### (e) Spindle Power

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \cdot \eta} = \frac{2 \pi \times 398 \times 80}{60 \times 0.8} = \frac{200{,}063}{48} = 4168 \;\; \text{W} \approx 4.17 \;\; \text{kW}$$

In HP: $P = 4168 / 746 = 5.59$ H.P.

## 3.6 Worked Example 2 — Machining Time for Tapping

> A M12 × 1.75 mm hole is to be tapped on a drilling machine. Hole depth = 30 mm. Spindle speed during tapping = 60 rpm. Find tapping time.

**Given:** Pitch p = 1.75 mm, l = 30 mm, l_a = 0.3 D = 0.3 × 12 = 3.6 mm, l_o = 2 mm. Total L = 30 + 3.6 + 2 = 35.6 mm. Feed during tapping = pitch × N = 1.75 × 60 = 105 mm/min.

$$T_{tap} = \frac{L}{N \cdot p} = \frac{35.6}{60 \times 1.75} = \frac{35.6}{105} = 0.339 \;\; \text{min} \approx 20.3 \;\; \text{s}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of a Drilling Machine (Power-Flow Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[AC Electric Motor] --> G1[Stepped Pulley Belt Drive]
    G1 --> S1[Spindle Assembly]
    S1 --> C1[Drill Chuck]
    C1 --> D1[Twist Drill Bit]
    D1 --> W1[Workpiece Clamped on Table]
    F1[Feed Handle / Power Feed] --> S1
    T1[Column and Base] --> S1
    T1 --> W1
    G1 --> F1
    S1 -. produces .-> RX1[Rotary Motion plus Axial Feed]
    RX1 --> D1
```

## 4.2 Drill Bit Nomenclature Block Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Drill Shank] --> A2[Neck with Size Engraved]
    A2 --> A3[Body with Flutes and Margins]
    A3 --> A4[Point Region]
    A4 --> A5[Cutting Lip 1]
    A4 --> A6[Cutting Lip 2]
    A4 --> A7[Chisel Edge at Centre]
    A4 --> A8[Lip Clearance Angle 8 to 12 deg]
    A4 --> A9[Point Angle 118 deg Standard]
    A5 --> A10[Flute Helix Angle 20 to 30 deg]
    A6 --> A10
```

## 4.3 Drilling Operation Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start Hole Making Requirement] --> Q1{Is it a new hole?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is hole blind or through?}
    Q2 -- Blind --> OP1[Drilling with Twist Drill]
    Q2 -- Through --> OP1
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Need higher accuracy or finish?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> OP2[Reaming]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Need internal thread?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> OP5[Tapping]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5{Need a flat seat recess?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> OP6[Counterboring or Spot Facing]
    Q5 -- No --> OP7[Larger diameter needed]
    OP7 -- Yes --> OP3[Boring]
    OP7 -- No --> OP8[Countersink for conical seat]
    OP1 --> END[Operation Complete]
    OP2 --> END
    OP3 --> END
    OP5 --> END
    OP6 --> END
    OP8 --> END
```

## 4.4 Force Analysis on a Twist Drill Lip

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LIP[Cutting Lip of Twist Drill] --> FC[Tangential Force Fc along Rotation Direction]
    LIP --> FR[Radial Force Fr pointing toward Drill Axis]
    LIP --> FA[Axial Force Fa pushing into Workpiece]
    FC -- arm D over 2 --> TOR[Drilling Torque T = Fc times D by 2]
    FA --> THR[Thrust Load on Spindle Feed Mechanism]
    FR -- cancels between two lips --> EQ[Net radial force approx zero for symmetric drill]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is net radial force ≈ 0?** In a *perfectly ground* twist drill, the two cutting lips are symmetric, so their radial components cancel. If the lips are unequal in length or angle, a net radial force deflects the drill, producing oversized holes — a key KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`
**List any six operations that can be performed on a drilling machine.**

**Model Answer:**
A drilling machine can perform the following operations:
1. **Drilling** — producing a new hole using a twist drill.
2. **Reaming** — finishing a pre-drilled hole for accuracy and surface finish.
3. **Boring** — enlarging an existing hole using a single-point tool.
4. **Tapping** — cutting internal threads with a tap.
5. **Counterboring** — producing a cylindrical recess for bolt heads.
6. **Countersinking** — producing a conical recess for flat-head screws.

*(Any six, 0.5 mark each — total 3 marks)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**Define (a) point angle, (b) helix angle, and (c) lip clearance angle of a twist drill. State their standard values.**

**Model Answer:**
- **(a) Point Angle** — The angle included between the two cutting lips measured in a plane containing the drill axis. **Standard value = 118°** for general-purpose drilling in mild steel; 135° for hard materials; 90° for soft non-ferrous metals.
- **(b) Helix Angle** — The angle between the flute (helix) and the drill axis. **Standard value = 20°–30°** (low helix for hard, high helix for soft/ductile materials).
- **(c) Lip Clearance Angle** — The angle measured at the cutting lip between the lip face and a tangent to the periphery just behind the edge. **Standard value = 8°–12°.** It prevents rubbing of the flank against the workpiece.

*(1 mark each definition + standard value pair — total 3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module-2 Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Module 2]`

**(a) With a neat sketch, describe the nomenclature of a twist drill. Highlight the function of the chisel edge and the flutes. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

A neat sketch of the twist drill (point region) should show: shank, body, neck, flutes, margins, point, cutting lips, chisel edge, lip clearance, point angle, and helix angle. *(Sketch 3 marks)*

- **Chisel Edge** — The straight edge connecting the two cutting lips at the very tip. It is *negative-rake* and does not cut in the conventional sense. Instead, it **plunges/pushes** into the material, creating a squeezing and indentation action. Approximately **50% of the total thrust force** in drilling is generated by the chisel edge. *(2 marks)*
- **Flutes** — Helical grooves running along the body. They perform three simultaneous functions: (i) **chip evacuation** (carry chips out of the hole), (ii) **cutting fluid supply** (allow coolant to reach the cutting lips), (iii) **cutting-edge formation** (the intersection of the flute helix with the flank surface creates the cutting edge). *(2 marks)*

---

**(b) A 25 mm diameter hole is drilled through a 50 mm thick mild-steel plate. Cutting speed = 20 m/min and feed = 0.25 mm/rev. Specific cutting pressure = 2000 N/mm². Point angle = 118° and machine efficiency = 80%. Determine: (i) spindle speed, (ii) machining time, (iii) cutting and thrust forces, (iv) torque, (v) power in kW. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** D = 25 mm, l = 50 mm, V = 20 m/min, $f_r$ = 0.25 mm/rev, $K_c$ = 2000 N/mm², η = 0.8.

**(i) Spindle speed N:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 25} = \frac{20000}{78.54} = 254.6 \approx 255 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**(ii) Machining time:**

Approach $l_a = 0.3 \times 25 = 7.5$ mm. Overrun $l_o = 2$ mm.

$$L = 50 + 7.5 + 2 = 59.5 \;\; \text{mm}$$

Linear feed $f = 0.25 \times 255 = 63.75$ mm/min.

$$T_m = \frac{59.5}{63.75} = 0.933 \;\; \text{min} = 56 \;\; \text{seconds}$$

**(iii) Forces:**

$$F_c = K_c \cdot f_r \cdot D = 2000 \times 0.25 \times 25 = 12{,}500 \;\; \text{N}$$

$$F_T = 1.1 \cdot F_c = 13{,}750 \;\; \text{N}$$

**(iv) Torque:**

$$T = F_c \cdot \frac{D}{2} = 12500 \times 12.5 = 156{,}250 \;\; \text{N·mm} = 156.25 \;\; \text{N·m}$$

**(v) Power:**

$$P = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times \eta} = \frac{2 \pi \times 255 \times 156.25}{60 \times 0.8} = \frac{250{,}260}{48} = 5214 \;\; \text{W} \approx 5.21 \;\; \text{kW}$$

*(Step marking: 1 mark each for (i), (ii), 1.5 for (iii), 1 for (iv), 1.5 for (v) = 7 marks)*

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a) Explain the following drilling operations with neat sketches: (i) counterboring, (ii) countersinking, (iii) spot facing. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer Outline:**

- **(i) Counterboring** — Producing a *cylindrical flat-bottomed recess* at the entry of a hole to seat a socket-head (Allen) bolt. The tool is a *counterbore* with a pilot that fits the drilled hole. (Sketch + 2 marks)
- **(ii) Countersinking** — Producing a *conical recess* (typically 82° or 90°) to seat a flat-head wood/machine screw. The tool is a conical cutter with a pilot. (Sketch + 2 marks)
- **(iii) Spot Facing** — A *shallow counterbore* used to level a rough cast or forged surface to provide a smooth flat seat for a bolt-head or washer. Essentially a milling-type finishing of a small area. (Sketch + 2 marks)
- Distinguishing table (counterbore cylindrical, countersink conical, spot face very shallow). (1 mark)

---

**(b) A hole of 15 mm diameter and 30 mm depth is to be drilled in cast iron. Cutting speed = 18 m/min, feed = 0.15 mm/rev. Find: (i) approach distance, (ii) spindle speed, (iii) feed in mm/min, (iv) machining time assuming overrun of 2 mm. Take specific cutting pressure for cast iron as 1500 N/mm² and compute the (v) torque and (vi) power at 80% efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** D = 15 mm, l = 30 mm, V = 18 m/min, $f_r$ = 0.15 mm/rev, $l_o$ = 2 mm, $K_c$ = 1500 N/mm², η = 0.8.

**(i) Approach distance (118° point):**

$$l_a = 0.3 D = 0.3 \times 15 = 4.5 \;\; \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Spindle speed:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \times 18}{\pi \times 15} = \frac{18000}{47.12} = 382 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**(iii) Linear feed:**

$$f = 0.15 \times 382 = 57.3 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

**(iv) Machining time:**

$$L = 30 + 4.5 + 2 = 36.5 \;\; \text{mm}$$

$$T_m = \frac{36.5}{57.3} = 0.637 \;\; \text{min} = 38.2 \;\; \text{seconds}$$

**(v) Torque:**

$$F_c = K_c f_r D = 1500 \times 0.15 \times 15 = 3375 \;\; \text{N}$$

$$T = F_c \cdot \frac{D}{2} = 3375 \times 7.5 = 25{,}312.5 \;\; \text{N·mm} \approx 25.3 \;\; \text{N·m}$$

**(vi) Power:**

$$P = \frac{2 \pi \times 382 \times 25.3}{60 \times 0.8} = \frac{60{,}720}{48} = 1265 \;\; \text{W} \approx 1.27 \;\; \text{kW}$$

*(Step marking 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Module-2 drilling questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the approach distance (≈ 0.3 D).** Examiners deliberately reduce marks if you use only the nominal hole depth in $T_m$. Always write $L = l + 0.3 D + l_o$.
> 2. **Mixing units in cutting-speed formula.** D must be in **mm**, N in **rpm**, V in **m/min** for $V = \pi D N / 1000$ to be dimensionally correct.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for two cutting lips** when using $F_c = K_c f_r D / 4$ form. KTU expects you to clearly state "per lip" vs "total".
> 4. **Not dividing by efficiency η** when computing motor power. Theoretical power < motor power, so $P_{motor} = P / \eta$.
> 5. **Confusing feed per revolution with feed per flute (chip thickness).** $f_r = 2 f_z$ for a two-flute drill.
> 6. **Assuming the chisel edge contributes zero to thrust.** Approximately 50% of the thrust is generated by the chisel edge — this is a frequent viva question.
> 7. **Wrong formula for tapping time.** Tapping feed = pitch × N (NOT mm/rev from a table).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Drilling (Module 2)**

- Drilling is a **rotary + axial feed** hole-making process using a **twist drill** (2 cutting lips).
- Standard **point angle = 118°**; helix = 20°–30°; lip clearance = 8°–12°.
- **Chisel edge ≈ 50% of total thrust force**; it does not cut, it punches.
- **Flutes** do three jobs: chip evacuation, coolant supply, cutting-edge formation.
- Cutting speed $V = \pi D N / 1000$ m/min (D in mm).
- **MRR** in drilling = full disc area × feed = $\pi D^2 f / 4$.
- Effective drilled length **L = l + 0.3 D + l_o** (must include approach + overrun).
- $F_c = K_c f_r D$ (total tangential); $F_T \approx 1.1 F_c$ (axial thrust).
- Torque $T = F_c \cdot D/2$; Power $P = 2 \pi N T / (60 \eta)$ in W.
- Six operations: **Drilling, Reaming, Boring, Tapping, Counterboring, Countersinking** (plus Spot Facing and Trepaning as bonus).
- Radial force cancels for a symmetric drill; **asymmetric grinding = oversized hole**.
- Tapping feed = pitch × N (not a table feed); spindle reverses at bottom.
- For mass production, use **multi-spindle / gang drilling**; for large workpieces, use **radial drilling**.
- 1 HP = 746 W; 1 kW = 102 kgf·m/s. **Always include η** in motor-power calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Milling Machines - Types, Milling Operations, Types of Milling Cutters, Milling Cutter Nomenclature, Process Parameters, Machining Time Calculations and Cutting Forces
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Milling Machines - Types, Milling Operations, Types of Milling Cutters, Milling Cutter Nomenclature, Process Parameters, Machining Time Calculations and Cutting Forces

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Milling

**Milling** is a fundamental material-removal machining process in which a multi-point rotary cutting tool (called a **milling cutter**) removes material from a stationary or linearly fed workpiece. The cutting action is achieved through the progressive engagement of successive teeth, with each tooth removing a discrete chip of material as it rotates past the workpiece.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Milling is an interrupted cutting process in which the cutting tool (cutter) rotates about its own axis while the workpiece is fed linearly against one or more cutting edges. The chip thickness varies cyclically from zero to a maximum and back to zero as each tooth enters and exits the cut.*

Mathematically, milling is classified as a **multi-point, interrupted, peripheral (or face) cutting process** governed by the relationship:

$$ v_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N $$

where $v_c$ is the cutting velocity in **m/min**, $D$ is the cutter diameter in **mm**, and $N$ is the spindle rotational speed in **rev/min**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spinning Pizza Cutter"

Imagine a **pizza cutter rolling across dough**: as the disc rotates, only the small portion of the blade currently in contact with the dough actually cuts. The rest of the wheel is "wasted" motion. Now scale this up — instead of a flat disc with one contact line, give the wheel **20–60 small teeth around its rim**, each acting like a tiny chisel. As the wheel spins, each tooth briefly plunges into the material, scoops out a tiny chip, and retracts. The workpiece (or the wheel) is slowly advanced, and gradually a slot, surface, or complex profile emerges.

| Real-World Analogy | Milling Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Pizza cutter wheel | Rotating milling cutter |
| Pizza dough | Workpiece |
| Tiny teeth on the wheel | Milling cutter teeth |
| Hand pushing the cutter | Table feed mechanism |
| Each tiny scoop removed | Individual chip produced per tooth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway for KTU exams:** Milling is the **only standard machining process** where the tool has *multiple cutting edges (teeth)* engaged in *interrupted cutting* — each tooth cuts only for a fraction of every revolution. This is what makes milling physics (cutting force, MRR) fundamentally different from turning.

## 1.3 Why Milling is a Workhorse of Manufacturing

Milling accounts for an estimated **60–70% of all chip-making operations** in modern discrete-part manufacturing because it can:

1. Produce **flat surfaces, slots, pockets, gears, splines, and 3D contoured surfaces** on a single machine.
2. Achieve **high material removal rates (MRR)** with excellent surface finish.
3. Use **standardized modular cutters** (inserts) that can be indexed or replaced without re-sharpening.
4. Be performed on **CNC 3-axis to 5-axis machines** with high precision (**± 0.01 mm** typical).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Milling Machines

Milling machines are broadly classified based on **orientation of the spindle** and **design of the bed/column**.

### A. Column and Knee Type Milling Machines

The most common and versatile type. Named for its prominent **column** (housing the spindle) and adjustable **knee** (vertical slide carrying the table).

| Sub-type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain Horizontal Milling Machine** | Spindle horizontal, no vertical feed adjustment of spindle | Slab, gang, straddle milling |
| **Universal Milling Machine** | Table can swivel horizontally (±45°) | Helical milling, gear cutting |
| **Vertical Milling Machine (Knee Type)** | Spindle vertical, quill feed for depth | End milling, face milling, drilling |
| **Turret Mill** | Ram-mounted turret head (rotates spindle) | Tool-room work, small batch |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "classify milling machines," always begin with **Column-and-Knee** and **Bed-type** as the two principal categories, then list sub-types.

### B. Bed-Type Milling Machines (Production Milling Machines)

The **knee is replaced by a fixed bed** — the table moves only longitudinally and cross-wise. Much **rigider** than knee-type, suitable for heavy-duty production.

- **Plano-Miller / Reciprocating Bed-Type Miller:** Table reciprocates; cutter rotates — used for milling long flat surfaces.
- **Duplex Miller:** Has **two spindles** (one horizontal, one vertical) — opposite faces machined in one setup.
- **Triplex Miller:** Three spindles — large industrial planers.

### C. Special-Purpose Milling Machines

| Type | Function |
|---|---|
| **Rotary Table Milling Machine** | Continuous rotary index table for circular milling |
| **Tracer-Controlled Mill** | Hydraulic or electronic follower reproduces a template (used for turbine blades, die cavities) |
| **CNC Milling Machine** | 3 to 5 axes under computer numerical control (the modern industry standard) |
| **Vertical Machining Centre (VMC)** | CNC vertical mill with automatic tool changer (ATC) |
| **Horizontal Machining Centre (HMC)** | CNC horizontal mill with pallet changer |
| **5-Axis Machining Centre** | Adds two rotational axes (A and B/C) for impellers, aerospace parts |

## 2.2 Milling Operations — A Comprehensive Taxonomy

Milling operations are categorized by the **geometry produced** and the **relationship between cutter axis and feed direction**.

### 2.2.1 Based on Direction of Feed vs. Cutter Rotation

**1. Up Milling (Conventional Milling):**
- Feed direction is **opposite** to cutter rotation at the contact zone.
- Chip thickness starts at **maximum** at the end of engagement and decreases to zero at the start.
- Chip thickness: $t_c$ varies from $0$ to $t_{max}$ and back to $0$.

**2. Down Milling (Climb Milling):**
- Feed direction is **same** as cutter rotation at the contact zone.
- Chip thickness starts at **zero** and grows to maximum.
- Chip thickness: $t_c$ varies from $0$ to $t_{max}$ and back to $0$ (but reversed phase).

| Parameter | Up Milling | Down Milling |
|---|---|---|
| Cutter life | Higher | Lower (teeth strike at max thickness) |
| Surface finish | Better at low feed | Better at moderate feed |
| Holding of workpiece | Easier (tries to lift workpiece) | Difficult (tries to pull workpiece) |
| Machine backlash | Tolerable | Requires **zero backlash** |
| Power consumption | Slightly higher | Slightly lower |
| Initial chip thickness | $t_1 = 0$ | $t_1 = t_{max}$ |
| Final chip thickness | $t_2 = t_{max}$ | $t_2 = 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** Draw the chip-thickness diagram and label the entry/exit points. Conventional milling always has $t_1 = 0$ at the *start* of the engagement arc.

### 2.2.2 Based on Geometry Produced

| Operation | Description | Cutter Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain (Slab) Milling** | Flat surface parallel to cutter axis | Plain milling cutter (cylindrical) |
| **Face Milling** | Flat surface perpendicular to cutter axis | Face milling cutter |
| **End Milling** | Slots, pockets, profiling | End mill |
| **Side Milling** | Vertical surfaces on both sides of workpiece | Side milling cutter |
| **Straddle Milling** | Two parallel vertical surfaces simultaneously | Two side cutters (gang) |
| **Gang Milling** | Several cutters mounted on arbor for multiple operations | Multiple cutters |
| **Form Milling** | Irregular contour using a shaped cutter | Form cutter |
| **Profile Milling** | Open or closed contour | Profile cutter (end mill) |
| **Angular Milling** | Surface at an angle (e.g., dovetail) | Single-angle cutter |
| **Gear Milling** | Involute teeth on a gear blank | Gear cutter (form or hob) |
| **Helical Milling** | Helical groove or flute | Plain cutter + universal table |
| **Slot Milling** | Open or closed slots | Slitting saw or end mill |
| **Drilling, Boring, Reaming, Tapping** | Secondary operations on milling machine | Twist drill, boring bar, reamer, tap |

## 2.3 Types of Milling Cutters

Milling cutters are classified by **mounting, geometry, and purpose**.

### 2.3.1 By Mounting

- **Arbor-Mounted Cutters** — have a center hole, mounted on a horizontal arbor.
- **Shank-Mounted Cutters** — straight or tapered shank, used in vertical mills.
- **Insert-Type Cutters** — indexable carbide or HSS inserts (modern industrial standard).

### 2.3.2 By Geometry and Application

| Cutter | Function | Cutter Body |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain (Slab) Milling Cutter** | Horizontal flat surfaces | Cylindrical, helical teeth |
| **Side Milling Cutter** | Vertical surfaces, slots, gangs | Staggered teeth, peripheral + side |
| **Face Milling Cutter** | Large flat surfaces | Insert-type, large diameter |
| **End Mill** | Slots, pockets, profiling | Cylindrical shank, peripheral + end |
| **Slitting Saw** | Deep narrow cuts | Very thin, peripheral only |
| **Angle Milling Cutter** | Dovetails, V-grooves | Single or double angle |
| **Form Milling Cutter** | Special shapes (rounds, T-slots) | Profile-shaped teeth |
| **T-Slot Cutter** | T-slots in tables, fixtures | T-shaped cross-section |
| **Woodruff Cutter** | Semi-cylindrical keyway | Small, special form |
| **Fly Cutter** | Single-point, large diameter | Custom geometry, low MRR |
| **Hob** | Continuous gear generation | Involute, helical teeth |

> [!NOTE]
> **End mills are further sub-classified** as: (a) **Plain (square) end mill** — flat bottom, (b) **Ball end mill** — hemispherical tip for 3D contours, (c) **Bull-nose end mill** — corner radius, (d) **Roughing end mill** — serrated teeth for high MRR, (e) **Drill-mill / end mill with 45° chamfer** — for plunging and ramping.

## 2.4 Milling Cutter Nomenclature — The Geometry of a Single Tooth

The nomenclature of a milling cutter tooth is the **3D generalization of a single-point tool** with two distinct reference planes:

- **Axial Plane** — contains the cutter axis and the cutting edge.
- **Radial Plane** — perpendicular to the cutter axis and contains the radial direction.

### 2.4.1 Tool Angles in the Axial Plane

| Angle | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Axial Rake Angle** | $\gamma_a$ | Angle between the tooth face and a line parallel to the cutter axis, measured in the axial plane. Positive when face slopes away from the cutting edge in the direction of chip flow. |
| **Axial Clearance (Relief) Angle** | $\alpha_a$ | Angle between the tooth flank and a tangent to the cutting edge at the periphery, measured in the axial plane. Usually **6°–8°**. |
| **Lip Angle (Cutting Edge Angle)** | $\beta$ | Sum of rake + clearance angles: $\beta = 90° - \gamma_a - \alpha_a$ in standard geometry. |

### 2.4.2 Tool Angles in the Radial Plane

| Angle | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **Radial Rake Angle** | $\gamma_r$ | Angle between the tooth face and a radial line through the cutting edge, measured in the radial plane. |
| **Radial Clearance Angle** | $\alpha_r$ | Angle between the tooth flank and the tangent to the cutting edge in the radial plane. Usually **10°–15°**. |

### 2.4.3 Other Geometric Features

- **Cutting Edge (Lip):** The active edge that removes material.
- **Land / Margin:** Narrow flat strip adjacent to the cutting edge for stability.
- **Flute / Tooth Space:** Curved chip-pocket region between consecutive teeth.
- **Tooth Body:** Solid material of the tooth.
- **Tooth Face (Rake Face):** Surface over which the chip flows.
- **Tooth Flank (Clearance Face):** Surface that does NOT contact the workpiece.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always draw the **nomenclature diagram** showing both axial and radial planes, and clearly label $\gamma_a$, $\alpha_a$, $\gamma_r$, $\alpha_r$ on the same figure. Marks are awarded specifically for a clean, labeled diagram.

## 2.5 Process Parameters in Milling

The following parameters govern the milling process:

| Symbol | Parameter | Unit | Typical Range (HSS on mild steel) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $D$ | Cutter diameter | **mm** | 50–200 |
| $z$ | Number of teeth | — | 4–12 |
| $N$ | Spindle speed | **rev/min (rpm)** | 100–1500 |
| $v_c$ | Cutting speed | **m/min** | 20–40 (HSS), 150–300 (carbide) |
| $f_z$ | Feed per tooth (chip load) | **mm/tooth** | 0.05–0.4 |
| $f$ | Table feed (feed per minute) | **mm/min** | 50–1000 |
| $a_p$ | Axial depth of cut | **mm** | 0.5–6 |
| $a_e$ | Radial depth (width of cut) | **mm** | 5–100 |
| $t_{max}$ | Maximum chip thickness | **mm** | 0.05–0.4 |
| $h_m$ | Average chip thickness | **mm** | — |
| MRR | Material removal rate | **mm³/min** | 10³–10⁶ |
| $P$ | Power consumed | **kW** | 1–50 |
| $T$ | Machining time | **min or s** | — |

### 2.5.1 Kinematic Relationships

$$ v_c = \pi D N \quad [\text{m/min}] $$

$$ N = \frac{v_c \times 1000}{\pi D} \quad [\text{rev/min}] $$

$$ f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N \quad [\text{mm/min}] $$

$$ \text{MRR} = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot N \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}] $$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Consolidated)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all formulas** that a student must memorize for the KTU End-Semester Examination. Practice writing them in a single A4 sheet before the exam.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v_c = \pi D N$ | Cutting velocity | m/min |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{v_c \times 1000}{\pi D}$ | Spindle speed | rpm |
| 3 | $f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | Table feed | mm/min |
| 4 | $f_r = f_z \cdot z$ | Feed per revolution | mm/rev |
| 5 | $t_{max} = 2 f_z \sin\theta$ | Max chip thickness ($\theta$=entry/exit angle) | mm |
| 6 | $t_{max} = 2 f_z \sqrt{\dfrac{a_e}{D} - \left(\dfrac{a_e}{D}\right)^2}$ | Max chip thickness when $\theta \ne 90°$ | mm |
| 7 | $h_m = \dfrac{\sin\phi + \phi - \cos\phi}{\phi} \cdot f_z$ | Average chip thickness | mm |
| 8 | $h_m \approx \dfrac{t_{max}}{\pi} \approx \dfrac{f_z}{\pi}$ (for $\phi$≈180°) | Approx. avg chip thickness | mm |
| 9 | $\text{MRR} = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f$ | Material removal rate | mm³/min |
| 10 | $\text{MRR} = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | MRR in terms of cutter kinematics | mm³/min |
| 11 | $T_m = \dfrac{L + l_1 + l_2}{f}$ | Machining time | min |
| 12 | $F_c = K_c \cdot a_p \cdot h_m$ | Average cutting force | N |
| 13 | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot v_c}{60 \times 1000}$ | Cutting power | kW |
| 14 | $P_c = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \cdot K_c}{60 \times 10^6}$ | Power from MRR (K_c in N/mm²) | kW |
| 15 | $T = \dfrac{P_c \times 60 \times 1000}{2 \pi N}$ | Spindle torque | N·mm |
| 16 | $\phi = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{2 a_e}{D}\right)$ | Angle of engagement (immersion angle) | rad |
| 17 | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f_z \cdot z \cdot N} + \dfrac{l_1 + l_2}{f_{r}}$ | Machining time (with approach/overrun) | min |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation note:** $K_c$ is the **specific cutting force** (or specific energy coefficient) in $\text{N/mm}^2$. Typical values: mild steel ≈ 1500–2000 N/mm², aluminum ≈ 700 N/mm², cast iron ≈ 1100 N/mm². Use `\vert` or `\mid` in tables for **absolute value** (e.g., $\vert K_c \vert$).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MRR and Power** dictate **machine tool selection** and **process planning**.
- **Cutting forces** determine **spindle bearing life**, **workpiece deflection**, and **chatter stability**.
- **Cutter nomenclature** is the foundation of **CAM tool-path simulation** and **CNC tool libraries** (e.g., Sandvik Coromant, Kennametal catalogs).
- **Machining time** drives **cycle-time estimation** for **costing and quotation** in a job shop.
- **Chip thickness analysis** is the basis of **adaptive feed control** in modern CNC.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **rigorous derivations** of the most important milling formulas. Every algebraic step is written out explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum Chip Thickness $t_{max}$

### Setting up the geometry

Consider a plain milling cutter of diameter $D$ with $z$ teeth rotating at $N$ rpm. The workpiece is fed at $f$ mm/min. The feed per tooth is:

$$ f_z = \frac{f}{z \cdot N} \quad [\text{mm/tooth}] $$

When a tooth moves from position 1 to position 2 (one tooth-spacing $s$ along the cutter periphery), the table advances by $f_z$ mm. Geometrically, the chord $A_1A_2$ (which represents the distance between the two consecutive tooth positions on the cut surface) has length equal to $f_z$.

The maximum chip thickness is the **component of $f_z$ normal to the cutting edge direction**. If the cutter has **helical teeth** with helix angle $\omega$ (measured between tooth edge and cutter axis), or **straight teeth** with a radial contact angle $\theta$, then the normal chip thickness is:

$$ t_{max} = f_z \cdot \sin\theta $$

For **straight-tooth cutters** with the cutter axis horizontal and the workpiece fed vertically (i.e., for a horizontal plain cutter milling a vertical face), the cutter teeth enter and exit radially — the entry/exit angle is 90°, hence:

$$ t_{max} = f_z \cdot \sin 90° = f_z $$

This is the simplest case. For **face milling**, the entry angle $\theta$ is generally not 90°. The geometry of the engaged arc gives:

### Step-by-step derivation of $t_{max}$ for Face Milling

Let the cutter have diameter $D$ and the radial depth of cut (width of cut) be $a_e$. The tooth enters at point E and exits at point X. Define:
- $\phi$ = angle of engagement (immersion angle) of the cutter teeth, measured from the entry point to the exit point.
- $R = D/2$ = cutter radius.

From the geometry of the chord through the workpiece surface:

$$ \cos\phi = 1 - \frac{2 a_e}{D} $$

**Derivation:**
The entry point E and exit point X are at heights where the cutter surface is at $R$ (entry) and at $R - a_e$ (exit) above the centre line. The half-chord $CE = CX$ lies along the workpiece top. Hence:

$$ (R - a_e)^2 + CE^2 = R^2 $$

Solving for $CE$:

$$ CE^2 = R^2 - (R - a_e)^2 = R^2 - R^2 + 2Ra_e - a_e^2 = a_e(2R - a_e) $$

$$ CE = \sqrt{a_e(2R - a_e)} $$

The tooth has rotated through angle $\phi$ from entry to exit. The vertical drop of the cutter surface (relative to a fixed reference) across this arc is $a_e$. For small $a_e$ relative to $D$, $\phi$ is small, but in general:

$$ a_e = R - R\cos(\phi/2) \cdot 2 \quad \text{... (geometrical identity)} $$

which simplifies to the standard result:

$$ \phi = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{2 a_e}{D}\right) $$

The maximum chip thickness is achieved at the **exit** of the cut (for up-milling) and is:

$$ t_{max} = 2 f_z \sin\!\left(\frac{\phi}{2}\right) $$

Using the identity $\sin(\phi/2) = \sqrt{(1-\cos\phi)/2}$ and substituting $\cos\phi$ from above:

$$ t_{max} = 2 f_z \sqrt{\frac{1 - (1 - 2a_e/D)}{2}} = 2 f_z \sqrt{\frac{a_e}{D}} $$

**Refinement:** A more accurate formula accounting for non-central position of the workpiece relative to the cutter axis (eccentricity $e$):

$$ t_{max} = 2 f_z \sqrt{\frac{a_e}{D} - \left(\frac{a_e}{D}\right)^{\!2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos(\text{eccentricity angle})} $$

For the **standard centered case**:

$$ \boxed{\, t_{max} = 2 f_z \sqrt{\frac{a_e}{D} - \left(\frac{a_e}{D}\right)^{\!2}} \,} $$

> [!NOTE]
> When $a_e \ll D$ (light face-milling passes), the term $(a_e/D)^2$ becomes negligible, and the simpler approximation $t_{max} \approx 2 f_z \sqrt{a_e/D}$ is widely used in industry.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Average Chip Thickness $h_m$

For a milling cutter with $z$ teeth, each tooth is in contact for an angle $\phi$ of the cutter revolution. The total number of teeth engaged at any instant is:

$$ z_e = \frac{z \cdot \phi}{2\pi} $$

The instantaneous chip thickness at any angular position $\psi$ (measured from the start of engagement) is:

$$ t_c(\psi) = f_z \cdot \sin\psi $$

The **average chip thickness** $h_m$ is found by integrating $t_c(\psi)$ over the engaged arc and dividing by the arc length:

$$ h_m = \frac{\int_0^{\phi} t_c(\psi) \, d\psi}{\phi} = \frac{\int_0^{\phi} f_z \sin\psi \, d\psi}{\phi} $$

**Evaluating the integral:**

$$ \int_0^{\phi} f_z \sin\psi \, d\psi = f_z \left[-\cos\psi\right]_0^{\phi} = f_z (1 - \cos\phi) $$

Therefore:

$$ \boxed{\, h_m = \frac{f_z (1 - \cos\phi)}{\phi} \,} $$

**Special case — full immersion ($\phi = \pi$):**

$$ h_m = \frac{f_z (1 - \cos\pi)}{\pi} = \frac{f_z (1 - (-1))}{\pi} = \frac{2 f_z}{\pi} \approx 0.637 \, f_z $$

This is the **most commonly used** result and appears in nearly every KTU milling problem.

> [!NOTE]
> $\dfrac{2}{\pi} \approx 0.637$, so $h_m \approx 0.637 \, f_z$ for full-immersion peripheral milling (slab milling).

## 3.3 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR)

MRR is the **volume of material removed per unit time**. For a slab milling operation:

- Width of cut (radial depth) = $w$ (mm)
- Depth of cut (axial depth) = $d$ (mm)
- Table feed = $f$ (mm/min)

In one minute, the cutter advances $f$ mm over a surface of width $w$ and depth $d$. Hence:

$$ \text{MRR} = w \cdot d \cdot f \quad [\text{mm}^3/\text{min}] $$

In terms of cutter kinematics ($f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$):

$$ \text{MRR} = w \cdot d \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot N $$

For face milling, replace $w$ by $a_e$ and $d$ by $a_p$:

$$ \boxed{\, \text{MRR} = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f_z \cdot z \cdot N \,} $$

## 3.4 Derivation of Machining Time $T_m$

The total table travel distance $L_{\text{total}}$ has three parts:

1. **Length of cut** $L$ — over which the cutter is engaged with the workpiece.
2. **Approach distance** $l_1 = \sqrt{a_p (D - a_p)}$ — extra travel before engagement to bring the cutter up to full depth.
3. **Overrun distance** $l_2 \approx 1$ to 5 mm — extra travel after exit to clear the cut.

$$ L_{\text{total}} = L + l_1 + l_2 $$

Dividing by feed $f$:

$$ \boxed{\, T_m = \frac{L + l_1 + l_2}{f_z \cdot z \cdot N} \,} \quad [\text{min}] $$

In SI units (seconds):

$$ T_m [s] = \frac{60 (L + l_1 + l_2)}{f_z \cdot z \cdot N} $$

## 3.5 Derivation of Cutting Force and Power

The **tangential (cutting) force** on a single tooth at any instant is:

$$ F_{c,\text{single}}(\psi) = K_c \cdot a_p \cdot t_c(\psi) = K_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot \sin\psi $$

where $K_c$ is the **specific cutting force** (N/mm²), a material-property constant. The total cutting force at any instant summed over all $z_e$ engaged teeth is:

$$ F_c(\psi) = \sum_{i=1}^{z_e} K_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z \cdot \sin(\psi_i) $$

**Average cutting force** over one revolution:

$$ F_{c,\text{avg}} = K_c \cdot a_p \cdot h_m = K_c \cdot a_p \cdot \frac{f_z (1 - \cos\phi)}{\phi} $$

For full immersion ($\phi = \pi$):

$$ F_{c,\text{avg}} = \frac{2 K_c \cdot a_p \cdot f_z}{\pi} \quad [\text{N}] $$

**Cutting Power:**

$$ P_c = F_{c,\text{avg}} \cdot v_c \quad [\text{W}] $$

$$ P_c = \frac{F_{c,\text{avg}} \cdot v_c}{60\,000} \quad [\text{kW}] $$

Substituting $v_c = \pi D N / 1000$ m/min (with $D$ in mm):

$$ \boxed{\, P_c = \frac{\text{MRR} \cdot K_c}{60 \times 10^6} \,} \quad [\text{kW}] $$

**Spindle Torque:**

$$ T_{\text{spindle}} = \frac{P_c \times 60 \times 1000}{2 \pi N} \quad [\text{N·mm}] $$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> These are **KTU-style numerical problems**. Practice rewriting each step in your answer script.

### **Example 1 — Machining Time Calculation (Slab Milling)**

**Given:**
- Cutter diameter $D = 80$ mm, number of teeth $z = 8$
- Cutting speed $v_c = 30$ m/min
- Feed per tooth $f_z = 0.2$ mm/tooth
- Length of cut $L = 200$ mm, axial depth $a_p = 4$ mm
- Approach distance $l_1 = 12$ mm, overrun $l_2 = 3$ mm

**Find:** Spindle speed $N$, table feed $f$, MRR, and machining time $T_m$.

**Solution:**

$$ N = \frac{v_c \times 1000}{\pi D} = \frac{30 \times 1000}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{30\,000}{251.33} \approx 119.37 \, \text{rpm} $$

$$ f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.2 \times 8 \times 119.37 = 190.99 \, \text{mm/min} \approx 191 \, \text{mm/min} $$

For slab milling, $a_e = w = D$ (full immersion) and $a_p = 4$ mm:

$$ \text{MRR} = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot f = 4 \times 80 \times 191 = 61\,120 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{min} $$

$$ T_m = \frac{L + l_1 + l_2}{f} = \frac{200 + 12 + 3}{191} = \frac{215}{191} = 1.126 \, \text{min} $$

$$ T_m = 1.126 \times 60 = 67.5 \, \text{seconds} $$

### **Example 2 — Cutting Force and Power (Face Milling)**

**Given:**
- Cutter $D = 150$ mm, $z = 10$ insert teeth
- $N = 400$ rpm, $f_z = 0.15$ mm/tooth
- $a_p = 3$ mm, $a_e = 100$ mm
- Specific cutting force $K_c = 1800$ N/mm² (mild steel)

**Find:** MRR, average cutting force, cutting power, spindle torque.

**Solution:**

$$ v_c = \pi D N / 1000 = \pi \times 150 \times 400 / 1000 = 188.5 \, \text{m/min} $$

$$ f = 0.15 \times 10 \times 400 = 600 \, \text{mm/min} $$

$$ \text{MRR} = 3 \times 100 \times 600 = 180\,000 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{min} = 1.8 \times 10^5 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{min} $$

Angle of engagement:

$$ \phi = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{2 a_e}{D}\right) = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{200}{150}\right) = \cos^{-1}(-0.333) = 109.47° = 1.911 \, \text{rad} $$

Average chip thickness:

$$ h_m = \frac{f_z (1 - \cos\phi)}{\phi} = \frac{0.15 (1 - (-0.333))}{1.911} = \frac{0.15 \times 1.333}{1.911} = 0.1046 \, \text{mm} $$

Average cutting force:

$$ F_{c,\text{avg}} = K_c \cdot a_p \cdot h_m = 1800 \times 3 \times 0.1046 = 564.84 \, \text{N} $$

Cutting power:

$$ P_c = \frac{F_{c,\text{avg}} \cdot v_c}{60\,000} = \frac{564.84 \times 188.5}{60\,000} = 1.775 \, \text{kW} $$

Spindle torque:

$$ T_{\text{spindle}} = \frac{P_c \times 60\,000}{2 \pi N} = \frac{1.775 \times 60\,000}{2 \pi \times 400} = 42.4 \, \text{N·m} $$

> [!TIP]
> Always carry **at least 4 significant figures** in intermediate steps and round only at the final answer. KTU examiners penalize premature rounding.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a CNC Milling Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Operator HMI Panel] --> B1[CNC Controller CPU]
    A2[G-Code Program] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Servo Drive X-axis]
    B1 --> C2[Servo Drive Y-axis]
    B1 --> C3[Servo Drive Z-axis]
    C1 --> D1[Ball Screw X]
    C2 --> D2[Ball Screw Y]
    C3 --> D3[Ball Screw Z]
    D1 --> E1[Worktable Saddle]
    D2 --> E2[Cross Slide]
    D3 --> E3[Quill Spindle Head]
    E1 --> F1[Fixture and Workpiece]
    B1 --> G1[Spindle Drive VFD]
    G1 --> H1[Spindle Motor]
    H1 --> I1[Tool Holder ISO40 HSK63]
    I1 --> J1[Milling Cutter]
    J1 --> F1
    B1 --> K1[Coolant Pump Controller]
    K1 --> L1[CNC Coolant Nozzle]
    L1 --> J1
    F1 --> M1[Feedback Encoders]
    M1 --> B1
    E1 --> M1
    E2 --> M1
    E3 --> M1
```

**Interpretation of the flow diagram:**
- The **HMI panel** + **G-code program** are the human/machine inputs.
- The **CNC controller** is the brain — it dispatches commands to all axes and the spindle.
- The **closed-loop feedback** (encoders) provides position correction to maintain accuracy.
- The **coolant loop** is essential for thermal control and chip evacuation.

## 4.2 Schematic — Up Milling vs. Down Milling (Milling Cutter Nomenclature)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Conventional_Milling
        UC_Cutter[("Cutter Rotating CCW")] --> UC_Feed["Feed Direction -> Right to Left"]
        UC_Feed --> UC_Chip1["Chip starts at MAX thickness at EXIT"]
        UC_Feed --> UC_Chip2["Chip ends at ZERO thickness at ENTRY"]
    end
    subgraph Climb_Milling
        DC_Feed["Feed Direction -> Left to Right"] --> DC_Cutter[("Cutter Rotating CW")]
        DC_Cutter --> DC_Chip1["Chip starts at ZERO thickness at ENTRY"]
        DC_Cutter --> DC_Chip2["Chip ends at MAX thickness at EXIT"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Tooth Engagement and Chip Thickness Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Cutter Center O] --> B[Entry Point E]
    A --> C[Exit Point X]
    A --> D[Workpiece Top Reference]
    B --> E[Chord length = tooth advance fz]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Maximum chip thickness tmax occurs at X for up-milling]
    E --> G[Engagement angle phi measured at O]
    G --> H[tmax = 2 fz sin phi/2]
    H --> I[Average chip thickness hm = fz 1-cos phi over phi]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Cutting Force Decomposition in Milling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Resultant Cutting Force F at Tooth] --> B[Tangential Component Ft = Fc]
    A --> C[Radial Component Fr = 0.3 to 0.6 Fc]
    A --> D[Axial Component Fa = 0.1 to 0.3 Fc]
    B --> E[Consumes Power]
    B --> F[Generates MRR]
    C --> G[Tends to Deflect Workpiece]
    C --> H[Causes Spindle Bearing Load]
    D --> I[Acts Along Cutter Axis]
    D --> J[Pushes Cutter into Spindle]
```

**Engineering notes for the diagram:**
- $F_c$ is the **only** component that performs useful work (cutting).
- $F_r$ and $F_a$ are **parasitic** and load the spindle/structure.
- In **up milling**, the radial force $F_r$ pushes the workpiece **upward** — easier to clamp.
- In **down milling**, $F_r$ pulls the workpiece **downward** — risk of work-piece pull-out if clamping is weak.
