## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*What is a hob? List any four advantages of gear hobbing over gear shaping.*

**Model Answer:**
A **hob** is a multi-point cutting tool used in the gear hobbing process, shaped like a worm (helical cylinder) with longitudinal gashes machined along its body to form cutting edges. The hob and the gear blank rotate in a continuously synchronized generating relationship, with the hob also given an axial feed. The cumulative envelope of the cutting edges forms the involute tooth profile on the blank.

**Four advantages of gear hobbing over gear shaping:**
1. **Higher productivity** — continuous cutting (no indexing) gives 5 to 10 times higher output.
2. **Better surface finish** — multi-tooth simultaneous cutting yields Ra 0.8 μm or better.
3. **Higher accuracy** — IT 6 to IT 8 achievable vs IT 7 to IT 9 in shaping.
4. **Longer tool life** — hob can be re-sharpened many times by regrinding the gash faces.
5. (Bonus) Lower operator skill required due to automated change-gear ratio.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*State the generating ratio of a gear hobbing process and explain its significance.*

**Model Answer:**
The **generating ratio** in gear hobbing is:
$$\frac{N_w}{N_c} = \frac{T_{hob}}{Z_w}$$

where $N_w$ = workpiece (gear blank) rpm, $N_c$ = hob rpm, $T_{hob}$ = number of starts (threads) on the hob, $Z_w$ = number of teeth on the gear being cut.

**Significance:**
- It defines the **mathematical lock** between hob and workpiece rotations, ensuring pure conjugate rolling without slip.
- It is enforced by the **change-gear train** of the hobbing machine.
- The ratio is **independent of cutting speed or feed** — it is a geometric constraint dictated by the worm-and-wheel meshing law.
- If the ratio deviates, the generated involute profile gets distorted, causing profile error and noise in service.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both choices are given below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **principle of gear hobbing**. Discuss the role of the **change-gear train** in maintaining the generating motion.

**Model Solution:**

**Principle of Gear Hobbing:**
Gear hobbing is a **continuous generating process** for producing involute gear teeth. The tool (hob) is a worm-shaped cutter with cutting edges formed by longitudinal gashes. The hob rotates continuously (cutting motion) while the gear blank rotates in a strictly synchronized relationship (generating motion) such that:

$$N_w = N_c \times \frac{T_{hob}}{Z_w}$$

This mimics the rolling of a worm against a worm-wheel. Simultaneously, the hob advances axially along the gear blank at a feed rate $f_a$ mm per hob revolution. As the hob teeth traverse across the gear blank, each cutting edge removes a small chip. The **envelope of all successive cutting-edge positions** forms the involute tooth profile.

**Schematic Layout (described in text):**
The setup shows a horizontal hob mounted on a vertical spindle (or vice versa, depending on machine orientation) meshing with a gear blank mounted on a horizontal work spindle. The two spindles are kinematically linked by a change-gear train. A lead screw drives the hob head along the gear axis for the axial feed. A coolant nozzle directs cutting fluid at the cutting zone.

**Role of Change-Gear Train:**
- The change-gear train **mechanically enforces** the generating ratio $N_w / N_c = T_{hob} / Z_w$.
- The gear ratio of the change-gear set is calculated as:
$$\text{Index ratio} = \frac{\text{Driven gears product}}{\text{Driver gears product}} = \frac{T_{hob}}{Z_w} \times \frac{\text{index constant of machine}}{1}$$
- This ensures **strict synchronization** even as $N_c$ varies during operation.
- Different gear combinations allow the same machine to cut gears of any tooth count without changing the hob.
- The change-gear accuracy directly determines the **profile accuracy** of the finished gear.

**Mark Allocation (typical):** 3 marks principle + 2 marks sketch + 2 marks change-gear explanation.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A **2 mm module, 25-tooth spur gear** is to be cut on a hobbing machine. The hob is a **single-thread hob** with an outside diameter of 63 mm. The recommended cutting speed is **25 m/min**, and the axial feed is **1.0 mm/rev**. The face width of the gear is **20 mm**. Overtravel on each side is **5 mm**. Calculate:
- (i) Hob spindle speed $N_c$
- (ii) Workpiece spindle speed $N_w$
- (iii) Machining time per gear (neglect return time)

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Module $m = 2$ mm
- Gear teeth $Z_w = 25$
- Hob outside diameter $D_c = 63$ mm
- Cutting speed $V_c = 25$ m/min
- Axial feed $f_a = 1.0$ mm/rev
- Face width $b = 20$ mm
- Overtravel per side $L_o = 5$ mm
- Single-thread hob: $T_{hob} = 1$

**Part (i): Hob Spindle Speed $N_c$**
$$N_c = \frac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D_c} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 63}$$

$$N_c = \frac{25000}{197.92} = 126.32 \text{ rpm} \approx 127 \text{ rpm}$$

**Part (ii): Workpiece Spindle Speed $N_w$**
$$N_w = N_c \times \frac{T_{hob}}{Z_w} = 127 \times \frac{1}{25}$$

$$N_w = 5.08 \text{ rpm}$$

**Part (iii): Machining Time $t_m$**

Total traverse length:
$$L_{total} = b + 2 L_o = 20 + 2 \times 5 = 30 \text{ mm}$$

Feed velocity:
$$V_f = f_a \times N_c = 1.0 \times 127 = 127 \text{ mm/min}$$

Machining time (cutting stroke only):
$$t_m = \frac{L_{total}}{V_f} = \frac{30}{127} = 0.2362 \text{ min} \approx 14.17 \text{ seconds}$$

**Final Answers:** $N_c \approx 127$ rpm, $N_w \approx 5.08$ rpm, $t_m \approx 14.17$ s

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper, Alternative Set]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the construction of a **gear hob** with a neat sketch. Discuss the function of **gashes**, **lead angle**, and **number of threads**.

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of a Gear Hob:**
A gear hob is constructed from a cylindrical blank of **High-Speed Steel (HSS)** (or HSS-Co for hard materials). The manufacturing sequence is:
1. **Cylindrical blank** is rough-machined to give the basic shape.
2. **Helical flutes** (worm-thread grooves) are cut by a thread-milling or thread-grinding operation, forming the worm profile at the prescribed **lead angle $\lambda$** and **pressure angle $\phi$** (typically 20°).
3. **Longitudinal gashes** (straight flutes) are milled across the worm threads. These gashes intersect the worm threads, and their flanks form the **rake faces** of the cutting teeth.
4. The gash flanks are **sharpened** (honing) to form sharp cutting edges at their intersection with the worm-thread profile.
5. (For some hobs) The teeth may be **chamfered** or **tip-relieved** to provide relief angles and prevent fouling.
6. (For carbide hobs) The HSS blank is replaced by a steel body with brazed carbide inserts, or a solid carbide blank is ground.

**Function of Gashes:**
- Gashes are **straight, longitudinal slots** machined into the hob body.
- They **form the cutting edges** by intersecting the helical worm profile.
- They provide **rake angle** (clearance) to the cutting teeth.
- They create **chip space (flutes)** to allow chips to escape.
- They are relieved (gashed) at an **axial rake angle** (usually 0° to 12° positive) to assist chip flow.
- Typical number of gashes = **8 to 16** for a single-thread hob; this determines the number of cutting points per revolution of the hob.

**Function of Lead Angle $\lambda$:**
- $\lambda$ is the helix angle of the worm threads on the hob.
- It is determined by the relationship:
$$\lambda = \arctan\!\left(\frac{T_{hob} \times \pi \times m}{\pi \times D}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{T_{hob} \times m}{D}\right)$$
- For a single-thread hob cutting a standard spur gear, $\lambda$ is small (typically 1° to 5°).
- It determines the **rake geometry** in the cutting direction.
- For **helical gear hobbing**, the hob is tilted by $(\lambda \pm \beta_g)$ to match the gear's helix angle.
- It governs the **profile of the generated involute**: a zero lead angle would produce a rack-like (straight) tooth, not a curved involute.

**Function of Number of Threads (Starts) $T_{hob}$:**
- A single-thread hob ($T_{hob} = 1$) has one continuous helical thread; it is the **standard for accuracy** and produces one cut per revolution per flute.
- A multi-thread hob ($T_{hob} = 2, 3, 4$) has parallel threads; it gives **higher productivity** because each hob revolution removes more material (the generating ratio is multiplied by $T_{hob}$).
- However, multi-thread hobs produce **slightly coarser profile** and require the machine's **indexing error** to be very small.
- The choice depends on the **quality class** required: precision gears → single thread; roughing or large modules → multi-thread.

**Mark Allocation (typical):** 3 marks construction + 2 marks gashes + 1 mark lead angle + 1 mark threads.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **gear hobbing** and **gear shaping** processes. List the **advantages** and **limitations** of gear hobbing.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Gear Hobbing | Gear Shaping |
|---|---|---|
| **Process Nature** | Continuous generating | Indexing generating |
| **Tool** | Hob (multi-point worm) | Pinion-shaped cutter (rack or pinion) |
| **Tooth Profile** | Auto-generated by envelope | Auto-generated by envelope |
| **Cutting Stroke** | Continuous (no return stroke) | Reciprocating (cut + return) |
| **Productivity** | **High** (5 to 10× shaping) | Medium |
| **Internal Gears** | **Not possible** (blank too small) | **Possible** |
| **Helical Gears** | Easy (tilt hob) | Easy (helical shaper) |
| **Tool Cost** | High | Medium |
| **Accuracy** | IT 6 to IT 8 | IT 7 to IT 9 |
| **Surface Finish** | Excellent | Good |
| **Setup Time** | Higher (change-gear, tilt) | Lower |

**Advantages of Gear Hobbing:**
1. **Highest productivity** among all gear-cutting methods due to continuous multi-tooth cutting.
2. **Excellent accuracy** — IT 6 to IT 8 can be achieved routinely.
3. **Superior surface finish** — Ra 0.8 μm typical.
4. **Versatile** — can cut spur, helical, worm-wheels, splines, racks (with rack-hobbing attachment).
5. **Long tool life** — hob can be re-sharpened multiple times (only the gash faces are reground).
6. **Self-generating** — the involute profile is generated by the rolling motion, so small hob errors are averaged out.

**Limitations of Gear Hobbing:**
1. **Cannot cut internal gears** — the blank cannot be smaller than the hob's outer diameter minus clearance.
2. **Cannot cut gears very close to a shoulder** — the hob needs overtravel on both sides.
3. **Hob cost is high** — and the hob must be stored carefully.
4. **Setup time is longer** — change-gear calculation, hob-tilt setting, etc.
5. **Limited to modules** typically below 30 mm (hob size grows prohibitively for larger modules).
6. **Cannot cut gears with interfering shoulders** or with very wide face widths without special fixtures.

**Mark Allocation (typical):** 3 marks comparison + 2 marks advantages + 2 marks limitations.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Gear Hobbing Problems (Read Carefully Before Exam):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the cutting speed formula's unit** — KTU standard is m/min, but the formula uses $D$ in mm. Don't forget the **÷ 1000** factor. Many students write $N_c = V_c / (\pi D_c)$ and lose 1 mark.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up $T_{hob}$ and $Z_{hob}$** — $T_{hob}$ is the number of *threads* (starts) on the hob (usually 1 for standard hobs). $Z_{hob}$ is the *equivalent* number of teeth of the worm (a fictitious value). Only $T_{hob}$ appears in the generating ratio. Using $Z_{hob}$ by mistake loses 2 marks.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the overtravel in machining time** — $L_{total} = b + 2 L_o$, not just $b$. Examiners specifically test this; missing $L_o$ costs 1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Sign convention for helical-gear hob tilt** — "+" for opposite-hand helices, "−" for same-hand. Drawing the wrong tilt direction (visually) loses 1 mark.
> 
> 5. **Writing $N_w = N_c / Z_w$ instead of $N_w = N_c \times T_{hob} / Z_w$** — a very common slip. Always write the **complete formula** with $T_{hob}$ included.
> 
> 6. **Not stating units in the final answer** — even if the numerical answer is correct, missing units (rpm, mm/min, min, mm) typically costs 0.5 to 1 mark per sub-question.
> 
> 7. **Confusing feed direction** — the axial feed is **per revolution of the hob** ($f_a$ in mm/rev of hob), not per revolution of the workpiece. Misinterpretation leads to wrong $V_f$ and wrong machining time.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Gear Hobbing = Continuous Generating Process** (must be stated in every definition question).
- **Hob = Worm with Gashes** — the foundational concept; memorize the anatomy.
- **Generating ratio (THE formula):** $N_w = N_c \times T_{hob} / Z_w$ — *single most important equation*.
- **Cutting speed formula:** $V_c = \pi D_c N_c / 1000$ (m/min with $D_c$ in mm).
- **Workpiece speed is always LESS than hob speed** (since $T_{hob} \ll Z_w$).
- **Axial feed $f_a$** is per revolution of hob, not workpiece.
- **Machining time:** $t_m = (b + 2L_o) / (f_a \times N_c)$ — remember the overtravel.
- **Hob tilt for helical gears:** $\alpha_{tilt} = \lambda_h \pm \beta_g$ — opposite-hand = "+", same-hand = "−".
- **Advantages:** High productivity, accuracy (IT 6–8), finish, versatility, long tool life.
- **Limitations:** No internal gears, no close-to-shoulder cuts, high hob cost, long setup.
- **Hob types to memorize:** Single-thread (standard), Multi-thread (productivity), Roughing, Finishing, Spline, Worm-wheel.
- **Standard values to remember:** Pressure angle = 20°; lead angle (single-thread) = 1°–5°; number of gashes = 8 to 16; rake = 0° radial (standard) or positive.
- **Hob cannot cut internal gears** — most-tested single line in Part A.
- **MMR / MRR is higher in hobbing** than shaping because cutting is continuous (no idle return stroke).
- **Change-gear train** maintains the generating ratio; change-gear inaccuracy → profile error.
- **CNC hobbing machines** use electronic gearing (no physical change-gears), but the mathematical ratio is identical.
- **Coolant** is essential in hobbing — typically flood coolant with extreme-pressure (EP) additives; dry hobbing is possible with coated carbide hobs at high speeds.
- **Module range:** Standard hobbing covers m = 0.2 to ~25 mm; large-module gears may need specialized machines.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Holding - Jigs and Fixtures - Functions and Comparison
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Work Holding - Jigs and Fixtures - Functions and Comparison

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Work Holding?

In any metal-cutting or forming operation, the workpiece must be held rigidly and located accurately with respect to the cutting tool. The device that performs this function is called a **Work Holding Device**. Among the various work holding devices, **Jigs and Fixtures** are the most important and widely used in mass production environments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> A **Jig** is a special work holding device that both **locates** and supports the workpiece AND **guides** the cutting tool during the operation.
> A **Fixture** is a work holding device that **locates**, supports, and holds the workpiece in a fixed position, but **does not guide** the cutting tool. The tool is guided by the machine tool itself.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are drilling a hole in a wooden plank to hang it on a wall:

- **Without a jig/fixture**: You hold the plank with one hand, mark the spot with a pencil, and try to drill straight. The hole may tilt, the plank may slip, and your hand might be injured.
- **With a Fixture**: You place the plank in a wooden frame (fixture) that holds it still. You can drill accurately because the frame keeps the plank from moving — but you still need to align the drill by eye or by a machine guide.
- **With a Jig**: You place the plank in a frame (jig) AND the frame has a metal ring (bushing) that **physically guides** the drill bit. The drill cannot tilt — it can only go straight down. This is the level of accuracy and safety required in factories.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** **J**ig = **J**igs **G**uide the tool (J → G). A **F**ixture just **F**astens the workpiece (F → F).

## 1.3 Why are Jigs and Fixtures Important?

| Production Parameter | Effect of Using Jigs/Fixtures |
|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | Improved dramatically (locational repeatability within **±0.025 mm**) |
| **Skill required** | Reduced — semi-skilled operators can produce accurate parts |
| **Production rate** | Increased (no time wasted on marking and setting) |
| **Cost per piece** | Decreased for large batches |
| **Interchangeability** | Achieved — all parts in a batch are identical |
| **Operator safety** | Improved (no hand near cutting zone) |

## 1.4 Operations Where Each is Used

- **Jigs are used for**: Drilling, Reaming, Tapping, Boring — i.e., operations where the tool position must be guided relative to the workpiece.
- **Fixtures are used for**: Milling, Turning, Grinding, Broaching, Welding, Inspection (Gauging), Assembly, Forging, Die-casting — i.e., operations where the tool path is controlled by the machine tool itself.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Essential Functions of Jigs and Fixtures

A jig or fixture must perform **six cardinal functions** simultaneously. If any one fails, the part is rejected:

1. **Locate** — Establish a repeatable, accurate reference position on the workpiece using the **3-2-1 locating principle**.
2. **Support** — Bear the weight of the workpiece and withstand cutting forces without deflection.
3. **Clamp** — Hold the workpiece against locating surfaces with sufficient force to prevent movement during cutting.
4. **Guide (Jigs only)** — Direct the cutting tool to the exact position via **drill bushings**.
5. **Set/Index** — Allow the workpiece to be repositioned accurately for multiple operations or multiple parts.
6. **Eject/Unload** — Permit easy removal of the finished component without damage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When a question asks "List the functions of jigs and fixtures," the most common 6-mark answer includes the above six points. Memorize the exact wording: *Locate – Support – Clamp – Guide – Set – Eject*.

## 2.2 The 3-2-1 Locating Principle (Degree of Freedom Analysis)

A free rigid body in space has **6 degrees of freedom (DOF)**: 3 translational (X, Y, Z) and 3 rotational (about X, Y, Z axes). To fully constrain the workpiece, **all 6 DOF must be eliminated**.

| Locating Pin Set | DOF Eliminated | Location Type | Position |
|---|---|---|---|
| **3 primary locators** (on Primary / X-Y plane) | 1 translation (Z) + 2 rotations (about X and Y) = **3 DOF** | Rest the part on a base | Bottom surface |
| **2 secondary locators** (on Secondary / Y-Z plane) | 1 translation (X) + 1 rotation (about Z) = **2 DOF** | Side reference | One side surface |
| **1 tertiary locator** (on Tertiary / X-Z plane) | 1 translation (Y) = **1 DOF** | End reference | Other side surface |
| **TOTAL** | **6 DOF** | Fully constrained | Three perpendicular planes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing **Clamping** with **Locating**. The clamps hold the part; the locators *position* the part. Clamps are placed OPPOSITE to locators. If a clamp is placed on a locating surface, accuracy is lost.

## 2.3 Principles of Clamping (Genuine KTU Board Points)

1. The clamping force must be directed **toward the locating surfaces**, never away.
2. The clamp should be **self-locking** (no spring-back) under cutting forces.
3. Clamping pressure should not **distort or bend** thin/thin-walled workpieces.
4. Clamping and releasing must be **quick** (within 2–3 seconds) — preferably one motion.
5. The clamping force magnitude should be **adequate but not excessive** (typically 1.5 to 3 times the cutting force).
6. The clamp should not obstruct the **cutting tool** or the **operator's view**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_c = \dfrac{1000 \cdot P}{V}$ | Cutting force from power | N (P in kW, V in m/min) | Drilling, Milling |
| 2 | $T = F_c \cdot \dfrac{d}{2}$ | Torque on drill/cutter | N·mm | Spindle design |
| 3 | $F_{clamp} = k \cdot F_c$ | Clamping force with safety factor k = 1.5–3 | N | All clamping |
| 4 | $P_b = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{pin}}$ | Bearing pressure on locating pin | N/mm² (allowable ≤ 10–20 N/mm² for hardened steel) | Pin design |
| 5 | $d_b = d_{drill} + \delta$ | Drill bushing diameter (clearance $\delta$ = 0.05–0.25 mm) | mm | Drill bushing selection |
| 6 | $D_{pitch} = n \cdot p$ | Pitch circle diameter of holes (n holes, pitch p) | mm | Indexing jigs |
| 7 | $N_{holes} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{\theta}$ | Number of indexing positions (θ = angular step) | dimensionless | Indexing jigs |
| 8 | $L_{locator} = (1.5 \text{ to } 2) \cdot d_{locator}$ | Length of cylindrical locator for stability | mm | Locator design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary conditions to remember:**
> - Bearing pressure on **hardened steel pin** ≈ **10–20 N/mm²**
> - Bearing pressure on **cast iron pin** ≈ **5–10 N/mm²**
> - Drill bushing clearance: **press fit (H7/g6)** for renewable bushings, **sliding fit (H7/f7)** for fixed bushings.

## 2.5 Comparison Table — Jigs vs Fixtures (Most Repeated KTU Question)

| S.No. | Parameter | **JIG** | **FIXTURE** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tool guidance | **Guides the cutting tool** via bushings | Does **not** guide; machine tool guides the tool |
| 2 | Operations | Drilling, Reaming, Tapping, Boring | Milling, Turning, Grinding, Welding, Broaching |
| 3 | Cost | Higher (bushings, tool guides) | Comparatively lower |
| 4 | Complexity | More complex | Simpler |
| 5 | Wear | Subject to wear on bushings | Less wear (no direct tool contact) |
| 6 | Production rate | Very high | High |
| 7 | Component | Drill bushings, template | Clamps, locators, body |
| 8 | Accuracy | Higher (tool is fully constrained) | Slightly lower (depends on machine) |
| 9 | Operator skill | Low | Low |
| 10 | Initial investment | Higher | Lower |
| 11 | Use | Mainly for hole-making | For all other operations |
| 12 | Standards | IS 5353, IS 5390 | IS 5173, IS 5354 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Hint:** A 14-mark question in KTU 2023 May Exam was: *"Compare jigs and fixtures in detail. List the essential elements of any jig/fixture design."* Always present the answer in **two-column comparison table format** for maximum marks.

## 2.6 Essential Elements of Jigs and Fixtures

1. **Body (Base)** — Cast iron / fabricated steel structure, the main skeleton.
2. **Locating Devices** — Pins, rests, V-blocks, mandrels, pins in bushings (locate the part).
3. **Clamping Devices** — Strap clamps, finger clamps, toggle clamps, cam clamps, screw clamps.
4. **Tool Guides / Bushings (Jigs only)** — Hardened steel drill bushings pressed into jig body.
5. **Indexing Devices** — Index plates, plungers (for multi-station work).
6. **Setting Blocks / Feet** — Rest the jig on machine table.
7. **Fasteners** — Bolts, dowel pins, studs for mounting on machine.

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> While the topic is largely descriptive, the following four standard analytical derivations are **frequently asked in KTU exams for 7-mark sub-parts**. Each derivation is shown in exhaustive step-by-step form.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Clamping Force Required for a Drilling Operation

**Problem Statement:** A drilling operation is performed on a workpiece clamped in a drill jig. Calculate the clamping force required to hold the workpiece against the cutting force, given the power consumed, spindle speed, drill diameter, and a safety factor.

### Given:
- Power $P = 5$ kW
- Spindle speed $N = 800$ rpm
- Drill diameter $d = 20$ mm
- Safety factor $k = 2.5$

### Step 1 — Compute Cutting Velocity

The cutting velocity (peripheral) for a drill is given by:

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{1000}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 20 \cdot 800}{1000}
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{50265.5}{1000}
$$

$$
V \approx 50.27 \text{ m/min}
$$

*Conversion logic:* $d$ in mm, $N$ in rpm. The product $\pi d N$ gives mm/min, so divide by **1000** to convert to m/min.

### Step 2 — Compute Tangential Cutting Force

From the standard relation $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60{,}000}$ (where P in kW, V in m/min, F_c in N):

$$
F_c = \dfrac{1000 \cdot P}{V}
$$

Substituting:

$$
F_c = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 5}{50.27}
$$

$$
F_c = \dfrac{5000}{50.27}
$$

$$
F_c \approx 99.46 \text{ N}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Torque (for context)

$$
T = F_c \cdot \dfrac{d}{2}
$$

$$
T = 99.46 \cdot \dfrac{20}{2} = 99.46 \cdot 10
$$

$$
T = 994.6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Thrust / Feed Force (Side Load on Jig)

For drilling, the thrust force is empirically related to the cutting force:

$$
F_t \approx 0.5 \cdot F_c
$$

$$
F_t \approx 0.5 \cdot 99.46
$$

$$
F_t \approx 49.73 \text{ N}
$$

This thrust force tries to push the workpiece off the locators — it must be resisted by clamping.

### Step 5 — Apply Safety Factor for Clamping Force

$$
F_{clamp} = k \cdot F_t
$$

$$
F_{clamp} = 2.5 \cdot 49.73
$$

$$
F_{clamp} \approx 124.3 \text{ N}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{F_{clamp} \approx 124.3 \text{ N}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget the **factor of safety** (k). Also, drilling produces a *thrust force along the tool axis*, not a tangential force. The clamp must counter this axial thrust — not the torque.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Size of a Locating Pin (Bearing Pressure Check)

**Problem:** A 25 mm diameter locating pin supports a clamping force of 1500 N. The pin is made of hardened steel (allowable bearing pressure = 15 N/mm²). Check if the pin is safe.

### Step 1 — Projected Bearing Area of the Pin

For a cylindrical pin resting against a flat, the projected area is the cross-section:

$$
A_{pin} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2
$$

$$
A_{pin} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (25)^2
$$

$$
A_{pin} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot 625
$$

$$
A_{pin} \approx 490.87 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Actual Bearing Pressure

$$
P_b = \dfrac{F_{clamp}}{A_{pin}}
$$

$$
P_b = \dfrac{1500}{490.87}
$$

$$
P_b \approx 3.056 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

### Step 3 — Compare with Allowable

$$
P_b = 3.056 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{vs} \quad P_{allow} = 15 \text{ N/mm}^2
$$

Since $P_b \ll P_{allow}$, the pin is **safe**, but the design is **over-sized** and material is wasted.

### Step 4 — Re-design for Economy

Setting $P_b = P_{allow} = 10$ N/mm² (recommended design value, mid-range):

$$
A_{required} = \dfrac{1500}{10} = 150 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
\dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 = 150
$$

$$
d^2 = \dfrac{150 \cdot 4}{\pi} = \dfrac{600}{\pi}
$$

$$
d^2 \approx 190.99
$$

$$
d \approx 13.82 \text{ mm}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{d_{recommended} \approx 14 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** Length of cylindrical locator = **1.5 to 2 times** its diameter. So $L = 21$ to $28$ mm for a 14 mm locator.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Indexing Calculation for a Multi-Hole Drilling Jig

**Problem:** A circular flange has **8 equally spaced holes** on a pitch circle. Determine the pitch and number of indexing positions if a different number of holes is required.

### Step 1 — Pitch Circle Diameter Formula

For $n$ holes equally spaced on a pitch circle of diameter $D$:

$$
p = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D}{n}
$$

If $D = 200$ mm and $n = 8$:

$$
p = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 200}{8} = \dfrac{628.32}{8} = 78.54 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Number of Indexing Positions

For an indexing jig rotating by equal angular step $\theta$:

$$
N = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{\theta}
$$

If $\theta = 45^{\circ}$:

$$
N = \dfrac{360}{45} = 8 \text{ positions}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{p = 78.54 \text{ mm}, \quad N = 8 \text{ positions}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Drill Bushing Selection (Clearance Calculation)

**Problem:** A 10 mm drill must pass through a drill bushing. Calculate the bushing hole diameter and the bushing length for **press-fit (renewable)** and **sliding-fit (fixed)** conditions.

### Step 1 — Sliding Fit Bushing (For high accuracy, replaceable)

$$
d_b = d_{drill} + \delta
$$

where $\delta = 0.05$ to $0.25$ mm depending on drill size. For $d = 10$ mm, take $\delta = 0.10$ mm:

$$
d_b = 10 + 0.10 = 10.10 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Press Fit Bushing (For permanent mounting)

Tolerance grade: **H7/g6** → interference = 0.005 to 0.015 mm:

$$
d_b = 10.005 \text{ to } 10.015 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Bushing Length

The standard bushing length is $L = (1.5 \text{ to } 2) \cdot d_{drill}$:

$$
L = 1.5 \cdot 10 = 15 \text{ mm (minimum)}
$$

$$
L = 2.0 \cdot 10 = 20 \text{ mm (maximum)}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{d_b = 10.10 \text{ mm (sliding fit)}, \quad L = 15 \text{–}20 \text{ mm}}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection Logic for Jig vs Fixture

| Operation | Tool type | Tool guidance needed? | **Use JIG or FIXTURE?** | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drilling 6 holes in a plate | Twist drill | **Yes** (holes must be perpendicular) | **JIG** | Bushing guides the drill to maintain hole position and perpendicularity |
| Milling a flat surface | End mill | No (machine slide guides) | **FIXTURE** | No tool guidance needed; fixture holds the part |
| Tapping M10 thread | Tap | Yes (must be square) | **JIG** | Tap bushing guides the tap and prevents breakage |
| Boring a cylinder | Boring bar | Yes | **JIG** | Boring tool must be guided for size and alignment |
| Turning OD of a shaft | Turning tool | No | **FIXTURE** (chuck/jaw) | Lathe carriage guides the tool |
| Surface grinding | Grinding wheel | No | **FIXTURE** | Magnetic chuck or mechanical fixture holds the part |
| TIG welding two plates | TIG torch | No (manual) | **FIXTURE** | Fixture holds the plates in position; welder guides torch |
| CMM inspection | Probe | No (CMM-guided) | **FIXTURE (gauge)** | Holds part for accurate measurement |
