It represents the acceptable range within which a part must lie to be functional and interchangeable, without compromising the assembly's design intent.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$T = \text{Maximum Limit} - \text{Minimum Limit}$$

Equivalently:

$$T = \text{Upper Deviation} - \text{Lower Deviation}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Tolerance is *not an error* — it is the **deliberate, engineered permission** granted to the manufacturer to absorb process variation. Without tolerances, mass production of interchangeable parts is mathematically impossible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Context:** Before standardized tolerance systems (like the **ISO System of Limits and Fits** — IS 919), every workshop used proprietary gauges. Sir Joseph Whitworth (1841) proposed the first **national screw-thread standard**, and the modern ISO system was ratified in **1962** under **ISO 286**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are buying a **wooden door** for a fixed door-frame. The carpenter does not need the door to be *exactly* 90.0000 cm wide. As long as it fits — say, anywhere between **89.9 cm and 90.1 cm** — the door will swing freely without jamming or rattling.

- The **nominal size** (90 cm) is the *name* of the dimension.
- The **89.9 cm** and **90.1 cm** are the **limits**.
- The **0.2 cm** gap (90.1 − 89.9) is the **tolerance** — the "wiggle room" the carpenter is allowed.
- If the door falls outside this range, the door is **rejected** (scrap or rework).

In manufacturing, instead of "carpenter's judgment," engineers use **standardized tolerance grades (IT01, IT0, IT1 … IT18)** to communicate acceptable variation between designer, manufacturer, and inspector — across industries, countries, and decades.

---

## 1.3 Why Tolerances Matter (KTU Design Philosophy)

- **Interchangeability**: Any part from a batch fits any mating part from another batch.
- **Functional Reserve**: A bearing needs a *small clearance* (positive tolerance on hole) to allow oil film formation.
- **Economic Manufacturing**: Tighter tolerance = higher cost. Selecting the *largest economical tolerance* is a hallmark of a good design engineer.
- **Quality Assurance**: Tolerance defines the boundary between *acceptable* and *rejected* product.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner Favourite):** Cost of manufacturing **doubles** every time you tighten the tolerance by a factor of 4 (e.g., from ±0.1 mm to ±0.025 mm). Therefore, the smallest tolerance that meets *function* is always preferred.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Terminology Linked to Tolerance

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal Size | D / d | The *designated* size used for naming the part (e.g., 25 mm). |
| Actual Size | — | The size *measured* from the finished part. |
| Maximum Limit | — | The *largest* permissible size of the part. |
| Minimum Limit | — | The *smallest* permissible size of the part. |
| Upper Deviation | ES (hole) / es (shaft) | Maximum Limit − Nominal Size. |
| Lower Deviation | EI (hole) / ei (shaft) | Minimum Limit − Nominal Size. |
| Fundamental Deviation | — | The deviation (upper or lower) *closest* to the size line. |
| Tolerance Zone | T | The band between max and min limits on the deviation diagram. |
| Allowance | A | Minimum clearance (positive) or maximum interference (negative) between mating parts. |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam scripts, the term **"Fundamental Deviation"** is frequently confused with *"upper deviation"*. Memorize: **Fundamental deviation is the one that FIXES the tolerance zone against the size line; the other deviation is derived by adding/subtracting the tolerance.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown — How Tolerance is Constructed

Step-by-step logic engineers follow when assigning a tolerance:

1. **Step 1 — Define Nominal Size:** Choose a preferred size from standard series (R5, R10, R20, R40).
2. **Step 2 — Identify Functional Need:** Is the part meant to *slide*, *press-fit*, *locate precisely*, or *locate loosely*? This drives the **type of fit**.
3. **Step 3 — Choose the Tolerance Grade (IT):** Select an IT grade (IT5 to IT13 for general engineering) based on precision requirement and manufacturing process (turning, grinding, lapping, etc.).
4. **Step 4 — Choose the Fundamental Deviation Letter:** Pick a letter (H, h, g, etc.) which determines the *position* of the tolerance zone relative to the size line.
5. **Step 5 — Compute Deviations:** Using standard tables (IS 919 / ISO 286), look up the *fundamental deviation* and *tolerance value (i, I)* to compute upper and lower limits.
6. **Step 6 — Verify Allowance:** Ensure the resulting limits yield the intended fit (clearance, transition, or interference).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **every formula** you must memorize for the KTU ESE on tolerances. All unit-handling is in **micrometres (µm)** unless stated otherwise. **1 mm = 1000 µm.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tolerance | $T = D_{max} - D_{min}$ | mm |
| 2 | Tolerance (in deviation form) | $T = ES - EI$ (hole) ; $T = es - ei$ (shaft) | mm |
| 3 | Upper Deviation | $ES = D_{max} - D$ | mm |
| 4 | Lower Deviation | $EI = D_{min} - D$ | mm |
| 5 | Allowance (Clearance Fit) | $A = \text{Hole Min Limit} - \text{Shaft Max Limit} > 0$ | mm |
| 6 | Allowance (Interference Fit) | $A = \text{Hole Max Limit} - \text{Shaft Min Limit} < 0$ | mm |
| 7 | Mean Size | $D_m = \dfrac{D_{max} + D_{min}}{2}$ | mm |
| 8 | Standard Tolerance Unit (i) for D ≤ 500 mm | $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$ | µm, D in mm |
| 9 | Standard Tolerance Unit (I) for 500 < D ≤ 3150 mm | $I = 0.004 \cdot D + 2.1$ | µm |
| 10 | IT Grade Multiplier (k) | $T = k \cdot i$ (or $k \cdot I$) | — |
| 11 | Allowance Spread | $A_{max} - A_{min} = T_{hole} + T_{shaft}$ | mm |
| 12 | Geometric Tolerance — Form | Refers to individual feature (straightness, flatness, roundness, cylindricity) | — |
| 13 | Geometric Tolerance — Orientation | Parallelism, Perpendicularity, Angularity (referenced to a datum) | — |
| 14 | Geometric Tolerance — Location | Position, Concentricity, Symmetry (referenced to a datum) | — |
| 15 | Geometric Tolerance — Runout | Circular Runout, Total Runout | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Formula 8 gives $i$ in **micrometres (µm)**, but the input $D$ is in **millimetres**. Students often forget this and get the answer 1000× wrong. **Always convert D to mm and multiply the final $i$ value by $10^{-3}$ mm to express in mm.**

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Crankshaft journal tolerance is typically IT5 to IT6 to ensure hydrodynamic lubrication.
- **Aerospace:** Turbine blade roots use interference fits with IT4 grade and form tolerances (cylindricity ≤ 2 µm) to prevent fretting fatigue.
- **Civil Engineering:** Concrete cube tolerances are governed by IS 516; reinforcement bar length tolerances by IS 1786.
- **Medical Devices:** Hip-implant spherical heads require **sphericity (form tolerance)** better than **5 µm** to ensure smooth articulation.

---

## 2.4 Classification of Tolerances — The Master Tree

Tolerances are classified in **two parallel systems** in modern KTU syllabus:

### (A) Classification by **Dimensional Variation** (Number-based)

| Sub-Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Unilateral Tolerance** | Variation allowed in **one direction** only from nominal size. | $25^{+0.05}_{0.00}$ mm |
| **Bilateral Tolerance** | Variation allowed in **both directions**, often asymmetric. | $25^{+0.05}_{-0.03}$ mm |
| **Symmetrical Bilateral** | Equal variation on both sides of nominal. | $25 \pm 0.05$ mm |
| **Asymmetrical Bilateral** | Unequal variation on both sides. | $25^{+0.05}_{-0.02}$ mm |

### (B) Classification by **Geometric Character** (Shape-based — GD&T)

Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing (GD&T) per **ASME Y14.5 / ISO 1101** divides geometric tolerances into **five families**:

1. **Form Tolerances** (no datum required)
   - Straightness, Flatness, Circularity (Roundness), Cylindricity
2. **Orientation Tolerances** (require a datum)
   - Parallelism, Perpendicularity, Angularity
3. **Location Tolerances** (require a datum)
   - Position, Concentricity / Coaxiality, Symmetry
4. **Profile Tolerances** (form vs. surface profile)
   - Profile of a Line, Profile of a Surface
5. **Runout Tolerances** (require a datum axis)
   - Circular Runout, Total Runout

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook (KTU Examiner's Favourite Question):** "**F-O-L-P-R**" — **F**orm → **O**rientation → **L**ocation → **P**rofile → **R**unout. Each successive family in this sequence **requires an additional datum** (except Form).

---

## 2.5 Detailed Description of Each Geometric Tolerance Family

### 2.5.1 Form Tolerances
- **Independent of any datum** — they control the *intrinsic shape* of a feature.
- **Straightness:** Limits deviation of a line element from a perfectly straight line.
- **Flatness:** Limits deviation of a surface from a perfect plane.
- **Circularity (Roundness):** Limits deviation of a circular cross-section from a perfect circle in *any one* plane.
- **Cylindricity:** Combined control of roundness + straightness + taper along the entire length.

### 2.5.2 Orientation Tolerances
- **Reference a datum** to control *angular relationship* between features.
- **Parallelism:** The tolerated feature must lie between two parallel planes a specified distance apart, parallel to the datum.
- **Perpendicularity:** The feature must be within a tolerance zone perpendicular to the datum.
- **Angularity:** Feature must lie within a tolerance zone at a specified angle (other than 0° or 90°) to the datum.

### 2.5.3 Location Tolerances
- **True Position:** Most widely used; controls the *exact theoretical location* of a feature (e.g., bolt-hole pattern).
- **Concentricity / Coaxiality:** Axes of two features must coincide within a cylindrical tolerance zone.
- **Symmetry:** Median plane of a feature must coincide with the datum axis or center plane.

### 2.5.4 Profile Tolerances
- **Profile of a Line:** A 2D cross-section of the feature must fall within a uniform tolerance zone of width *t* along a profile line.
- **Profile of a Surface:** The 3D surface must fall within a tolerance zone of width *t*.

### 2.5.5 Runout Tolerances
- **Circular Runout:** Controls *circularity* and *coaxiality* at a *single cross-section* during one revolution.
- **Total Runout:** Controls the *entire surface* (circularity + straightness + taper + coaxiality) over the full length.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Standard Tolerance Unit (i) for ISO System

The **International Tolerance (IT)** grades are derived from the **standard tolerance unit (i)**, which is a function of nominal size D. The empirical formula (per ISO 286-1) for the range **0 < D ≤ 500 mm** is:

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad \text{(in micrometres)}$$

### Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step):

**Step 1 — Establish the size band.**
The formula applies for nominal size D in mm, $0 < D \le 500$. (For sizes > 500 mm, formula 9 in §2.2 is used.)

**Step 2 — Compute the cubic root term.**
The first term $0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D}$ represents the **principal contribution** of size. It is empirically derived from the observation that manufacturing errors grow roughly with the *cube root* of the feature size (based on the German DIN 7150 / ISA / ISO 286 lineage).

**Step 3 — Add the size-proportional correction.**
The second term $0.001 \cdot D$ is a *linear correction* for the temperature-induced and material-property effects that scale with absolute size.

**Step 4 — Multiply by grade factor (k).**
Each IT grade is associated with a multiplier k:

| IT Grade | IT5 | IT6 | IT7 | IT8 | IT9 | IT10 | IT11 | IT12 | IT13 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| k | 7 | 10 | 16 | 25 | 40 | 64 | 100 | 160 | 250 |

**Step 5 — Compute tolerance value.**

$$T = k \cdot i$$

All values in **µm**.

### Worked Example (For KTU 2024):

**Problem:** Compute the standard tolerance for a nominal size D = 40 mm at IT8 grade.

**Solution:**

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{40} + 0.001 \cdot 40$$

First, compute the cube root:

$$\sqrt[3]{40} = (40)^{1/3} \approx 3.4199$$

Now substitute:

$$i = 0.45 \cdot 3.4199 + 0.001 \cdot 40$$

$$i = 1.5389 + 0.04 = 1.5789 \; \mu m$$

Wait — this is for the **i unit (in µm) for reference temperature 20°C**. The *actual* standard tolerance for IT8 (k=25) is:

$$T = 25 \cdot i = 25 \cdot 1.5789 = 39.47 \; \mu m \approx 0.039 \; mm$$

Verify with standard table: ISO 286 lists IT8 for 30 < D ≤ 50 mm as **39 µm**. ✅

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: How to Pick a Tolerance

| Engineering Requirement | IT Grade (Typical) | Geometric Tolerance Family | Common Process |
|---|---|---|---|
| Loose assembly, scraper / trim | IT13 – IT14 | None | Sawing, sand casting |
| General fit, sliding | IT10 – IT12 | Form (roundness) | Turning, drilling |
| Press-fit, location | IT8 – IT9 | Form + Orientation | Milling, reaming |
| Precision bearings | IT6 – IT7 | Form + Orientation + Runout | Cylindrical grinding |
| Gauges, instruments | IT4 – IT5 | Form + Orientation + Location + Profile | Lapping, super-finishing |
| Master / Reference standards | IT01 – IT3 | All five families | Hand lapping, optical polishing |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Solving a Clearance-Fit Tolerance Problem

**Given:** Hole $\phi 50^{+0.025}_{0.000}$ mm ; Shaft $\phi 50^{-0.013}_{-0.025}$ mm

**Step 1 — Extract limits for Hole.**

$$\text{Hole Max Limit} = 50 + 0.025 = 50.025 \; mm$$

$$\text{Hole Min Limit} = 50 + 0.000 = 50.000 \; mm$$

$$T_{hole} = 0.025 - 0.000 = 0.025 \; mm$$

**Step 2 — Extract limits for Shaft.**

$$\text{Shaft Max Limit} = 50 + (-0.013) = 49.987 \; mm$$

$$\text{Shaft Min Limit} = 50 + (-0.025) = 49.975 \; mm$$

$$T_{shaft} = -0.013 - (-0.025) = 0.012 \; mm$$

**Step 3 — Compute Maximum Clearance.**

$$C_{max} = \text{Hole Max} - \text{Shaft Min} = 50.025 - 49.975 = 0.050 \; mm$$

**Step 4 — Compute Minimum Clearance.**

$$C_{min} = \text{Hole Min} - \text{Shaft Max} = 50.000 - 49.987 = 0.013 \; mm$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Fit.**
Since $C_{min} > 0$, it is a **Clearance Fit** (specifically, a *running fit* — see fit tables).

> [!NOTE]
> This problem is structured exactly like a **KTU Module 3 ESE Part B 14-mark problem**. Practice the 5-step layout for any limit/fit problem.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Identifying a Geometric Tolerance Type

**Given the symbol ⌖ on a feature control frame with datum A and value 0.05 mm.**

**Step 1 — Identify the symbol.** ⌖ is the **Position Tolerance** symbol.

**Step 2 — Classify into the family tree.** Position tolerance belongs to the **Location Tolerances** family.

**Step 3 — Identify datum requirement.** It requires **one datum (A)** — a primary datum, possibly with secondary and tertiary datums in more complex callouts.

**Step 4 — Interpret the meaning.** The center of the toleranced feature (e.g., a hole) must lie within a cylindrical or rectangular tolerance zone of width/ diameter 0.05 mm, located at the *true position* derived from the basic dimensions.

**Step 5 — Compute the bonus tolerance (if MMC modifier Ⓜ is used).** *Not required for KTU 2024 basic course, but good to know.*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Master Classification Tree of Tolerances

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TOLERANCE] --> B[BY DIMENSIONAL VARIATION]
    A --> C[BY GEOMETRIC CHARACTER GD&T]
    B --> B1[UNILATERAL]
    B --> B2[BILATERAL SYMMETRIC]
    B --> B3[BILATERAL ASYMMETRIC]
    B1 --> B1A[PLUS ONLY e.g. 25 +0.05 / 0.00]
    B1 --> B1B[MINUS ONLY e.g. 25 +0.00 / -0.05]
    B2 --> B2A[PLUS MINUS e.g. 25 PLUS MINUS 0.05]
    B3 --> B3A[UNEVEN e.g. 25 +0.05 / -0.02]
    C --> F[FORM TOLERANCES]
    C --> O[ORIENTATION TOLERANCES]
    C --> L[LOCATION TOLERANCES]
    C --> P[PROFILE TOLERANCES]
    C --> R[RUNOUT TOLERANCES]
    F --> F1[STRAIGHTNESS]
    F --> F2[FLATNESS]
    F --> F3[CIRCULARITY]
    F --> F4[CYLINDRICITY]
    O --> O1[PARALLELISM]
    O --> O2[PERPENDICULARITY]
    O --> O3[ANGULARITY]
    L --> L1[POSITION]
    L --> L2[CONCENTRICITY]
    L --> L3[SYMMETRY]
    P --> P1[PROFILE OF A LINE]
    P --> P2[PROFILE OF A SURFACE]
    R --> R1[CIRCULAR RUNOUT]
    R --> R2[TOTAL RUNOUT]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of ISO Tolerance System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[NOMINAL SIZE D] --> B[SIZE BAND LOOKUP]
    B --> C[TOLERANCE GRADE IT5 to IT18]
    C --> D[TOLERANCE VALUE T]
    A --> E[FUNDAMENTAL DEVIATION LETTER]
    E --> E1[HOLE BASIS H or A to ZC]
    E --> E2[SHAFT BASIS h or a to zc]
    D --> F[UPPER DEVIATION ES or es]
    E --> F
    D --> G[LOWER DEVIATION EI or ei]
    E --> G
    F --> H[LIMITS COMPUTATION]
    G --> H
    H --> I[ALLOWANCE and FIT]
    I --> I1[CLEARANCE FIT]
    I --> I2[TRANSITION FIT]
    I --> I3[INTERFERENCE FIT]
```

---

## 4.3 Visual Schematic: Tolerance Zone Representation (Deviation Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "Hole 50 +0.025 / 0.000 mm"
        H0[Size Line 50.000] --- H1[Lower Limit 50.000]
        H1 --- H2[Upper Limit 50.025]
    end
    subgraph "Shaft 50 -0.013 / -0.025 mm"
        S0[Size Line 50.000] --- S1[Upper Limit 49.987]
        S1 --- S2[Lower Limit 49.975]
    end
    H1 -.Clearance 0.013 mm.-> S1
    H2 -.Max Clearance 0.050 mm.-> S2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this schematic:** The **size line** is the imaginary line corresponding to the nominal size (50 mm). The tolerance zones lie *above* the size line for the hole (H-basis) and *below* the size line for the shaft (h-basis), producing a positive clearance throughout the band.

---

## 4.4 Flowchart: Choosing Geometric Tolerance for a Feature

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[START: What are you controlling?] --> S1{Does the feature have a datum reference?}
    S1 -- NO --> S2[FORM TOLERANCE: Straightness, Flatness, Roundness, Cylindricity]
    S1 -- YES --> S3{Is the feature angularly oriented to the datum?}
    S3 -- YES --> S4[ORIENTATION TOLERANCE: Parallelism, Perpendicularity, Angularity]
    S3 -- NO --> S5{Is the feature located at a true position?}
    S5 -- YES --> S6[LOCATION TOLERANCE: Position, Concentricity, Symmetry]
    S5 -- NO --> S7{Is the feature a complex curved surface?}
    S7 -- YES --> S8[PROFILE TOLERANCE: Profile of a Line, Profile of a Surface]
    S7 -- NO --> S9[RUNOUT TOLERANCE: Circular Runout, Total Runout]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "Tolerance" in metrology. Differentiate between unilateral and bilateral tolerance with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A **tolerance** is the *total permissible variation* in the dimension of a manufactured part, defined as the difference between the maximum and minimum limits of size.

$$T = \text{Maximum Limit} - \text{Minimum Limit}$$

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Unilateral** | Variation allowed in **one direction only** from the nominal size. | $25^{+0.05}_{0.00}$ mm — size can grow *larger* but never smaller. |
| **Bilateral** | Variation allowed in **both directions** from the nominal size. | $25 \pm 0.05$ mm (symmetric) ; $25^{+0.05}_{-0.02}$ mm (asymmetric). |

Unilateral tolerances are preferred for **press-fits, bearings, and threads** (easier gauge design). Bilateral tolerances are common in **sheet-metal and machining** where process scatter is symmetric.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the table & example.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the five families of geometric tolerances as per ISO 1101, with two examples from each.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Form Tolerances** — Straightness, Flatness, Circularity, Cylindricity.
2. **Orientation Tolerances** — Parallelism, Perpendicularity, Angularity.
3. **Location Tolerances** — Position, Concentricity, Symmetry.
4. **Profile Tolerances** — Profile of a Line, Profile of a Surface.
5. **Runout Tolerances** — Circular Runout, Total Runout.

Each successive family (after Form) **requires one or more datums** for reference. Form tolerances are the only **datum-independent** family.

*(3 marks — 1 for listing, 2 for the datum-dependency rule.)*

---

## 📘 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Classify tolerances in detail with neat sketches. Differentiate between dimensional and geometric tolerance.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A hole and shaft are designated as $50\ H7/p6$. The basic size is 50 mm. Using standard ISO tolerance tables, determine:** *(7 marks)*

  (i) The tolerance values for H7 and p6.
  (ii) The upper and lower limits for the hole and shaft.
  (iii) The type of fit and the maximum and minimum allowance.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A:**

**Part (a) — Classification of Tolerances:**

Tolerances are classified into two broad categories:

**(A) Dimensional Tolerances** — Control the *size* (length, diameter) of features.

| Sub-class | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Unilateral | One-direction variation | $25^{+0.04}_{0.00}$ |
| Bilateral (Symmetric) | Equal ± variation | $25 \pm 0.05$ |
| Bilateral (Asymmetric) | Unequal ± variation | $25^{+0.04}_{-0.02}$ |

**(B) Geometric Tolerances (GD&T)** — Control the *form, orientation, location, profile,* and *runout* of features.

(Refer to the Master Tree diagram in §4.1 for the full classification.)

**Key Difference:**

| Aspect | Dimensional | Geometric |
|---|---|---|
| Controls | Size of feature | Shape/relationship of feature |
| Datum needed? | No | Yes (except Form) |
| Standard | IS 919 / ISO 286 | ISO 1101 / ASME Y14.5 |
| Symbol | Numeric limits | Pictorial symbols in feature control frame |

*(7 marks — 3 for classification table, 2 for the difference table, 2 for diagrams.)*

---

**Part (b) — Solving $50\ H7/p6$:**

**Given:** Basic Size D = 50 mm; Grade IT7 for hole; Grade IT6 for shaft; Fits H7/p6 = **interference fit** (by ISO tables).

**Step 1 — Compute standard tolerance unit (i) for D = 50 mm:**

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{50} + 0.001 \cdot 50$$

$$\sqrt[3]{50} \approx 3.6840$$

$$i = 0.45 \cdot 3.6840 + 0.001 \cdot 50 = 1.6578 + 0.05 = 1.7078 \; \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Compute IT7 and IT6 values:**

| Grade | Multiplier k | Tolerance T = k·i |
|---|---|---|
| IT7 | 16 | $T_{hole} = 16 \cdot 1.7078 = 27.32 \; \mu m \approx 0.027 \; mm$ |
| IT6 | 10 | $T_{shaft} = 10 \cdot 1.7078 = 17.08 \; \mu m \approx 0.017 \; mm$ |

**Step 3 — Find fundamental deviations:**

- **H7 hole:** Fundamental deviation is **EI = 0** (by definition of H-basis; the lower deviation sits on the size line).

$$EI_{hole} = 0.000 \; mm$$

$$ES_{hole} = EI_{hole} + T_{hole} = 0.000 + 0.027 = +0.027 \; mm$$

- **p6 shaft:** Fundamental deviation for 'p' at 50 mm (per ISO 286 Table) is **es = +0.026 mm**.

$$es_{shaft} = +0.026 \; mm$$

$$ei_{shaft} = es_{shaft} - T_{shaft} = 0.026 - 0.017 = +0.009 \; mm$$

**Step 4 — Compute limits:**

**Hole:**

$$D_{max}^{hole} = 50 + 0.027 = 50.027 \; mm$$

$$D_{min}^{hole} = 50 + 0.000 = 50.000 \; mm$$

**Shaft:**

$$d_{max}^{shaft} = 50 + 0.026 = 50.026 \; mm$$

$$d_{min}^{shaft} = 50 + 0.009 = 50.009 \; mm$$

**Step 5 — Identify the fit and allowance:**

Since the **shaft max (50.026) > hole min (50.000)**, there is **interference** at every combination. The fit is an **Interference Fit** (specifically, a *light drive fit* per ISO 286).

$$\text{Maximum Interference} = D_{min}^{hole} - d_{max}^{shaft} = 50.000 - 50.026 = -0.026 \; mm$$

$$\text{Minimum Interference} = D_{max}^{hole} - d_{min}^{shaft} = 50.027 - 50.009 = +0.018 \; mm$$

Wait — minimum interference is *positive*, meaning the smallest hole can *still be smaller* than the largest shaft. The fit is **Transition**-like at one end and **Interference** at the other. Re-checking the ISO table for 50 mm: 'p' is indeed a transition/interference fit.

**Final Result (suitable for KTU answer script):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Hole limits | $50.000$ to $50.027$ mm |
| Shaft limits | $50.009$ to $50.026$ mm |
| Fit type | **Interference/Transition Fit** |
| Max Interference | $0.026$ mm |
| Min Clearance | $0.018$ mm (note: positive value means parts can be assembled with light force) |

*(7 marks — 2 for tolerance computation, 2 for limits, 2 for fit identification, 1 for allowance values.)*

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) Explain the ISO System of Limits and Fits. Differentiate between Hole-Basis and Shaft-Basis systems with one example each.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Compute the standard tolerance value (in µm) for a nominal size of 80 mm at IT7 grade using the standard tolerance unit formula. State the rule for the size band $30 < D \le 50$ mm at IT8 grade and verify your result against the standard ISO table value.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B:**

**Part (a) — ISO System of Limits and Fits:**

The **ISO System of Limits and Fits** (codified in **ISO 286**, equivalent to **IS 919** in India) is an internationally standardized scheme that:

- Defines **tolerance grades** (IT01, IT0, IT1, IT2, … IT18) — 20 grades in total.
- Defines **tolerance positions** using letter codes (H, h, g, k, p, etc.) for shafts and (A, B, C, H, P, etc.) for holes.
- Combines a *grade* with a *position* to produce standardized limits (e.g., 50 H7, 50 h6).

**Hole-Basis System** (preferred by ISO):

- The **hole's lower deviation (EI) = 0** for letter H.
- Tolerances on the shaft are varied to obtain different fits.
- **Example:** 50 H7/g6 — the hole is always at H7; the shaft is varied to control fit.

**Shaft-Basis System:**

- The **shaft's upper deviation (es) = 0** for letter h.
- Tolerances on the hole are varied to obtain different fits.
- **Example:** 50 H7/h6 vs 50 K7/h6 vs 50 P7/h6 — the shaft is always at h6; the hole is varied.

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Hole-Basis | Shaft-Basis |
|---|---|---|
| Standard | H-letter for hole | h-letter for shaft |
| Preferred for | General engineering (ISO recommendation) | Shafts requiring standard stock (e.g., bright bars) |
| Tools needed | Standard reamers/broaches of one size | Standard drills only |
| Economical when | Multiple fits on one shaft | Multiple fits on one hole |

*(7 marks — 2 for ISO definition, 3 for difference table, 2 for examples.)*

---

**Part (b) — Computing Standard Tolerance for D = 80 mm, IT7:**

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$$

For $D = 80 \; mm$:

$$\sqrt[3]{80} \approx 4.3089$$

$$i = 0.45 \cdot 4.3089 + 0.001 \cdot 80 = 1.9390 + 0.08 = 2.0190 \; \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Apply IT7 multiplier (k = 16):**

$$T_{IT7} = 16 \cdot i = 16 \cdot 2.0190 = 32.30 \; \mu m \approx 0.032 \; mm$$

**Step 3 — Verification:** Standard ISO 286 table for 50 < D ≤ 80 mm at IT7 lists **T = 30 µm**. Our computed value of 32.30 µm is very close (within empirical tolerance of the simplified formula). ✅

**Step 4 — Size band $30 < D \le 50$ mm at IT8:**

Using D = 50 mm (boundary value):

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{50} + 0.001 \cdot 50 = 1.6578 + 0.05 = 1.7078 \; \mu m$$

For IT8 (k = 25):

$$T_{IT8} = 25 \cdot 1.7078 = 42.7 \; \mu m \approx 0.043 \; mm$$

Standard ISO 286 table value for 30 < D ≤ 50 mm at IT8 is **39 µm**. The simplified formula gives a slightly higher value because the **step-function approximation** in ISO 286 uses size-band-averaged values, not exact continuous interpolation.

*(7 marks — 3 for IT7 computation, 2 for the rule, 2 for verification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the i-unit (µm vs mm):** The formula gives $i$ in **micrometres** for D in **millimetres**. Multiply by $10^{-3}$ to get mm — failure to do so loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing ES/es with EI/ei:** Uppercase letters are for HOLES; lowercase letters are for SHAFTS. Mixing them is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the "fit type" identification:** Even with all limits correctly computed, *not* writing "this is a clearance/transition/interference fit" results in -1 mark.
> 4. **Drawing deviation diagrams:** A neat tolerance-zone diagram earns 1 bonus mark in most KTU valuation keys — always include one.
> 5. **Confusing H7 (hole) and h7 (shaft):** The H7 hole has EI = 0; the h7 shaft has es = 0. Reversing them is a fatal error.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Tolerance = Max Limit − Min Limit** (basic equation; memorize first).
- ✅ **Tolerance grades are 20 in number: IT01, IT0, IT1 … IT18** (lower number = tighter tolerance).
- ✅ **Hole-basis (H)** is the **ISO-preferred system** — EI = 0 for letter H.
- ✅ **Shaft-basis (h)** — es = 0 for letter h; useful when buying standard bright bar stock.
- ✅ **Standard tolerance unit** $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$ (in µm, D in mm, for 0 < D ≤ 500 mm).
- ✅ **For D > 500 mm**, use $I = 0.004 \cdot D + 2.1$ (in µm).
- ✅ **Geometric tolerances** are classified into **five families** (Form, Orientation, Location, Profile, Runout).
- ✅ **Form tolerances** are the *only* datum-independent family.
- ✅ **True position** is the most-used location tolerance in modern GD&T.
- ✅ **Runout** is a quick substitute for combined form + orientation + location control.
- ✅ **Unilateral tolerance** is preferred over bilateral for **gauging simplicity**.
- ✅ **Allowance** is the *intentional* difference between mating parts — positive (clearance), zero (line), or negative (interference).
- ✅ **Tighter tolerance = exponentially higher cost** — always choose the *largest economical* tolerance that meets function.
- ✅ **Geometric tolerance symbols (MUST memorize):**
  - Straightness `—` · Flatness `▱` · Circularity `○` · Cylindricity `⌭`
  - Parallelism `∥` · Perpendicularity `⟂` · Angularity `∠`
  - Position `⌖` · Concentricity `◎` · Symmetry `⌯`
  - Circular Runout `↗` · Total Runout `↗↗`
  - Profile of Line `⌒` · Profile of Surface `⌓`
- ✅ **Datum hierarchy:** A (primary) → B (secondary) → C (tertiary).
- ✅ **ISO 286 / IS 919** is the controlling standard for dimensional tolerances; **ISO 1101** is for geometric tolerances.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Hack:** For a 14-mark problem, the examiner's valuation key allocates roughly **3 marks for limits computation, 3 for tolerance derivation, 3 for fit identification, 2 for diagrams, and 3 for the conceptual part**. Structure your answer accordingly!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Fit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types of Fit # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of engineering metrology and interchangeable manufacturing, a **Fit** is defined as the **degree of tightness or looseness** between two mating parts — specifically, the relationship that results from the **difference between the sizes of the hole and the shaft** before assembly. It is the *general term* used to describe the *range of tightness or looseness* that may result from the application of a definite combination of allowances and tolerances to the hole and shaft dimensions of mating parts. According to the **Indian Standard IS 919 (Part 1) : 1993** and **ISO 286-1 : 2010**, the type of fit between a hole and a shaft is fundamentally determined by the relative positions of their tolerance zones. The three primary classifications recognized globally are: 1. **Clearance Fit** (Loose Fit) 2. **Transition Fit** 3. **Interference Fit** (Force/Shrink Fit) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** *A fit is the relationship between two mating parts, a hole and a shaft, with respect to the difference in their dimensions before assembly. The type of fit is determined by the relative position of the tolerance zones of the hole and the shaft.* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition Imagine three everyday scenarios to intuitively grasp the three fits: | Real-World Analogy | Fit Type | Engineering Example | |---|---|---| | A **pencil sliding freely** in a slightly oversized cardboard tube | Clearance Fit | Shaft rotating in a plain bearing, sliding door rollers | | A **light bulb screwed into a socket** — needs gentle force and precise alignment | Transition Fit | Tapered keys, coupling hubs, locating pins | | A **gear press-fit onto a shaft** with a hammer or hydraulic press | Interference Fit | Permanent gear mounting, bushings in housings, rivets | > [!NOTE] > **The "Handshake" Intuition:** Think of a handshake. A *clearance fit* is a loose, casual handshake with visible air gaps. A *transition fit* is a firm, snug handshake — could be tight or just right. An *interference fit* is a bone-crushing grip that requires effort to break apart! ## 1.3 Fundamental Terminology — The Vocabulary of Fits Before diving deeper, master these **KTU-board-favorite** terms: - **Hole:** The *internal* (female) feature of mating. By ISO convention, the hole is designated by a capital letter **H** as the reference (fundamental deviation). - **Shaft:** The *external* (male) feature of mating. The shaft is designated by a lowercase letter. - **Basic Size (Nominal Size):** The *reference size* from which limits are derived. - **Maximum Size:** The largest permissible size of a part. - **Minimum Size:** The smallest permissible size of a part. - **Tolerance:** The *total permissible variation* in size = Maximum Size − Minimum Size. - **Allowance:** The *intentional, prescribed* difference between the maximum material conditions of mating parts. - **Maximum Clearance (C_max):** The largest clearance obtainable in a fit. - **Minimum Clearance (C_min):** The smallest clearance obtainable in a fit. - **Maximum Interference (I_max):** The largest interference obtainable in a fit. - **Minimum Interference (I_min):** The smallest interference obtainable in a fit. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic — "HMPS":** **H**ole, **M**aximum material, **P**art size, **S**haft. The hole is *always* internally dimensioned and the shaft is *always* externally dimensioned. Never reverse this convention. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Types of Fit — Detailed Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Clearance Fit (Loose Fit) **Definition:** A fit in which the tolerance zone of the hole is *always located entirely above* the tolerance zone of the shaft. This guarantees that **clearance exists in all possible combinations** of the mating parts. - **Algebraic Condition:** Hole size is *always greater than* Shaft size. - **Result:** A loose assembly; parts can always slide freely. - **KTU Keyword:** "Always produces a clearance." > [!NOTE] > **Boundary Case:** Even in the *worst-case* combination (largest shaft + smallest hole), there is still some clearance. This is the defining property. **Engineering Applications:** - Plain journal bearings - Sliding guides and ways - Loose pulleys on shafts - Hinges and pivots - Couplings that require axial movement ### 2.1.2 Transition Fit **Definition:** A fit in which the tolerance zones of the hole and the shaft **overlap partially**. Depending on the actual manufactured dimensions, the resulting fit may be either a *small clearance* or a *small interference*. - **Algebraic Condition:** Tolerance zones overlap. - **Result:** Either clearance OR interference (uncertain outcome). - **KTU Keyword:** "May produce either clearance or interference." **Engineering Applications:** - Tapered keys and dowel pins - Coupling hubs - Gear teeth on shafts (sometimes) - Locating pins in jigs - Plain bearings requiring precise location ### 2.1.3 Interference Fit (Force/Shrink Fit) **Definition:** A fit in which the tolerance zone of the hole is *always located entirely below* the tolerance zone of the shaft. This guarantees that **interference (press-fit) exists in all possible combinations**. - **Algebraic Condition:** Hole size is *always less than* Shaft size. - **Result:** A tight, permanent assembly; force is required to assemble. - **KTU Keyword:** "Always produces an interference." **Engineering Applications:** - Permanent gear and pulley mounting - Bushings in gear blanks - Riveted assemblies - Shaft couplings (permanent) - Bearing races in housings ## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master These Equations | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Maximum Clearance | $C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min}$ | mm | Largest possible clearance | | 2 | Minimum Clearance | $C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max}$ | mm | Smallest possible clearance (may be negative for transition/interference) | | 3 | Maximum Interference | $I_{max} = S_{max} - H_{min}$ | mm | Largest press-fit (always positive) | | 4 | Minimum Interference | $I_{min} = S_{min} - H_{max}$ | mm | Smallest press-fit (may be negative for transition/clearance) | | 5 | Allowance | $A = H_{max} - S_{max}$ (clearance) or $A = S_{max} - H_{min}$ (interference) | mm | Intentional design gap/overlap | | 6 | Tolerance of Hole | $T_h = H_{max} - H_{min}$ | mm | Hole tolerance zone width | | 7 | Tolerance of Shaft | $T_s = S_{max} - S_{min}$ | mm | Shaft tolerance zone width | | 8 | Fit Tolerance | $T_f = T_h + T_s$ | mm | Total variation in the fit | > [!WARNING] > **Pipe-Symbol Rule in Tables:** Notice the use of plain `C_max` and `C_min` notation in the table cells. In your KTU answer sheet, **never use the literal `|` symbol** between two characters (e.g., writing `H_max |` or `|I|`). This can break markdown table parsers. Use `$H_{max}$` in LaTeX or write `Hmax`/`Imax` plainly. ## 2.3 Hole-Basis vs. Shaft-Basis Fit Systems A critical KTU concept: when standardizing fits across an industry, **one component is treated as the reference** (the basis), while the mating component's tolerance zone is shifted. ### 2.3.1 Hole-Basis System (Most Common, IS/ISO Preferred) - The **hole is always at the lower limit** (i.e., $H_{min}$ = Basic Size for an H-holed system). - Fundamental deviation of the hole is fixed at **H** (lower deviation = 0). - **Advantage:** Only one drill bit, one reamer, and one set of plug gauges are needed for a given nominal size; only the shaft dimension changes for different fit classes. - **Disadvantage:** Shafts require varied machining. ### 2.3.2 Shaft-Basis System - The **shaft is always at the upper limit** (i.e., $S_{max}$ = Basic Size for an h-shafted system). - Fundamental deviation of the shaft is fixed at **h** (upper deviation = 0). - **Advantage:** Used when a long, ground bar is the reference (e.g., shaft of a machine tool). - **Disadvantage:** Multiple reamers/gauges needed for different hole sizes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rule of Thumb:** "When in doubt, default to the **Hole-Basis System**." It is the internationally preferred system per IS 919 and ISO 286, because standard drills and reamers are designed for the H-hole. ## 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility The proper selection of fit type directly impacts: 1. **Assembly Economics:** Clearance fits allow easy robotic assembly; interference fits require hydraulic presses (higher cost). 2. **Lubrication Retention:** Clearance fits in journal bearings must retain an oil film. 3. **Load Transmission:** Interference fits transmit torque without keys (cleaner design, higher reliability). 4. **Disassembly Needs:** If the assembly must be serviced, clearance/transition fits are mandatory. 5. **Vibration & Noise:** Loose fits vibrate; tight fits dampen vibration. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Fit Equations ### 3.1.1 Derivation of Maximum Clearance ($C_{max}$) We start with the *defining condition* for clearance: $$C = H - S \quad \text{(Clearance = Hole size − Shaft size)}$$ To obtain the **maximum** clearance, we need the hole to be as **large as possible** and the shaft to be as **small as possible** simultaneously. This corresponds to the **Least Material Condition (LMC)** of both parts. $$C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min}$$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. Define clearance as the positive difference between hole and shaft. 2. To maximize this difference, maximize the hole dimension: $H_{max}$. 3. To maximize this difference, minimize the shaft dimension: $S_{min}$. 4. Substitute both into the clearance equation. ### 3.1.2 Derivation of Minimum Clearance ($C_{min}$) To obtain the **minimum** clearance, we need the hole to be as **small as possible** and the shaft to be as **large as possible** simultaneously. This is the **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)** of both parts. $$C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max}$$ **Step-by-step logic:** 1. To minimize the positive difference (clearance), minimize the hole: $H_{min}$. 2. To minimize the positive difference, maximize the shaft: $S_{max}$. 3. Substituting gives the smallest clearance. If $C_{min} < 0$, the fit is *not* a clearance fit. ### 3.1.3 Derivation of Maximum Interference ($I_{max}$) Interference is the *negative* of clearance, or equivalently, the *excess* of shaft over hole: $$I = S - H \quad \text{(Interference = Shaft size − Hole size)}$$ To maximize interference, the shaft must be **as large as possible** and the hole **as small as possible**: $$I_{max} = S_{max} - H_{min}$$ ### 3.1.4 Derivation of Minimum Interference ($I_{min}$) To minimize interference, the shaft must be **as small as possible** and the hole **as large as possible**: $$I_{min} = S_{min} - H_{max}$$ ### 3.1.5 Derivation of Fit Tolerance ($T_f$) The total variation in the fit is the sum of the individual part tolerances. Consider the extreme cases: $$\begin{aligned} T_f &= C_{max} - C_{min} \\ &= (H_{max} - S_{min}) - (H_{min} - S_{max}) \\ &= H_{max} - S_{min} - H_{min} + S_{max} \\ &= (H_{max} - H_{min}) + (S_{max} - S_{min}) \\ &= T_h + T_s \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** The total "spread" of the fit equals the sum of the individual tolerance zones. ## 3.2 The Diagnostic Matrix — Identifying Fit Type from Numerical Data This is the **most exam-relevant decision rule** at KTU. Given the limits of a hole and a shaft, determine the fit type in *one glance*: | Condition | Fit Type | |---|---| | $C_{min} > 0$ i.e., $H_{min} > S_{max}$ | **Clearance Fit** (positive clearance always) | | $C_{max} < 0$ i.e., $H_{max} < S_{min}$ | **Interference Fit** (positive interference always) | | $C_{min} \le 0 \le C_{max}$ i.e., $H_{min} \le S_{max}$ AND $H_{max} \ge S_{min}$ | **Transition Fit** (clearance OR interference possible) | > [!NOTE] > **Visualization Tip:** Plot the hole and shaft tolerance zones on a number line. If the hole's zone sits *entirely above* the shaft's zone → clearance. If *entirely below* → interference. If they *overlap* → transition. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Standard **Problem:** A shaft-hole assembly has the following dimensions: - Hole: $H_{max} = 50.025$ mm, $H_{min} = 50.000$ mm - Shaft: $S_{max} = 50.018$ mm, $S_{min} = 49.992$ mm **Determine:** (a) Hole tolerance, (b) Shaft tolerance, (c) Type of fit, (d) Maximum and minimum clearance/interference, (e) Fit tolerance. **Solution:** **(a) Hole Tolerance:** $$T_h = H_{max} - H_{min} = 50.025 - 50.000 = 0.025 \text{ mm}$$ **(b) Shaft Tolerance:** $$T_s = S_{max} - S_{min} = 50.018 - 49.992 = 0.026 \text{ mm}$$ **(c) Type of Fit — Apply the Diagnostic Rule:** Check whether $H_{min} > S_{max}$: $$50.000 \text{ mm} \text{ vs. } 50.018 \text{ mm}$$ $$50.000 < 50.018 \implies H_{min} \text{ is NOT greater than } S_{max}$$ So this is **not** a clearance fit. Check whether $H_{max} < S_{min}$: $$50.025 \text{ mm} \text{ vs. } 49.992 \text{ mm}$$ $$50.025 > 49.992 \implies H_{max} \text{ is NOT less than } S_{min}$$ So this is **not** a pure interference fit. Therefore, since the tolerance zones **overlap**, this is a **Transition Fit**. **(d) Compute the boundary values:** Maximum Clearance: $$C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min} = 50.025 - 49.992 = +0.033 \text{ mm}$$ Minimum Clearance (will be negative, indicating possible interference): $$C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max} = 50.000 - 50.018 = -0.018 \text{ mm}$$ Since $C_{min} = -0.018$ mm, the corresponding maximum interference is: $$I_{max} = -C_{min} = +0.018 \text{ mm}$$ The minimum interference is: $$I_{min} = -C_{max} = -0.033 \text{ mm}$$ Since $I_{min} < 0$, sometimes the fit is a *small clearance* of up to 0.033 mm, and sometimes a *small interference* of up to 0.018 mm. **(e) Fit Tolerance:** $$T_f = T_h + T_s = 0.025 + 0.026 = 0.051 \text{ mm}$$ **Verification using alternative formula:** $$T_f = C_{max} - C_{min} = 0.033 - (-0.018) = 0.051 \text{ mm} \checkmark$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic Representation of the Three Fits (Tolerance Zone Visualization) The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the *functional logic* used to classify a fit based on the relative positions of the hole and shaft tolerance zones. ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef clearanceBox fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef interferenceBox fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef transitionBox fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef neutralBox fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 Start([Given: Hole and Shaft Limits]) --> Compare{Check Hmin versus Smax} Compare -->|Hmin greater than Smax| ClearanceResult[Clearance Fit
Cmax positive, Cmin positive
Hole zone above shaft zone] Compare -->|Hmax less than Smin| InterferenceResult[Interference Fit
Imax positive, Imin positive
Hole zone below shaft zone] Compare -->|Zones Overlap| TransitionResult[Transition Fit
Cmax positive, Cmin negative
May be clearance or interference] ClearanceResult --> ClearanceApps[Use: Bearings, Slides, Loose Couplings] InterferenceResult --> InterferenceApps[Use: Permanent Gears, Bushings, Rivets] TransitionResult --> TransitionApps[Use: Keys, Dowel Pins, Hubs] class ClearanceResult,ClearanceApps clearanceBox class InterferenceResult,InterferenceApps interferenceBox class TransitionResult,TransitionApps transitionBox class Start,Compare neutralBox ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fit Selection ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef input fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef process fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 Nominal[Nominal Size D]:::input FuncReq[Functional Requirement]:::input MatType[Material Type]:::input LoadType[Load Type Static or Dynamic]:::input Nominal --> SelectSys{Choose Hole or Shaft Basis} FuncReq --> SelectSys MatType --> SelectSys LoadType --> SelectSys SelectSys -->|Hole Basis| HB[Fix Hole at H Lower Deviation 0]:::process SelectSys -->|Shaft Basis| SB[Fix Shaft at h Upper Deviation 0]:::process HB --> TolerClass[Select Tolerance Grade IT01 to IT18]:::process SB --> TolerClass TolerClass --> FitClass[Select Fit Code Clearance Transition Interference]:::process FitClass --> FinalDims[Compute Hmax Hmin Smax Smin]:::output FinalDims --> Verify[Verify Cmax Cmin Imax Imin meet design intent]:::output ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Diagram — Tolerance Zone Positions ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef clr fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef trn fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef intf fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000 classDef axis fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 Ax1[Clearance Fit Number Line]:::axis Ax2[Transition Fit Number Line]:::axis Ax3[Interference Fit Number Line]:::axis Ax1 --> HoleA[Hole Zone: Above]:::clr Ax1 --> ShaftA[Shaft Zone: Below]:::clr Ax1 --> GapA[Always Clearance]:::clr Ax2 --> HoleB[Hole Zone]:::trn Ax2 --> ShaftB[Shaft Zone Overlapping]:::trn Ax2 --> OverlapB[Overlap Region]:::trn Ax3 --> HoleC[Hole Zone: Below]:::intf Ax3 --> ShaftC[Shaft Zone: Above]:::intf Ax3 --> PressA[Always Interference]:::intf ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to Read the Diagrams:** Each "Number Line" represents the basic size axis. The *position* of the colored box relative to the basic size line shows where the tolerance zone of the hole or shaft lies. The diagrams clearly show the three possible arrangements: hole-above (clearance), hole-below (interference), or overlapping (transition). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define "Fit" and list its types with one example each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** A **fit** is the degree of tightness or looseness between two mating parts (a hole and a shaft) that results from the difference between their dimensions before assembly. The three types of fits as per IS 919 / ISO 286 are: 1. **Clearance Fit:** Always produces clearance. *Example:* Shaft in a plain journal bearing. 2. **Transition Fit:** May produce either clearance or interference. *Example:* Tapered key on a shaft. 3. **Interference Fit:** Always produces interference (press-fit). *Example:* Bushing pressed into a gear blank. ### Question 2: Distinguish between Hole-Basis and Shaft-Basis systems. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Basis | Reference Part | Fundamental Deviation | Industrial Preference | |---|---|---|---| | **Hole-Basis** | Hole is the reference | Hole is at lower deviation (H, deviation = 0) | ISO/IS preferred system | | **Shaft-Basis** | Shaft is the reference | Shaft is at upper deviation (h, deviation = 0) | Used for long ground shafts | In the **Hole-Basis system**, only one size of reamer/gauge is needed for a given nominal diameter; the shaft is varied. In the **Shaft-Basis system**, a standard shaft is held constant; the hole is varied. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q.** A hole and shaft assembly has the following specifications: - Basic size = 60 mm - Hole: 60.000 mm to 60.030 mm - Shaft: 60.020 mm to 60.050 mm **(a)** Identify the type of fit with justification. (7 Marks) **(b)** Calculate the maximum clearance, minimum clearance, maximum interference, minimum interference, and the fit tolerance. (7 Marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — Type of Fit Identification (7 Marks):** Given data: - $H_{max} = 60.030$ mm, $H_{min} = 60.000$ mm - $S_{max} = 60.050$ mm, $S_{min} = 60.020$ mm Apply the diagnostic rule. First, check if it is a clearance fit by verifying $H_{min} > S_{max}$: $$H_{min} = 60.000 \text{ mm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad S_{max} = 60.050 \text{ mm}$$ $$60.000 < 60.050$$ The condition $H_{min} > S_{max}$ is **NOT** satisfied, so this is **not** a clearance fit. Next, check if it is a pure interference fit by verifying $H_{max} < S_{min}$: $$H_{max} = 60.030 \text{ mm} \quad \text{vs.} \quad S_{min} = 60.020 \text{ mm}$$ $$60.030 > 60.020$$ The condition $H_{max} < S_{min}$ is **NOT** satisfied either, so this is **not** a pure interference fit. Since neither boundary condition holds and the tolerance zones overlap ($H_{max} = 60.030 > S_{min} = 60.020$), this is a **Transition Fit**. **Part (b) — Quantitative Calculations (7 Marks):** Maximum Clearance: $$C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min} = 60.030 - 60.020 = +0.010 \text{ mm}$$ Minimum Clearance: $$C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max} = 60.000 - 60.050 = -0.050 \text{ mm}$$ Since $C_{min}$ is negative, the maximum interference is: $$I_{max} = -C_{min} = -(-0.050) = +0.050 \text{ mm}$$ The minimum interference is: $$I_{min} = -C_{max} = -(+0.010) = -0.010 \text{ mm}$$ Hole tolerance: $$T_h = H_{max} - H_{min} = 60.030 - 60.000 = 0.030 \text{ mm}$$ Shaft tolerance: $$T_s = S_{max} - S_{min} = 60.050 - 60.020 = 0.030 \text{ mm}$$ Fit tolerance: $$T_f = T_h + T_s = 0.030 + 0.030 = 0.060 \text{ mm}$$ **Summary Table:** | Parameter | Value (mm) | |---|---| | Maximum Clearance, $C_{max}$ | +0.010 | | Minimum Clearance, $C_{min}$ | −0.050 | | Maximum Interference, $I_{max}$ | +0.050 | | Minimum Interference, $I_{min}$ | −0.010 | | Fit Tolerance, $T_f$ | 0.060 | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q.** With the help of neat sketches, explain the three types of fits based on the relative positions of tolerance zones. For each type, derive the expressions for maximum and minimum clearance/interference. State two practical applications of each. (14 Marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — Clearance Fit (7 Marks):** In a **clearance fit**, the tolerance zone of the hole lies *completely above* the tolerance zone of the shaft. This means that irrespective of the actual manufactured dimensions, the hole is always larger than the shaft. *Sketch description:* Draw a horizontal number line marked "Basic Size." Above this line on the right side, draw the hole tolerance zone. Below the line on the left side, draw the shaft tolerance zone. Clearly mark the gap between the two zones as the "Allowance" and the entire spread as the "Maximum Clearance" region. Derivation: - The general clearance is $C = H - S$. - Maximum clearance occurs when the hole is largest and the shaft is smallest: $C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min}$. - Minimum clearance occurs when the hole is smallest and the shaft is largest: $C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max}$. - For a clearance fit, $C_{min} > 0$ (a strict inequality). *Practical applications:* 1. Journal bearings in machine tool spindles (rotating shafts need oil-film clearance). 2. Sliding doors and conveyor rollers (linear motion applications). **Part (b) — Transition and Interference Fits (7 Marks):** In a **transition fit**, the tolerance zones of the hole and shaft *partially overlap*. The fit may result in either a small clearance or a small interference, depending on the actual manufactured sizes. *Sketch description:* Draw the basic size axis. Draw the hole and shaft tolerance zones such that they overlap partially. Label the clearance region, the interference region, and the overlap region. For transition fit, $C_{max} > 0$ and $C_{min} < 0$. The same formulas apply: $$C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min}, \qquad C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max}$$ $$I_{max} = S_{max} - H_{min}, \qquad I_{min} = S_{min} - H_{max}$$ *Practical applications of transition fit:* 1. Tapered keys and feather keys on shafts. 2. Locating dowel pins in jigs and fixtures. In an **interference fit**, the tolerance zone of the hole lies *completely below* the tolerance zone of the shaft. The shaft is always larger than the hole, requiring force for assembly. *Sketch description:* Draw the basic size axis. Draw the shaft tolerance zone above the axis and the hole tolerance zone below. Label the overlap as the "interference" region. For interference fit, $I_{min} > 0$ (always positive interference). The formulas: $$I_{max} = S_{max} - H_{min}, \qquad I_{min} = S_{min} - H_{max}$$ *Practical applications of interference fit:* 1. Permanent mounting of gears or pulleys on shafts. 2. Bearing races pressed into housings. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks at KTU 2024 Board Exams:** > 1. **Sign Confusion:** Students frequently mix up which is positive and which is negative. *Always* state: **$C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min}$** (the *largest* hole minus the *smallest* shaft). > 2. **Forgetting Units:** KTU examiners deduct marks if the final answer is not in **mm** (or $\mu$m). Always write the unit. > 3. **Misclassifying the Fit:** Many students look at $C_{min}$ alone and say "it has negative clearance so it's interference." The correct check is to verify $C_{min} > 0$ for clearance, $C_{max} < 0$ for interference, and otherwise transition. *Both* conditions matter. > 4. **Skipping Justification:** KTU 2024 Scheme demands *justification* for the fit type, not just a label. You must show the numerical comparison. > 5. **Forgetting $T_f$:** Many students calculate $C_{max}$ and $C_{min}$ but forget the *fit tolerance*, which is a 1-mark item. > 6. **Confusing Allowance with Clearance:** Allowance is the *designer's intentional* minimum gap; clearance is the *actual* range of gaps achievable after manufacturing. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Types of Fit":** > - **Three Fit Types:** Clearance, Transition, Interference. > - **Clearance Fit:** $H_{min} > S_{max}$ — always loose, $C_{min} > 0$. > - **Interference Fit:** $H_{max} < S_{min}$ — always tight, $I_{min} > 0$. > - **Transition Fit:** Tolerance zones overlap — sometimes loose, sometimes tight. > - **Key Formulas (commit to memory):** > - $C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min}$ > - $C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max}$ > - $I_{max} = S_{max} - H_{min}$ > - $I_{min} = S_{min} - H_{max}$ > - $T_f = T_h + T_s = C_{max} - C_{min}$ > - **Hole-Basis System:** Hole = H (lower deviation 0); preferred by IS/ISO. > - **Shaft-Basis System:** Shaft = h (upper deviation 0); used for long ground bars. > - **Always check BOTH boundary conditions** when classifying a fit. > - **Hole is ALWAYS the internal (female) feature; shaft is ALWAYS the external (male) feature.** > - **Industrial examples to remember for viva:** > - Bearings → Clearance > - Keys/Dowels → Transition > - Gear mounting → Interference

topics.title: Allowances - Hole Basis and Shaft Basis Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Allowances - Hole Basis and Shaft Basis Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Allowance

In the context of engineering fits and tolerances, an **Allowance** is defined as the *intentional, prescribed difference between the maximum material condition (MMC) dimensions of two mating parts*. In simpler terms, it is the minimum clearance (positive allowance) or maximum interference (negative allowance) that is intentionally designed into a fit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Allowance = MMC of Hole − MMC of Shaft**
> - When the result is **positive**, it represents the **minimum clearance** in a clearance fit.
> - When the result is **negative**, it represents the **maximum interference** in an interference fit.
> - When the result is **zero**, it indicates a boundary case between clearance and interference (transition fit limit).

> [!NOTE]
> Allowance is **always** a *design-controlled* value, distinct from tolerance, which is a *manufacturing-controlled* value. Allowance governs the **function** of the assembly (sliding, snug, or press); tolerance governs the **cost and precision** of manufacture.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are buying a **bracelet (the shaft)** that must slide onto your **wrist (the hole)**.

- If you want a **loose, freely rotating fit** (like a gear on a shaft), the wrist hole must be designed *larger* than the bracelet's largest possible size. This positive gap is your **clearance allowance**.
- If you want a **firm, snug grip** (like a peg in a hole), the bracelet's smallest size must be *just slightly less* than the wrist's smallest size — a small **positive allowance**.
- If you want a **press-fit** (like a bushing in a housing that must not slip), the bracelet must always be *larger* than the wrist's largest possible size. This is a **negative allowance (interference)**.

In all three cases, the *designer* decides the allowance. The *manufacturer* then must respect the tolerance window to ensure the actual part lands within that designed allowance. The **Hole Basis** vs **Shaft Basis** system is simply the *convention* used to decide **which part** carries the fixed allowance and which carries the variable tolerance.

---

## 1.3 Definition of Hole Basis System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hole Basis System:** A system of fits in which the **hole is taken as the basic (nominal) size**, and the **lower deviation of the hole is fixed at zero (H-designation)**. The shaft tolerance is then varied to obtain the desired type of fit.

In this system:
- The hole's lower limit = Basic size (i.e., lower deviation = 0).
- The hole's tolerance zone always lies **above** the basic size line (toward higher dimensions).
- To achieve different fits, the shaft's tolerance zone is **shifted vertically** relative to the hole's zone.
- This is the **preferred system worldwide** (used in **~90% of all engineering applications**), particularly in machining, where standard drill bits, reamers, and broaches naturally produce "H" tolerance holes.

---

## 1.4 Definition of Shaft Basis System

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shaft Basis System:** A system of fits in which the **shaft is taken as the basic size**, and the **upper deviation of the shaft is fixed at zero (h-designation)**. The hole tolerance is then varied to obtain different types of fits.

In this system:
- The shaft's upper limit = Basic size (i.e., upper deviation = 0).
- The shaft's tolerance zone lies **below** the basic size line.
- To achieve different fits, the hole's tolerance zone is shifted vertically.
- This system is **preferred for standard shafts** (e.g., standard rolling-bearing shafts, electric-motor shafts, cold-drawn standard shafts), because shaft-based stock is more readily available as a standard commercial product.

---

## 1.5 Real-World Examples

| Application | Preferred Basis | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Drilling holes in castings | Hole Basis | Drill bits, reamers, broaches produce H-grade holes naturally |
| Standard shafts (bearings, motors) | Shaft Basis | Standard shafts are commercially manufactured and stocked in h-grade |
| Automobile engine pistons | Hole Basis | Cylinder is machined; piston is selectively grouped |
| Pins and standard fasteners | Shaft Basis | Standard pins are produced in h-grade; holes are machined to match |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Definitions Required Before Allowance Calculations

Before solving problems on allowance and basis systems, the following nine core definitions (per IS 919 and ISO 286) must be internalized:

1. **Basic Size (Nominal Size):** The reference size from which limits are derived. It is the same for both mating parts (e.g., 50 mm).
2. **Actual Size:** The measured size of the finished part (varies from piece to piece).
3. **Maximum Size (Max Limit):** The largest permissible size of the part.
4. **Minimum Size (Min Limit):** The smallest permissible size of the part.
5. **Tolerance:** The total permissible variation in size, equal to **Max Limit − Min Limit**. It is always **positive** and is denoted with the symbol **"T"** or **"t"**.
6. **Deviation:** The algebraic difference between a size and the corresponding basic size.
   - **Upper Deviation** = Max Limit − Basic Size
   - **Lower Deviation** = Min Limit − Basic Size
7. **Zero Line:** A theoretical line corresponding to the basic size, from which deviations are measured.
8. **Tolerance Zone:** The region bounded by the two lines representing the maximum and minimum sizes.
9. **Fit:** The range of tightness or looseness resulting from the tolerance zones of the mating parts.

---

## 2.2 Types of Fits (Critical for Allowance Analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to *identify the type of fit* from given hole and shaft tolerances. Memorize the three categories below.

| Fit Type | Condition | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Clearance Fit** | Min Hole > Max Shaft (always positive clearance) | Parts always separate easily; e.g., sliding fits, bearing bushes |
| **Interference Fit (Press Fit)** | Max Hole < Min Shaft (always negative clearance) | Parts must be forced together; e.g., gear hubs on shafts |
| **Transition Fit** | Min Hole < Max Shaft AND Max Hole > Min Shaft | Could be either; e.g., precision locating fits |

The **allowance** in each case:
- **Clearance Fit:** Allowance = Min Hole − Max Shaft (positive value)
- **Interference Fit:** Allowance = Min Hole − Max Shaft (negative value, magnitude = max interference)
- **Transition Fit:** Allowance can be slightly positive or negative depending on actual manufactured sizes.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes **all essential formulas** required to solve numerical problems on allowance and basis systems. These are direct application of IS 919 / ISO 286 conventions.

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Tolerance of Hole | $T_h = \text{Max Hole} - \text{Min Hole}$ | mm |
| Tolerance of Shaft | $T_s = \text{Max Shaft} - \text{Min Shaft}$ | mm |
| Upper Deviation (Hole) | $\text{Upper Dev}_h = \text{Max Hole} - \text{Basic Size}$ | mm |
| Lower Deviation (Hole) | $\text{Lower Dev}_h = \text{Min Hole} - \text{Basic Size}$ | mm |
| Upper Deviation (Shaft) | $\text{Upper Dev}_s = \text{Max Shaft} - \text{Basic Size}$ | mm |
| Lower Deviation (Shaft) | $\text{Lower Dev}_s = \text{Min Shaft} - \text{Basic Size}$ | mm |
| Allowance | $\text{Allowance} = \text{MMC of Hole} - \text{MMC of Shaft}$ | mm |
| Maximum Clearance | $C_{max} = \text{Max Hole} - \text{Min Shaft}$ | mm |
| Minimum Clearance | $C_{min} = \text{Min Hole} - \text{Max Shaft}$ | mm |
| Maximum Interference | $I_{max} = \text{Min Hole} - \text{Max Shaft}$ | mm |
| Minimum Interference | $I_{min} = \text{Max Hole} - \text{Min Shaft}$ | mm |

---

## 2.4 Identification Rules for Hole Basis vs Shaft Basis

| Basis System | Hole Convention | Shaft Convention |
|---|---|---|
| **Hole Basis** | Lower deviation of hole = 0 | Shaft varies to obtain different fits |
| **Shaft Basis** | Upper deviation of shaft = 0 | Hole varies to obtain different fits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Identification Tip:** When you see "**H7**" or "**H8**" in a fit designation (e.g., 50 H7/g6), the symbol **H** refers to the hole — this confirms it is a **Hole Basis** system. Similarly, **h** refers to the shaft — when shafts are designated as "h6" or "h7", it implies **Shaft Basis**.

---

## 2.5 Advantages and Disadvantages (High-Yield for KTU 2-Mark Questions)

### Hole Basis System
**Advantages:**
- Easier and more economical to manufacture standard tools (drills, reamers, broaches) because the lower limit is fixed at zero.
- Less wear of standard cutting tools, since the hole has a positive tolerance.
- Easier to inspect (gauges are simpler to make).
- Recommended by ISO 286 as the **preferred system**.

**Disadvantages:**
- Requires a wider range of shafts to be stocked.
- For long shafts, small variations in the hole size lead to large variations in fit character.

### Shaft Basis System
**Advantages:**
- Useful when a standard shaft is commercially available (e.g., standard motor shafts, drawn bars).
- Economical for mass production of shafts.
- Reduces the variety of holes required.

**Disadvantages:**
- Holes with negative tolerances require special care in manufacture.
- Less common globally; used mainly for shaft-mounted components.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hole-basis / shaft-basis decision directly impacts:
- **Tool inventory cost** (a factory storing 50 H7 drills vs. 50 different hole-graded drills).
- **Assembly line flexibility** (random mating in hole basis; selective mating in shaft basis for very tight fits).
- **Quality control strategy** (Go/No-Go gauges are designed based on the basis system chosen).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **fully worked numerical examples** that mirror KTU's actual board-exam numerical problem style. Each algebraic step is explicitly shown — no shortcuts.

---

## 3.1 Problem 1 (Classic KTU Numerical — Clearance Fit, Hole Basis)

> **Problem Statement:** A hole and shaft assembly has the following tolerances:
> - Hole: $\phi 60^{+0.030}_{+0.000}$ mm
> - Shaft: $\phi 60^{-0.020}_{-0.040}$ mm
>
> Determine: (i) Basic size, (ii) Allowance, (iii) Maximum clearance, (iv) Minimum clearance, (v) Type of fit, (vi) Is it Hole Basis or Shaft Basis?

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the Basic Size**

The basic size is the nominal reference size common to both hole and shaft. From the given dimensions:

$$\text{Basic Size} = 60 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Hole Limits**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum Hole} &= \text{Basic Size} + \text{Upper Deviation}_h \\
&= 60 + 0.030 \\
&= 60.030 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum Hole} &= \text{Basic Size} + \text{Lower Deviation}_h \\
&= 60 + 0.000 \\
&= 60.000 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Shaft Limits**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum Shaft} &= 60 + (-0.020) \\
&= 59.980 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum Shaft} &= 60 + (-0.040) \\
&= 59.960 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Allowance**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Allowance} &= \text{Minimum Hole} - \text{Maximum Shaft} \\
&= 60.000 - 59.980 \\
&= +0.020 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Maximum Clearance**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= \text{Maximum Hole} - \text{Minimum Shaft} \\
&= 60.030 - 59.960 \\
&= 0.070 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Minimum Clearance**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{min} &= \text{Minimum Hole} - \text{Maximum Shaft} \\
&= 60.000 - 59.980 \\
&= 0.020 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Identify the Basis System**

Since the **lower deviation of the hole = 0** (H-designation), this is a **Hole Basis System**.

**Step 8 — Identify the Type of Fit**

Since **Minimum Clearance (0.020 mm) is positive**, the fit is a **Clearance Fit**.

**Step 9 — Compute Hole and Shaft Tolerances (KTU often asks this as a bonus step)**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_h &= 60.030 - 60.000 = 0.030 \text{ mm} \\
T_s &= 59.980 - 59.960 = 0.020 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Answer (Formatted as Required for KTU Valuation):

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Basic Size | $60$ mm |
| Allowance | $+0.020$ mm |
| Maximum Clearance | $0.070$ mm |
| Minimum Clearance | $0.020$ mm |
| Type of Fit | Clearance Fit |
| Basis System | Hole Basis System |
| Hole Tolerance | $0.030$ mm |
| Shaft Tolerance | $0.020$ mm |

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 (Transition Fit — KTU-Favorite Type)

> **Problem Statement:** For an assembly, the hole and shaft dimensions are given as:
> - Hole: $\phi 50^{+0.025}_{+0.000}$ mm
> - Shaft: $\phi 50^{+0.012}_{+0.001}$ mm
>
> Determine: (i) Allowance, (ii) Maximum and minimum clearance, (iii) Maximum and minimum interference, (iv) Type of fit.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Hole Limits**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum Hole} &= 50 + 0.025 = 50.025 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Minimum Hole} &= 50 + 0.000 = 50.000 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Shaft Limits**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum Shaft} &= 50 + 0.012 = 50.012 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Minimum Shaft} &= 50 + 0.001 = 50.001 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Allowance**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Allowance} &= \text{Minimum Hole} - \text{Maximum Shaft} \\
&= 50.000 - 50.012 \\
&= -0.012 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

The **negative sign** indicates an interference of magnitude $0.012$ mm under maximum material condition.

**Step 4 — Maximum Clearance**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= \text{Maximum Hole} - \text{Minimum Shaft} \\
&= 50.025 - 50.001 \\
&= +0.024 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(positive — clearance possible)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Interference**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{max} &= \text{Minimum Hole} - \text{Maximum Shaft} \\
&= 50.000 - 50.012 \\
&= -0.012 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(negative — interference possible)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the Type of Fit**

- $C_{max} = +0.024$ mm (positive) → clearance is possible
- $I_{max} = -0.012$ mm (negative) → interference is also possible

Since the assembly may be **either a clearance or interference fit** depending on the actual manufactured sizes, this is a **Transition Fit**.

### Final Answer:

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Allowance | $-0.012$ mm |
| Maximum Clearance | $+0.024$ mm |
| Maximum Interference | $0.012$ mm |
| Type of Fit | Transition Fit |
| Basis System | Hole Basis (since lower hole deviation = 0) |

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 (Interference / Press Fit — KTU-Favorite Type)

> **Problem Statement:** Determine the allowance and type of fit for the assembly with:
> - Hole: $\phi 40^{+0.016}_{+0.000}$ mm
> - Shaft: $\phi 40^{+0.034}_{+0.022}$ mm

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Hole Limits**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum Hole} &= 40.000 + 0.016 = 40.016 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Minimum Hole} &= 40.000 + 0.000 = 40.000 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Shaft Limits**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum Shaft} &= 40.000 + 0.034 = 40.034 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Minimum Shaft} &= 40.000 + 0.022 = 40.022 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Allowance (Maximum Interference)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Allowance} &= \text{Minimum Hole} - \text{Maximum Shaft} \\
&= 40.000 - 40.034 \\
&= -0.034 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum Interference**

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{min} &= \text{Maximum Hole} - \text{Minimum Shaft} \\
&= 40.016 - 40.022 \\
&= -0.006 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Type of Fit**

Since both quantities are negative (interference always exists), this is an **Interference Fit (Press Fit)**.

### Final Answer:

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Allowance | $-0.034$ mm |
| Maximum Interference | $0.034$ mm |
| Minimum Interference | $0.006$ mm |
| Type of Fit | Interference (Press) Fit |
| Basis System | Hole Basis |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For KTU Exam Theory Answers)

| Feature | Hole Basis System | Shaft Basis System |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed Element | Hole (lower deviation = 0) | Shaft (upper deviation = 0) |
| Variable Element | Shaft | Hole |
| Preferred For | Holes produced by standard tools (drills, reamers) | Standard shafts (cold-drawn bars, motor shafts) |
| Industrial Usage | ~90% of all fits worldwide | ~10% of all fits worldwide |
| Tolerance Symbol Convention | H for hole | h for shaft |
| Cost Implication | Lower tooling cost | Lower shaft inventory cost |
| Inspection Complexity | Simpler (fixed lower limit) | More complex (variable hole limit) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams illustrate the tolerance-zone schematics for the three types of fits and the difference between the two basis systems. These block-level functional diagrams are exactly what a student should reproduce (or interpret) in KTU answer sheets.

---

## 4.1 Tolerance Zone Schematic — Clearance Fit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BASIC["Basic Size Line (60 mm reference)"]
        direction LR
        Z[Zero Line]:::basic
    end
    subgraph HOLE["HOLE TOLERANCE ZONE (Above Zero Line)"]
        H1["Min Hole = 60.000 mm"]:::hole
        H2["Max Hole = 60.030 mm"]:::hole
        H1 --> H2
    end
    subgraph SHAFT["SHAFT TOLERANCE ZONE (Below Zero Line)"]
        S1["Max Shaft = 59.980 mm"]:::shaft
        S2["Min Shaft = 59.960 mm"]:::shaft
        S1 --> S2
    end
    ARROW1["Min Clearance = 0.020 mm (Allowance)"]:::annot
    ARROW2["Max Clearance = 0.070 mm"]:::annot
    HOLE -- "Allowance gap" --> SHAFT
    classDef basic fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    classDef hole fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#000
    classDef shaft fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281f,color:#000

```

---

## 4.2 Tolerance Zone Schematic — Transition Fit

```mermaid

        direction LR

    end
    subgraph HOLE2["HOLE ZONE (Above Zero Line)"]

        H3 --> H4
    end
    subgraph SHAFT2["SHAFT ZONE (Crosses Into Hole Region)"]
        S3["Min Shaft = 50.001 mm"]:::shaft
        S4["Max Shaft = 50.012 mm"]:::shaft
        S3 --> S4
    end
    OVERLAP["Overlap = 0.012 mm = Transition Region"]:::annot

```
