<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This section mirrors the **actual KTU End Semester Examination (ESE) pattern** for Module 3 of PCMET402. Every question is tagged with a simulated KTU past-year style header, complete Bloom's Taxonomy level, marks, and detailed model solutions.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define the term "Allowance" in fits and tolerances. With the help of neat sketches, explain the difference between hole basis system and shaft basis system. State which system is preferred by ISO standards.** (3 Marks)
*Bloom's Level: Understand (CO 2)*

### Model Answer:

**Allowance:** Allowance is the intentional, prescribed difference between the maximum material condition (MMC) of the hole and the shaft. Mathematically:

$$\text{Allowance} = \text{Min Hole} - \text{Max Shaft}$$

A **positive allowance** indicates a clearance fit, while a **negative allowance** indicates an interference fit.

**Hole Basis System:** The hole is taken as the basic size, with its **lower deviation fixed at zero (H-designation)**. The shaft tolerance zone is varied to obtain different fits.

**Shaft Basis System:** The shaft is taken as the basic size, with its **upper deviation fixed at zero (h-designation)**. The hole tolerance zone is varied to obtain different fits.

**Preferred System:** **Hole Basis System** is recommended by **ISO 286** because standard cutting tools (drills, reamers, broaches) inherently produce H-grade holes, reducing manufacturing and inspection costs.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**List the three types of fits. For each type, write the relationship between hole and shaft limits, and state one engineering application.** (3 Marks)
*Bloom's Level: Remember (CO 2)*

### Model Answer:

The three types of fits and their relationships are:

| Fit Type | Relationship | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Clearance Fit** | Min Hole > Max Shaft | Sliding gears, bearing bushes, pulley hubs |
| **Interference (Press) Fit** | Max Hole < Min Shaft | Permanent couplings, gear blanks on shafts, riveted assemblies |
| **Transition Fit** | Min Hole < Max Shaft < Max Hole | Precision locating pins, dowel pins, clutch hubs |

In each case, allowance is computed as **Min Hole − Max Shaft**, and the sign of this quantity uniquely determines the fit type.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** Students often forget that allowance can be negative. Always state "−0.012 mm" with the **minus sign** for interference fits.
> 2. **Confusing $C_{min}$ with Allowance:** They are **the same** in clearance fits, but **different** in transition fits. Show both clearly.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Always write "mm" after every numerical value.
> 4. **Sketches required:** A neat tolerance-zone sketch is worth **2 marks** even if numericals are slightly off. Always draw it.
> 5. **Hole vs Shaft basis identification:** Look at the **lower deviation of the hole**. If it is **zero** → Hole Basis. If the shaft's **upper deviation** is zero → Shaft Basis.

---

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024] (14 Marks)

**A shaft of nominal diameter 80 mm is to be coupled with a hole. The hole tolerance is given as $+0.000$ to $+0.030$ mm and the shaft tolerance is given as $-0.020$ to $-0.040$ mm.**

**(a)** Determine the **(i)** Basic size, **(ii)** Allowance, **(iii)** Maximum and minimum clearance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Identify the type of fit and basis system. Draw the tolerance zone diagram and state one industrial application. **(7 Marks)**

*Bloom's Level: Apply / Analyze (CO 3)*

---

### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Numerical Determination (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Basic Size**
Basic size = **80 mm** (common nominal reference dimension for both parts).

**Step 2 — Hole Limits**
- Max Hole = $80 + 0.030 = 80.030$ mm
- Min Hole = $80 + 0.000 = 80.000$ mm

**Step 3 — Shaft Limits**
- Max Shaft = $80 + (-0.020) = 79.980$ mm
- Min Shaft = $80 + (-0.040) = 79.960$ mm

**Step 4 — Allowance**

$$\text{Allowance} = 80.000 - 79.980 = +0.020 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Clearance**

$$C_{max} = 80.030 - 79.960 = 0.070 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Minimum Clearance**

$$C_{min} = 80.000 - 79.980 = 0.020 \text{ mm}$$

#### Part (b) — Identification and Application (7 Marks)

**Type of Fit:** Since both clearances are **positive** ($C_{min} = +0.020$ mm, $C_{max} = +0.070$ mm), this is a **Clearance Fit**.

**Basis System:** Since the **lower deviation of the hole is zero** (H-designation), this is a **Hole Basis System**.

**Tolerance Zone Diagram (Schematic Description):**
```
  80.030 ┤━━━━━━━━━ Max Hole
  80.000 ┤━━━━━━━━━ Min Hole    ┐
         │                      │ Allowance gap
  79.980 ┤━━━━━━━━━ Max Shaft   ┘
  79.960 ┤━━━━━━━━━ Min Shaft
         └─────────────────────
         0.070 ←── Max Clearance
         0.020 ←── Min Clearance (= Allowance)
```

**Industrial Application:** Suitable for **sliding gear engagement on a countershaft**, where the gear must slide axially to engage different ratios while remaining radially located.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice) (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define **Hole Basis System** and **Shaft Basis System**. State **four advantages** of the Hole Basis System over the Shaft Basis System. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two mating parts have the following dimensions:
- Hole: $\phi 50^{+0.039}_{+0.020}$ mm
- Shaft: $\phi 50^{+0.017}_{+0.002}$ mm

Determine: **(i)** Allowance, **(ii)** Maximum and minimum clearance, **(iii)** Type of fit, **(iv)** Basis system. **(7 Marks)**

*Bloom's Level: Understand + Apply (CO 2 & CO 3)*

---

### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks)

**Hole Basis System:** A system of fits in which the **hole is taken as the basic size**, with the **lower deviation of the hole fixed at zero (H-designation)**. Different fits are obtained by varying the shaft tolerance zone.

**Shaft Basis System:** A system of fits in which the **shaft is taken as the basic size**, with the **upper deviation of the shaft fixed at zero (h-designation)**. Different fits are obtained by varying the hole tolerance zone.

**Four Advantages of Hole Basis System:**

1. **Lower Tooling Cost:** Standard drills, reamers, and broaches are manufactured to produce H-grade holes; no special tools are needed.
2. **Easier Inspection:** Go/No-Go gauges are simpler to manufacture since the hole's lower limit is fixed at zero.
3. **Recommended by ISO 286:** Globally preferred standard, ensuring interchangeability of parts across manufacturers.
4. **Reduced Tool Wear:** The hole always has a positive tolerance (above the basic size), so cutting tools are not subjected to under-size cutting.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Hole Limits**
- Max Hole = $50 + 0.039 = 50.039$ mm
- Min Hole = $50 + 0.020 = 50.020$ mm

**Step 2 — Shaft Limits**
- Max Shaft = $50 + 0.017 = 50.017$ mm
- Min Shaft = $50 + 0.002 = 50.002$ mm

**Step 3 — Allowance**

$$\text{Allowance} = 50.020 - 50.017 = +0.003 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Clearance**

$$C_{max} = 50.039 - 50.002 = 0.037 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Minimum Clearance**

$$C_{min} = 50.020 - 50.017 = 0.003 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Type of Fit**
Since $C_{min} = +0.003$ mm (positive) and $C_{max} = +0.037$ mm (positive), this is a **Clearance Fit**.

**Step 7 — Basis System**
Since the **lower deviation of the hole = +0.020 mm** (not zero), and the **upper deviation of the shaft = +0.017 mm** (not zero), strictly speaking this is **not a pure Hole Basis or Shaft Basis system**. It is an **arbitrary (mixed) system**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often mistakenly label this as "Hole Basis" because they see "H" intuitively. **Always check the lower deviation of the hole numerically** — if it is **not zero**, it is **not** a Hole Basis system. This is a common trap in KTU numericals.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this checklist during the last 30 minutes before the KTU exam for high-yield revision.

- **Allowance** = Min Hole − Max Shaft (can be +, −, or 0).
- **Tolerance** = Max Limit − Min Limit (always positive).
- **Hole Basis System:** Lower deviation of **hole** = 0 → H-designation.
- **Shaft Basis System:** Upper deviation of **shaft** = 0 → h-designation.
- **ISO 286 prefers Hole Basis System** globally.
- **Three fit types:** Clearance (always positive gap), Interference (always negative gap), Transition (sign depends on actual manufactured size).
- **Clearance Fit** formula: $C_{max} = \text{Max Hole} - \text{Min Shaft}$; $C_{min} = \text{Min Hole} - \text{Max Shaft}$.
- **Interference Fit** formula: $I_{max} = \text{Min Hole} - \text{Max Shaft}$ (magnitude).
- Always **draw the tolerance zone diagram** — it is worth ~2 marks even with minor numerical errors.
- Always **state the sign** explicitly for the allowance value (+ for clearance, − for interference).
- Identify the **basis system by checking deviations numerically**, not by intuition from H/h symbols alone.
- Industrial applications: Hole Basis → machined components, drilled holes. Shaft Basis → standard shafts, motor shafts, bearing journals.
- For KTU exam: memorize the **sign conventions table** from Section 2.3 — it is the single most-tested concept.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System of Limits and Fits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** System of Limits and Fits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to the System of Limits and Fits

The **System of Limits and Fits** is an internationally standardized engineering convention (governed by **ISO 286** and adopted by **Bureau of Indian Standards IS 919** in India) that defines the permissible variations in the dimensions of mating mechanical parts. It is a cornerstone of **interchangeability** in mass production, allowing any randomly selected part from a batch to assemble correctly with its mating counterpart without the need for selective fitting or hand-fitting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The System of Limits and Fits is a standardized set of rules governing the relationships between the sizes of mating parts, ensuring interchangeability in manufacturing and assembly operations.

## 1.2 Fundamental Terminology (As per KTU)

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Size** | — | A numerical value expressed in a specific unit (mm) of a length |
| **Basic Size** | D | The theoretical exact size from which limits are derived |
| **Actual Size** | — | The measured size of the finished part |
| **Maximum Size** | — | The greatest permissible size of a part |
| **Minimum Size** | — | The smallest permissible size of a part |
| **Upper Deviation** | ES (Hole) / es (Shaft) | Algebraic difference between max size and basic size |
| **Lower Deviation** | EI (Hole) / ei (Shaft) | Algebraic difference between min size and basic size |
| **Tolerance** | T | Difference between max and min size |
| **Zero Line** | — | The line corresponding to the basic size (deviation = 0) |
| **Tolerance Zone** | — | The region between max and min limits on a deviation diagram |

## 1.3 The Intuitive Analogy — "The Door and Frame" 🚪

Imagine a door (the **shaft**) and its frame (the **hole**):

- If you make the door **exactly** the same size as the frame opening, it will either stick or rattle — there is **no room for manufacturing error**.
- Instead, the factory says: *"We will make the door between **29.95 mm** and **30.05 mm** thick"* and *"The frame opening will be between **30.00 mm** and **30.10 mm**."*
- Now there is a **guaranteed gap** of at least **0 mm** and at most **0.15 mm** — the door always fits, even with manufacturing variation.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the essence of the **System of Limits and Fits**: instead of demanding impossible perfection, we define a **permissible range** (limits) for every dimension and standardize the **relationship** (fit) between mating parts.

## 1.4 Why Do We Need This System?

| Engineering Problem | How Limits & Fits Solves It |
|---------------------|----------------------------|
| Parts from different machines/batches don't match | Standardized tolerances ensure **interchangeability** |
| High precision is expensive and slow | Selectable tolerance grades (IT5–IT18) match **precision to cost** |
| Looseness vs. tightness is unclear in design | **Standardized fits** (clearance, transition, interference) define behavior |
| International trade in components | ISO system is recognized **worldwide** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Two Pillars of the ISO System:**
> 1. **Standard Tolerance Grades (IT grades)** — How much variation is allowed?
> 2. **Fundamental Deviations (Position of Tolerance Zone)** — Where is the variation placed relative to the basic size?
>
> Together, they create a **two-dimensional matrix** (tolerance grade × tolerance position) for both holes and shafts, giving every fit a unique ISO designation like **H7/g6**, **H7/k6**, or **H7/p6**.

## 1.5 Definition of a "Fit" — The Mating Relationship

A **Fit** is the relationship between two mating parts (a hole and a shaft) with respect to their dimensional **clearance** or **interference** before assembly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Categories of Fit:**
> 1. **Clearance Fit (Loose Fit):** Shaft is *always smaller* than hole → parts slide/assemble freely.
> 2. **Interference Fit (Force Fit):** Shaft is *always larger* than hole → parts must be pressed together.
> 3. **Transition Fit:** Shaft may be smaller OR larger than hole → very precise alignment, either slides or requires light force.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tolerance — The Heart of the System

**Tolerance** is the **total permissible variation** in a single dimension. It is the difference between the maximum and minimum size limits.

### Key Logic Step-by-Step:

1. **Designer specifies Basic Size (D):** The nominal required dimension (e.g., 50 mm).
2. **Manufacturing realities demand variation:** Tools wear, machines vibrate, temperatures fluctuate.
3. **Engineer allocates a tolerance band (T):** A range above and/or below the basic size within which the part is acceptable.
4. **Upper Limit = Basic Size + Upper Deviation**
5. **Lower Limit = Basic Size + Lower Deviation**
6. **Tolerance = Upper Limit − Lower Limit = Upper Deviation − Lower Deviation**

$$T = D_{max} - D_{min} = ES - EI = es - ei$$

## 2.2 Allowance — The Mating Story

While **tolerance** describes a single part, **allowance** describes the **relationship between two mating parts**.

| Fit Type | Allowance Definition | Expression |
|----------|---------------------|------------|
| **Clearance Fit** | Minimum clearance | $C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max} = EI_{hole} - es_{shaft}$ |
| | Maximum clearance | $C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min} = ES_{hole} - ei_{shaft}$ |
| **Interference Fit** | Minimum interference | $I_{min} = S_{min} - H_{max} = ei_{shaft} - ES_{hole}$ |
| | Maximum interference | $I_{max} = S_{max} - H_{min} = es_{shaft} - EI_{hole}$ |
| **Transition Fit** | Range | $T_{fit} = ES_{hole} - ei_{shaft}$ (positive side), $T_{fit} = ei_{shaft} - EI_{hole}$ (negative side) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Memory Trick:**
> - **Hole = House (bigger)**, **Shaft = Snake (smaller in clearance fits)**
> - Clearance = Hole − Shaft
> - Interference = Shaft − Hole

## 2.3 The Standard Tolerance Unit (i)

The foundation of the entire ISO tolerance system is the **Standard Tolerance Unit (i)**, which is a function of the basic size **D** (in mm). It captures the physical reality that **larger dimensions tolerate larger absolute variations** but smaller *relative* variations.

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad (\text{microns, when D is in mm})$$

### Why This Formula?

- The term **$0.45 \cdot D^{1/3}$** scales with diameter to reflect the difficulty of holding tight tolerances on small parts.
- The term **$0.001 \cdot D$** is a linear correction for very large diameters (above ~500 mm) where surface finish, thermal expansion, and measurement uncertainty grow linearly.

## 2.4 Tolerance Grades (IT Grades) — The Precision Selector

The ISO system defines **20 tolerance grades**: **IT01, IT0, IT1, IT2, ... IT18**. The higher the IT number, the **looser** (larger) the tolerance.

| Tolerance Grade | Multiplier of i | Typical Application |
|:---------------:|:---------------:|---------------------|
| **IT01** | 0.3 + 0.008·D | Gauge blocks, master flats |
| **IT0** | 0.5 + 0.012·D | Precision gauges |
| **IT1** | 0.8 + 0.020·D | High-precision bearings |
| **IT2** to **IT4** | 1.2 to 3 · i | Ball/roller bearings, gauges |
| **IT5** | 7 · i | Precision machine tools, fits requiring high precision |
| **IT6** | 10 · i | Bearings, fine mechanism fits |
| **IT7** | 16 · i | General engineering — most common hole grade |
| **IT8** | 25 · i | General purpose fits |
| **IT9** | 40 · i | Loose running fits, agricultural machinery |
| **IT10** | 64 · i | Loose fits, sheet metal |
| **IT11** | 100 · i | Coarse fits, castings |
| **IT12** | 160 · i | Rough castings |
| **IT13** | 250 · i | Rough forgings |
| **IT14 to IT18** | 400 to 4000 · i | Free cutting, very rough work |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grades IT5 through IT13 use a simple multiplier × i**. The very fine grades (IT01 to IT4) use linear formulas in D because their absolute values are so small that the cube-root scaling is meaningless.

## 2.5 Fundamental Deviation — The Position of the Tolerance Zone

While the IT grade controls the **width** of the tolerance zone, the **fundamental deviation** controls the **position** of the tolerance zone relative to the zero line (basic size).

### For Shafts (lowercase letters):

| Shaft Symbol | Zone Position | Fundamental Deviation (Lower ei for a–h, Upper es for j–zc) |
|--------------|---------------|-------------------------------------------------------------|
| **a, b, c, d, e, f, g** | Below zero line | $ei < 0$, $es = ei + IT_n$ |
| **h** | On zero line | $ei = 0$ (always!), $es = 0 + IT_n$ |
| **j, k, m, n, p, q, r, s, t, u, v, x, y, z, za, zb, zc** | Above zero line | $es > 0$, $ei = es - IT_n$ |

### For Holes (uppercase letters):

- **Holes A through H**: Zone is **above** zero line, **EI > 0**, $ES = EI + IT_n$
- **Hole H**: $EI = 0$ (the famous reference hole), $ES = 0 + IT_n$
- **Holes J through ZC**: Zone is **below** zero line, **ES < 0**

> [!NOTE]
> **The Reference Hole (H)** and **The Reference Shaft (h)** are the cornerstones of the two basis systems. Notice that for both, the deviation closest to the zero line is exactly **0**.

## 2.6 The Two Basis Systems

| System | Reference Part | Practical Use |
|--------|----------------|---------------|
| **Hole Basis System** | Hole is fixed as **H** (the reference hole). Various shafts (a–zc) are selected to achieve the desired fit. | **Most widely used** (≈ 80% of industrial applications). Easier to manufacture standard drills, reamers, and broaches. |
| **Shaft Basis System** | Shaft is fixed as **h** (the reference shaft). Various holes (A–ZC) are selected. | Used when a standard shaft (e.g., a transmission shaft with keyways) must accept bearings or collars of varying fits. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hole Basis Dominates:** It is far cheaper to vary a shaft's diameter (by turning) than to vary a hole's diameter (by reaming, broaching, or grinding). The hole is held as the standard.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Formula | Meaning |
|:-:|---------|---------|
| 1 | $T = D_{max} - D_{min}$ | Tolerance (single part) |
| 2 | $T = ES - EI = es - ei$ | Tolerance in terms of deviations |
| 3 | $i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D \text{ (μm)}$ | Standard tolerance unit |
| 4 | $IT_n = k \cdot i$ | Tolerance of grade n (k = 7, 10, 16, 25, 40, ...) |
| 5 | $C_{min} = EI_{hole} - es_{shaft}$ | Minimum clearance |
| 6 | $C_{max} = ES_{hole} - ei_{shaft}$ | Maximum clearance |
| 7 | $I_{min} = ei_{shaft} - ES_{hole}$ | Minimum interference |
| 8 | $I_{max} = es_{shaft} - EI_{hole}$ | Maximum interference |
| 9 | $T_{fit} = ES_{hole} - ei_{shaft}$ (clearance side) | Total tolerance of fit |
| 10 | $T_{fit} = T_{hole} + T_{shaft}$ | Total tolerance of fit (always true) |
| 11 | For H-hole: $EI = 0$, $ES = +IT_n$ | Reference hole deviation |
| 12 | For h-shaft: $es = 0$, $ei = -IT_n$ | Reference shaft deviation |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** A piston (shaft) and cylinder (hole) require a **clearance fit** (e.g., H7/g6) so that oil film forms between them.
- **Aerospace:** Turbine blade roots require **interference fits** (H7/u6) to lock blades to the rotor disk under extreme centrifugal loads.
- **Tooling:** Drill bushings in jigs use **H7/n6** (transition fit) for easy assembly but accurate location.
- **Bearings:** Rolling element bearings are universally mounted with **H7/k6** (transition) on shafts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Standard Tolerance Unit (i)

The standard tolerance unit **i** is derived empirically from the observation that small parts are harder to manufacture to tight absolute tolerances than large parts, but proportionally easier for large parts.

### Step 1: Empirical Foundation

ISO studies measured the **smallest achievable tolerance** on a population of parts for various diameters. They found that the relationship between tolerance and diameter roughly follows a cube-root law for small-to-medium sizes.

### Step 2: Cube-Root Term

For diameters up to about **500 mm**, the smallest tolerance scales as:

$$i_{cubic} \propto D^{1/3}$$

The constant **0.45** was chosen so that for D in **mm**, **i** comes out in **microns**:

$$i_{cubic} = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} \quad (\text{μm})$$

### Step 3: Linear Correction for Large D

For diameters above 500 mm, measurement and process errors grow linearly with size, so an additional term is added:

$$i_{linear} = 0.001 \cdot D \quad (\text{μm})$$

### Step 4: Combined Expression

Adding the two terms yields the standard formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= i_{cubic} + i_{linear} \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad (\text{μm})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification with D = 50 mm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{50} + 0.001 \cdot 50 \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot 3.6840 + 0.050 \\
i &= 1.6578 + 0.050 \\
i &= 1.708 \text{ μm}
\end{aligned}
$$

For practical use, this is rounded to standard published values from the ISO 286 lookup tables.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Five KTU-Style Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard ISO 286 Tolerance Values used below** (in μm, extracted from standard tables):
>
> | D (mm) | i (μm) | IT6 | IT7 | IT8 |
> |:------:|:------:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
> | 30 | 1.43 | 14 | 23 | 36 |
> | 40 | 1.56 | 16 | 25 | 39 |
> | 50 | 1.71 | 17 | 27 | 43 |
> | 80 | 2.02 | 20 | 32 | 50 |
> | 100 | 2.17 | 22 | 35 | 54 |

---

### **Example 1: A Clearance Fit (50 H7 / h6) — The Most Common Fit in Industry**

**Given:**
- Basic size: D = 50 mm
- Hole designation: **H7** (Reference hole, hole basis)
- Shaft designation: **h6** (Reference shaft)

**Step 1: Identify Hole H7 Limits**

For the H-hole, lower deviation **EI = 0** (always). Upper deviation is the IT7 tolerance value.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI_{hole} &= 0 \\
ES_{hole} &= 0 + IT7 = 0 + 27 = +27 \text{ μm} = +0.027 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **H7 hole** has limits: **50.000 mm** to **50.027 mm**.

**Step 2: Identify Shaft h6 Limits**

For the h-shaft, upper deviation **es = 0** (always). Lower deviation is −IT6.

$$
\begin{aligned}
es_{shaft} &= 0 \\
ei_{shaft} &= 0 - IT6 = 0 - 17 = -17 \text{ μm} = -0.017 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the **h6 shaft** has limits: **49.983 mm** to **50.000 mm**.

**Step 3: Compute Clearances**

Minimum clearance (worst case tightest assembly — largest shaft in smallest hole):

$$
C_{min} = EI_{hole} - es_{shaft} = 0 - 0 = 0 \text{ μm} = 0.000 \text{ mm}
$$

Maximum clearance (most loose assembly — smallest shaft in largest hole):

$$
C_{max} = ES_{hole} - ei_{shaft} = 0.027 - (-0.017) = 0.044 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Classify the Fit**

Since **$C_{min} \geq 0$** and **$C_{max} > 0$**, the fit is a **CLEARANCE FIT** (specifically a **sliding fit** — shaft can slide into hole with hand pressure).

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Hole limits | 50.000 to 50.027 mm |
| Shaft limits | 49.983 to 50.000 mm |
| Min clearance | 0 μm |
| Max clearance | 44 μm |
| Fit type | **Clearance (sliding)** |

---

### **Example 2: A Transition Fit (50 H7 / k6) — Precision Location**

**Given:**
- Basic size: D = 50 mm
- Hole: **H7** → EI = 0, ES = +0.027 mm
- Shaft: **k6** → For D = 50 mm, fundamental deviation table gives **es = +0.018 mm** (shaft tolerance zone straddles zero, slight preference for positive)

**Step 1: Compute Shaft Limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
es_{shaft} &= +18 \text{ μm} = +0.018 \text{ mm} \\
ei_{shaft} &= es_{shaft} - IT6 = 0.018 - 0.017 = +0.001 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Interferences & Clearances**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{min} &= ei_{shaft} - ES_{hole} = 0.001 - 0.027 = -0.026 \text{ mm (clearance)} \\
I_{max} &= es_{shaft} - EI_{hole} = 0.018 - 0.000 = +0.018 \text{ mm (interference)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Classify the Fit**

Since the value can be **either clearance or interference** depending on the specific pair of parts drawn from the batch, this is a **TRANSITION FIT** (specifically, an **easy sliding fit** requiring a light tap with a hammer for assembly).

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Hole limits | 50.000 to 50.027 mm |
| Shaft limits | 50.001 to 50.018 mm |
| Possible clearance | up to 26 μm |
| Possible interference | up to 18 μm |
| Fit type | **Transition** |

---

### **Example 3: An Interference Fit (50 H7 / p6) — Force Fit**

**Given:**
- Basic size: D = 50 mm
- Hole: **H7** → EI = 0, ES = +0.027 mm
- Shaft: **p6** → For D = 50 mm, fundamental deviation gives **es = +0.042 mm**

**Step 1: Compute Shaft Limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
es_{shaft} &= +0.042 \text{ mm} \\
ei_{shaft} &= es_{shaft} - IT6 = 0.042 - 0.017 = +0.025 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Interferences**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{min} &= ei_{shaft} - ES_{hole} = 0.025 - 0.027 = -0.002 \text{ mm (slight clearance)} \\
I_{max} &= es_{shaft} - EI_{hole} = 0.042 - 0.000 = +0.042 \text{ mm (interference)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the **p6 shaft** is always larger than the H7 hole (slight clearance in the calculation indicates a borderline case that ISO categorizes as an interference fit). The p6 shaft is always larger than the H7 hole because ei = +25 μm > ES = +27 μm ... wait, here 25 < 27, so technically a tiny clearance exists. This is actually a **light interference fit** where some pairs will slide and others will need pressing.

**Step 3: Classify**

The fit is a **forced/light interference fit** — requires press or thermal expansion for assembly.

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Hole limits | 50.000 to 50.027 mm |
| Shaft limits | 50.025 to 50.042 mm |
| Maximum clearance | 2 μm (rare case) |
| Maximum interference | 42 μm |
| Fit type | **Interference (drive fit)** |

---

### **Example 4: Standard Tolerance Calculation for D = 80 mm, IT8**

**Given:** D = 80 mm, grade IT8

**Step 1: Calculate i**

$$
i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{80} + 0.001 \cdot 80
$$

$$
i = 0.45 \cdot 4.3089 + 0.080 = 1.9390 + 0.080 = 2.019 \text{ μm}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate IT8**

$$
IT8 = 25 \cdot i = 25 \cdot 2.019 = 50.48 \text{ μm} \approx 50 \text{ μm}
$$

This matches the standard ISO 286 published value of **50 μm** for D = 80 mm, IT8. ✅

---

### **Example 5: Allowance and Total Fit Tolerance for 80 H7 / g6**

**Given:** D = 80 mm, Hole H7 (EI = 0, ES = +32 μm), Shaft g6 (es = −12 μm, ei = −32 μm)

> *Note: g6 for D = 80 mm uses table values: es = −12 μm, IT6 = 20 μm, so ei = −32 μm.*

**Step 1: Min Clearance**

$$
C_{min} = EI_{hole} - es_{shaft} = 0 - (-12) = +12 \text{ μm}
$$

**Step 2: Max Clearance**

$$
C_{max} = ES_{hole} - ei_{shaft} = +32 - (-32) = +64 \text{ μm}
$$

**Step 3: Total Fit Tolerance (Verification)**

$$
T_{fit} = T_{hole} + T_{shaft} = IT7 + IT6 = 32 + 20 = 52 \text{ μm}
$$

$$
T_{fit} = C_{max} - C_{min} = 64 - 12 = 52 \text{ μm} \quad ✅
$$

**Step 4: Classify**

Both $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$ are positive → **CLEARANCE FIT** (a **precision sliding fit** used for high-speed spindles, gauges, etc.).

## 3.3 Summary Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right IT Grade

| Application | Typical IT Grade | Fit Type |
|-------------|:----------------:|----------|
| Gauge blocks, master flats | IT01 – IT2 | Reference |
| Ball & roller bearings | IT5 – IT6 | Transition/Light interference |
| High-precision machine tool spindles | IT5 – IT6 | Light clearance |
| General machine assembly | IT7 – IT8 | Clearance / Transition |
| Agricultural machinery, rough drives | IT9 – IT11 | Clearance |
| Cast iron parts, unmachined | IT12 – IT14 | Loose |

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISO System of Limits and Fits — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ISO System of Limits and Fits] --> B[Standard Tolerance Grades]
    A --> C[Fundamental Deviations]
    A --> D[Two Basis Systems]
    A --> E[Three Categories of Fits]

    B --> B1[IT01 IT0 IT1 IT2 IT3 IT4]
    B --> B2[IT5 IT6 IT7 IT8 IT9]
    B --> B3[IT10 IT11 IT12 IT13 IT14 IT15 IT16 IT17 IT18]
    B --> BNote[Tolerance increases as IT number increases]
    BNote --> BNoteA[Finer precision to IT01]
    BNote --> BNoteB[Looser tolerance to IT18]

    C --> C1[Shaft Designations]
    C --> C2[Hole Designations]
    C1 --> C1A[a b c d e f g h]
    C1 --> C1B[j k m n p q r s t u v x y z za zb zc]
    C1A --> C1A1[a to h: Zone below zero line]
    C1A --> C1A2[h is reference shaft: es equals zero]
    C1B --> C1B1[j to zc: Zone above zero line]
    C2 --> C2A[A B C D E F G H]
    C2 --> C2B[J K M N P Q R S T U V X Y Z ZA ZB ZC]
    C2A --> C2A1[A to H: Zone above zero line]
    C2A --> C2A2[H is reference hole: EI equals zero]
    C2B --> C2B1[J to ZC: Zone below zero line]

    D --> D1[Hole Basis System]
    D --> D2[Shaft Basis System]
    D1 --> D1A[Reference hole is H]
    D1 --> D1B[Various shafts selected: g6 k6 p6]
    D1 --> D1C[Used in 80 percent of applications]
    D2 --> D2A[Reference shaft is h]
    D2 --> D2B[Various holes selected: G7 K7 P7]
    D2 --> D2C[Used for standard shafts with keyways]

    E --> E1[Clearance Fit]
    E --> E2[Transition Fit]
    E --> E3[Interference Fit]
    E1 --> E1A[Shaft always smaller than hole]
    E1 --> E1B[Cmin positive Cmax positive]
    E1 --> E1C[Examples: H7 g6 H7 h6]
    E2 --> E2A[May be clearance or interference]
    E2 --> E2B[Cmin negative Cmax positive]
    E2 --> E2C[Examples: H7 k6 H7 n6]
    E3 --> E3A[Shaft always larger than hole]
    E3 --> E3B[Imin positive Imax positive]
    E3 --> E3C[Examples: H7 p6 H7 s6]
```

## 4.2 Fit Analysis Flowchart — Decision Tree for Classifying Any Fit

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given Hole and Shaft Designations] --> Step1[Extract EI ES from Hole]
    Start --> Step2[Extract ei es from Shaft]
    Step1 --> HoleLimits[Hole Limits: D plus EI to D plus ES]
    Step2 --> ShaftLimits[Shaft Limits: D plus ei to D plus es]
    HoleLimits --> CompareEIei[Check ei minus ES sign]
    ShaftLimits --> CompareEIei
    CompareEIei --> Sign1{Is ei minus ES positive}
    Sign1 --> Sign1Yes[Interference possible]
    Sign1 --> Sign1No[No interference possible]
    Sign1Yes --> ComputeImax[Compute Imax equals es minus EI]
    Sign1No --> CompareESei[Check ES minus ei sign]
    ComputeImax --> CompareESei
    CompareESei --> Sign2{Is ES minus ei positive}
    Sign2 --> Sign2Yes[Clearance possible]
    Sign2 --> Sign2No[No clearance possible]
    Sign2Yes --> ComputeCmax[Compute Cmax equals ES minus ei]
    Sign2No --> ClassifyPure[Pure Interference Fit]
    ComputeCmax --> FinalCheck{Both conditions true}
    Sign1No --> FinalCheck
    FinalCheck --> FinalYes[TRANSITION FIT]
    FinalCheck --> FinalNo[CLEARANCE FIT]
    ComputeImax --> ImaxCheck{Is Imax greater than zero}
    ImaxCheck --> ImaxYes[Pure or borderline interference]
    ImaxCheck --> ImaxNo[Pure Clearance Fit]
```

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with neat sketches:**
**(a) Basic size, (b) Actual size, (c) Maximum and minimum sizes, (d) Tolerance, (e) Allowance.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Basic Size:** The theoretical or nominal size from which limits are derived, and to which all deviations are referenced. Represented on a deviation diagram as the **zero line**. Example: D = 50 mm.

**(b) Actual Size:** The size of a finished part as measured by actual measurement (micrometer, vernier, CMM). It must lie between the limits for the part to be acceptable.

**(c) Maximum and Minimum Sizes:** These are the two extreme permissible dimensions. For a hole:
- $D_{max} = D + ES$
- $D_{min} = D + EI$

For a shaft:
- $d_{max} = d + es$
- $d_{min} = d + ei$

**(d) Tolerance:** The total permissible variation in dimension, equal to the difference between the maximum and minimum sizes.

$$T = D_{max} - D_{min} = ES - EI = es - ei \quad \text{(in mm or μm)}$$

**(e) Allowance:** The intentional difference between the maximum material conditions of mating parts. It defines the **fit** (clearance or interference).

> For clearance fit: $Allowance = C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max}$
> For interference fit: $Allowance = I_{min} = S_{min} - H_{max}$

**[3 Marks — One mark for each pair of definitions]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a fit? Classify different types of fits with neat sketches.**

**Model Answer:**

A **fit** is the relationship between two mating parts (a hole and a shaft) with respect to the **ease or tightness** of assembly. It is characterized by the **clearance or interference** that results from the chosen limits.

**Classification of Fits:**

**1. Clearance Fit (Loose/Sliding Fit):**
- Shaft is **always smaller** than the hole.
- $C_{min} \geq 0$ and $C_{max} > 0$
- Parts assemble by **hand**; may or may not slide freely.
- Example: **H7/g6**, **H7/h6** — used for sliding gears, pulleys.

**2. Interference Fit (Force/Shrink Fit):**
- Shaft is **always larger** than the hole.
- $I_{min} > 0$ and $I_{max} > 0$
- Parts require **force, heating of the outer part, or cooling of the inner part** for assembly.
- Example: **H7/p6**, **H7/s6** — used for permanent couplings, bushings, gear teeth on shafts.

**3. Transition Fit:**
- The shaft may be either smaller or larger than the hole depending on the specific pair drawn.
- $C_{max} > 0$ but $I_{max} > 0$
- Parts require a **light hammer tap or hand pressure** for assembly.
- Example: **H7/k6**, **H7/n6** — used for precision location, bearings.

**[3 Marks — 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for classification + 1 mark for examples]**

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (1) forgetting the sign convention of deviations (deviation is *algebraic*, always carry the + or −), (2) confusing the H7 hole limits with the h6 shaft limits (the H7 has EI = 0, h6 has es = 0 — they are NOT the same), (3) using wrong units (microns vs mm — keep consistent), and (4) failing to state the **fit type** explicitly at the end. Always end with "**∴ This is a ___ fit**" to claim full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **ISO System of Limits and Fits** with reference to:
**(i) Standard tolerance grades and tolerance unit**
**(ii) Fundamental deviations for holes and shafts**
**(iii) Hole basis and shaft basis systems.** **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hole and shaft assembly has a basic size of **80 mm**. The hole is designated as **H8** and the shaft as **f7**.
- Determine the limits of the hole and shaft.
- Calculate the **minimum and maximum clearance**.
- State the **type of fit**. **(7 marks)**

**Given Data (from ISO 286 tables for D = 80 mm):**
- IT8 = 50 μm, IT7 = 32 μm
- For shaft **f7**: fundamental deviation **es = −30 μm** (zone below zero line for f-type)

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**(i) Standard Tolerance Grades:**
The ISO system defines 20 grades: IT01, IT0, IT1, IT2, ... IT18. As the IT number increases, the tolerance becomes larger (looser). For grades IT5 to IT18, the tolerance is:

$$IT_n = k \cdot i$$

where **i** is the standard tolerance unit and **k** is a multiplier. The standard tolerance unit captures the dependence of machinability on diameter:

$$i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad (\text{μm, D in mm})$$

**Multiplier table (key values):**
IT5: 7·i | IT6: 10·i | IT7: 16·i | IT8: 25·i | IT9: 40·i | IT10: 64·i | IT11: 100·i | IT12: 160·i | IT13: 250·i

The very fine grades (IT01 to IT4) use linear formulas in D because their absolute values are sub-micron.

**(ii) Fundamental Deviations:**
The fundamental deviation is the deviation closest to the zero line. It determines the **position** of the tolerance zone.

**For shafts (lowercase):**
- **a to h:** Tolerance zone is **below** the zero line. $ei$ is the negative fundamental deviation. $es = ei + IT_n$. For **h**: $es = 0$ (reference shaft).
- **j to zc:** Tolerance zone is **above** the zero line. $es$ is the positive fundamental deviation. $ei = es - IT_n$.

**For holes (uppercase):**
- **A to H:** Zone is **above** the zero line. $EI$ is the positive fundamental deviation. $ES = EI + IT_n$. For **H**: $EI = 0$ (reference hole).
- **J to ZC:** Zone is **below** the zero line. $ES$ is the negative fundamental deviation. $EI = ES - IT_n$.

**(iii) Basis Systems:**

- **Hole Basis System:** The hole is held as the **reference hole H**. Different shafts (a–zc) are chosen to create different fits. Used in ~80% of industrial applications because holes are more expensive to vary (requires reaming, broaching, grinding).
- **Shaft Basis System:** The shaft is held as the **reference shaft h**. Different holes (A–ZC) are chosen. Used when a standard shaft (e.g., a transmission shaft with keyways) must mate with parts of varying fits.

**[7 marks distributed: (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks, (iii) 2 marks]**

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Hole H8 Limits**

For the H-hole, $EI = 0$ (always). For D = 80 mm, $IT8 = 50 \text{ μm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EI_{hole} &= 0 \text{ μm} = 0.000 \text{ mm} \\
ES_{hole} &= 0 + IT8 = +50 \text{ μm} = +0.050 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore$ **Hole H8 limits: 80.000 mm to 80.050 mm**

**Step 2: Shaft f7 Limits**

For an f-shaft, the zone is below the zero line. $es = -30 \text{ μm} = -0.030 \text{ mm}$. For D = 80 mm, $IT7 = 32 \text{ μm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
es_{shaft} &= -30 \text{ μm} = -0.030 \text{ mm} \\
ei_{shaft} &= es_{shaft} - IT7 = -0.030 - 0.032 = -0.062 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore$ **Shaft f7 limits: 79.938 mm to 79.970 mm**

**Step 3: Minimum Clearance**

$$
C_{min} = EI_{hole} - es_{shaft} = 0.000 - (-0.030) = +0.030 \text{ mm} = +30 \text{ μm}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Clearance**

$$
C_{max} = ES_{hole} - ei_{shaft} = 0.050 - (-0.062) = +0.112 \text{ mm} = +112 \text{ μm}
$$

**Step 5: Verify Total Fit Tolerance**

$$
T_{fit} = T_{hole} + T_{shaft} = 50 + 32 = 82 \text{ μm}
$$

$$
T_{fit} = C_{max} - C_{min} = 112 - 30 = 82 \text{ μm} \quad ✅
$$

**Step 6: Classify the Fit**

Since both $C_{min} = +30 \text{ μm} > 0$ and $C_{max} = +112 \text{ μm} > 0$, the fit is a **CLEARANCE FIT**. Specifically, this corresponds to a **Loose Running Fit** (H8/f7) per ISO 1829 — used for high-speed rotating shafts, large temperature variations, and agricultural machinery.

**Result Summary Table:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Basic size | 80 mm |
| Hole H8 | 80.000 to 80.050 mm |
| Shaft f7 | 79.938 to 79.970 mm |
| Min clearance | 30 μm |
| Max clearance | 112 μm |
| **Fit type** | **Clearance (loose running)** |

**[7 marks: 2 marks hole + 2 marks shaft + 2 marks clearances + 1 mark fit classification]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Differentiate clearly between **Hole Basis System** and **Shaft Basis System** of fits. State which is preferred in industry and justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft of basic diameter **40 mm** is to be manufactured with a designation **40 m6**. Using the standard tolerance data:
- For D = 40 mm: $i = 1.56 \text{ μm}$, $IT6 = 16 \text{ μm}$, fundamental deviation for m6: $es = +25 \text{ μm}$.

Calculate the limits, tolerance, and state whether this shaft can be assembled with a **40 H7 hole** (use $IT7 = 25 \text{ μm}$). Identify the type of fit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

| Feature | Hole Basis System | Shaft Basis System |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Reference element | **Hole H** (EI = 0) | **Shaft h** (es = 0) |
| Variable element | Shaft designation varies (a, b, c, ..., zc) | Hole designation varies (A, B, C, ..., ZC) |
| Standard tooling | Standard drills, reamers, broaches for **H** | Standard shaft blanks (turned, ground) for **h** |
| Designation example | 50 H7/g6 | 50 G7/h6 |
| Industrial use | ~80% of applications | ~20% of applications (special cases) |

**Preferred System:** **Hole Basis System** is preferred in industry.

**Justification:**
1. **Cost of manufacturing:** Varying a hole's diameter requires reaming, broaching, or grinding — all expensive and tool-specific. Varying a shaft's diameter requires only turning, which is faster and cheaper.
2. **Tool standardization:** Standard drills, reamers, and broaches are produced in H-grade tolerance. Using hole basis means these tools produce the reference hole directly.
3. **Inspection simplicity:** Gauges (plug gauges, ring gauges) are made in H-tolerance as the master.
4. **Drawing clarity:** Most international engineering drawings use the hole basis system by default.

**Shaft basis is used when:** A standard shaft (e.g., a long transmission shaft with multiple bearings) needs different fits at different locations — one bearing may be a clearance fit, another may be a transition fit.

**[7 marks: 3 marks table + 2 marks justification + 2 marks examples]**

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1: Compute Shaft m6 Limits**

Given: $es = +25 \text{ μm} = +0.025 \text{ mm}$ and $IT6 = 16 \text{ μm} = 0.016 \text{ mm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
es_{shaft} &= +0.025 \text{ mm} \\
ei_{shaft} &= es - IT6 = 0.025 - 0.016 = +0.009 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore$ **Shaft m6 limits: 40.009 mm to 40.025 mm**

**Tolerance of shaft:**

$$
T_{shaft} = es - ei = 0.025 - 0.009 = 0.016 \text{ mm} = 16 \text{ μm} \quad ✅
$$

**Step 2: Compute Hole H7 Limits**

For H-hole: $EI = 0$, $ES = +IT7 = +0.025 \text{ mm}$.

$\therefore$ **Hole H7 limits: 40.000 mm to 40.025 mm**

**Step 3: Compute Clearance / Interference**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= ES_{hole} - ei_{shaft} = 0.025 - 0.009 = +0.016 \text{ mm (clearance)} \\
I_{max} &= es_{shaft} - EI_{hole} = 0.025 - 0.000 = +0.025 \text{ mm (interference)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify**

$$
T_{fit} = T_{hole} + T_{shaft} = 25 + 16 = 41 \text{ μm}
$$

$$
T_{fit} = I_{max} + C_{max} = 25 + 16 = 41 \text{ μm} \quad ✅
$$

**Step 5: Classify the Fit**

Since the relationship can be either **clearance (up to 16 μm)** or **interference (up to 25 μm)** depending on the specific pair of parts drawn from the batch, this is a **TRANSITION FIT** — specifically, a **tight fit (H7/m6)** requiring a light force (rubber mallet) for assembly. Used for **gear shafts, pulleys, and couplings** requiring accurate location but easy disassembly.

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Basic size | 40 mm |
| Hole H7 | 40.000 to 40.025 mm |
| Shaft m6 | 40.009 to 40.025 mm |
| Max clearance | 16 μm |
| Max interference | 25 μm |
| **Fit type** | **Transition (tight)** |

**[7 marks: 2 marks shaft + 2 marks hole + 2 marks fit analysis + 1 mark classification]**

---

## 🔥 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize This in 5 Minutes**

### ✅ The 5 Must-Know Definitions
- **Limit:** Max/min permissible size of a single part.
- **Tolerance:** Total allowed variation (Upper Limit − Lower Limit).
- **Fit:** Relationship of mating hole and shaft (clearance / transition / interference).
- **Deviation:** Algebraic difference from basic size. **Upper deviation** (ES/es) and **lower deviation** (EI/ei).
- **Allowance:** Tightest clearance or loosest interference between mating parts.

### ✅ The 3 Golden Formulas
- $T = D_{max} - D_{min}$ (Tolerance of one part)
- $i = 0.45 \cdot D^{1/3} + 0.001 \cdot D \text{ μm}$ (Standard tolerance unit)
- $IT_n = k \cdot i$ (Tolerance for grade n)

### ✅ The 2 Reference Parts
- **H-hole:** $EI = 0$, $ES = +IT_n$ → The basis of the **Hole Basis System**.
- **h-shaft:** $es = 0$, $ei = -IT_n$ → The basis of the **Shaft Basis System**.

### ✅ The 3 Fit Rules
- **Clearance fit:** $C_{min} \geq 0$ AND $C_{max} > 0$ → H7/g6, H7/h6
- **Interference fit:** $I_{min} > 0$ AND $I_{max} > 0$ → H7/p6, H7/s6
- **Transition fit:** Both $C_{max} > 0$ AND $I_{max} > 0$ → H7/k6, H7/n6, H7/m6

### ✅ The Sign Convention
- **Hole:** Capital letters (D, ES, EI)
- **Shaft:** Lowercase letters (d, es, ei)
- **Algebraic sign is mandatory:** Always write "+" or "−" in front of deviations, even for zero.

### ✅ The Hole Basis System is Preferred
- ~80% of industrial fits
- Cheaper to vary a shaft than a hole
- Standard tooling is designed for H-holes

### ✅ Common IT Grade Choices
- **IT6, IT7:** General machine design (most common)
- **IT8, IT9:** Less critical fits
- **IT5:** High precision (bearings, gauges)
- **IT11, IT12, IT13:** Castings, rough forgings

### ✅ The Order of Solving a Fit Problem
1. Read the designation and identify **D**, **Hole code**, **Shaft code**.
2. Look up **IT grade values** and **fundamental deviations** from the ISO 286 table.
3. Compute hole limits: $D + EI$ to $D + ES$.
4. Compute shaft limits: $D + ei$ to $D + es$.
5. Compute $C_{min}$, $C_{max}$ (or $I_{min}$, $I_{max}$).
6. **Classify** the fit (clearance / transition / interference).
7. **Verify** with $T_{fit} = T_{hole} + T_{shaft}$.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** When in doubt about the fit type, always compute **both** the maximum clearance and the maximum interference. If one is zero, the fit is **pure clearance** or **pure interference**. If both are positive, it is **transition**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Numerical Problems in Metrology (Module 3 Scope)

**Numerical Problems in Metrology** refer to quantitative exercises involving the calculation of tolerances, allowances, fundamental deviations, and upper/lower limits for mating parts (holes and shafts) designed for interchangeable assembly, in compliance with the **Indian Standard (IS 919) / ISO 286** system of limits and fits.

In simple terms, when two parts (like a shaft and a bearing) must fit together with a specific clearance or interference, we use numerical methods to mathematically determine the maximum and acceptable size limits of each component. This ensures that any randomly selected shaft from a batch will mate correctly with any randomly selected hole from its corresponding batch — the principle of **interchangeability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Metrology):** Numerical problems primarily focus on **Limits, Fits, and Tolerances**, including unilateral/bilateral tolerance schemes, hole-basis and shaft-basis fits, calculation of limits from given tolerance grades (IT grades), and allowance/interference problems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are manufacturing **1,00,000 door locks** in a factory. You want any key to open any lock perfectly. If the key is even slightly too thick, it won't go in. If it's too thin, it will wobble. **How do you ensure a perfect fit without testing every key with every lock?**

The answer: Define a **tolerance** (an acceptable range of size) for both the key and the lock, and use numerical calculations to design these ranges. The numerical problems we solve help engineers pre-calculate these ranges on paper **before** manufacturing a single part.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants and Standards:**
> - **Tolerance Unit (i)** for IT grades is calculated in **micrometers (µm)**.
> - Standard reference temperature for measurement: **20 °C**.
> - IS 919 (Indian Standard) aligns with **ISO 286-1 (International Organization for Standardization)**.
> - **1 µm = 0.001 mm**.

### 1.3 Core Terminology for Numerical Problems

- **Hole (H)**: A term used in fits nomenclature to denote the *internal* (enclosing) member of a fit.
- **Shaft (h)**: The *external* (enclosed) member of a fit.
- **Basic Size (Nominal Size, D or d)**: The reference size from which limits are derived. *(Units: mm)*
- **Actual Size**: The measured size of the manufactured part.
- **Limits (High and Low)**:
  - **Maximum limit of size** = Basic size + Upper deviation
  - **Minimum limit of size** = Basic size + Lower deviation
- **Tolerance**: The difference between max and min limits. **Tolerance = High limit − Low limit**.
- **Allowance**: The *minimum* clearance (in clearance fits) or *maximum* interference (in interference fits) between the mating parts.
- **Deviation**: The algebraic difference between a limit and the corresponding basic size.
- **Fundamental Deviation**: The deviation (upper or lower) closest to the basic size. It defines the position of the tolerance zone relative to the zero line.
- **Tolerance Grade (IT01 to IT18)**: Defines the *magnitude* (width) of the tolerance zone.
- **Fit**: The relationship between two mating parts (clearance, transition, or interference).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Background for Solving Numerical Problems

To solve numerical problems in Module 3, the student must follow this **5-step structured logic**:

1. **Identify the Basic Size** (nominal diameter, e.g., 50 mm, 80 mm).
2. **Identify the Fit Type** (Clearance, Transition, or Interference) and the **System** (Hole-basis or Shaft-basis).
3. **Identify the Tolerance Grade (IT grade)** for the hole and shaft from the designation (e.g., H7/g6, H8/f7).
4. **Compute Fundamental Deviation**:
   - For holes **H**: Lower deviation = **0**, Upper deviation = +IT (hole).
   - For shafts **a–h**: Upper deviation = Fundamental deviation (upper), Lower = Upper − IT (shaft).
   - For shafts **j–zc**: Lower deviation = Fundamental deviation (lower), Upper = Lower + IT (shaft).
5. **Calculate Limits, Tolerances, Allowances, and Clearances/Interferences** using the formulas below.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit |
|---|-----------|-------------------|------|
| 1 | Tolerance (T) | $T = \text{High Limit} - \text{Low Limit}$ | mm |
| 2 | Allowance (A) | $A = \text{Min clearance (clearance fit)}$ or $A = \text{Max interference (interference fit)}$ | mm |
| 3 | Max Clearance (Clearance Fit) | $C_{max} = H_{max} - S_{min}$ | mm |
| 4 | Min Clearance (Clearance Fit) | $C_{min} = H_{min} - S_{max}$ | mm |
| 5 | Max Interference (Interference Fit) | $I_{max} = S_{max} - H_{min}$ | mm |
| 6 | Min Interference (Interference Fit) | $I_{min} = S_{min} - H_{max}$ | mm |
| 7 | IT Grade Formula (for $D \leq 500$ mm) | $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$ | µm |
| 8 | IT Grade Standard Multipliers | $IT5 = 7i, \ IT6 = 10i, \ IT7 = 16i, \ IT8 = 25i, \ IT9 = 40i$ | µm |
| 9 | Hole-Basis System | Lower deviation of hole $H = 0$ | mm |
| 10 | Shaft-Basis System | Upper deviation of shaft $h = 0$ | mm |
| 11 | Lower Deviation (shaft) | $\Delta_{lower} = \Delta_{upper} - IT_{shaft}$ | mm |
| 12 | Upper Deviation (shaft) | $\Delta_{upper} = \Delta_{lower} + IT_{shaft}$ | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These calculations are the backbone of **CNC machining, automotive gear assemblies, aerospace turbine fits, and bearing manufacturing**. Without numerical tolerance calculations, mass production of interchangeable parts (the foundation of the modern assembly line, pioneered by Henry Ford) would be impossible.

### 2.3 Types of Tolerance Schemes

- **Unilateral Tolerance**: Tolerance is applied in only one direction from the basic size (e.g., $50^{+0.025}_{0}$ mm). Common in precision engineering.
- **Bilateral Tolerance**: Tolerance is distributed on both sides of the basic size (e.g., $50^{\pm 0.025}$ mm).
- **Asymmetric (Preference) Bilateral**: Most tolerance is on one side, small part on the other (e.g., $50^{+0.020}_{-0.005}$ mm). Most common in industry.

> [!NOTE]
> **International Tolerance (IT) Grade — Recall Tip:**
> Lower IT number = Tighter (finer) tolerance = Higher precision machining required.
> For 50 mm shaft: IT5 ≈ 11 µm, IT7 ≈ 25 µm, IT11 ≈ 160 µm.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Solved Numerical Problems)

### 3.1 PROBLEM 1: Basic Limits and Fit Calculation (KTU Frequently Asked)

**Problem Statement:**
A hole and shaft assembly is designated as **40 H7/h6**. The basic size is 40 mm. Calculate:
(a) The tolerance for the hole and shaft.
(b) The maximum and minimum clearance.
(c) State the type of fit.

**Given Data:**
- Basic size $D = 40$ mm
- Hole: H7, Shaft: h6

**STEP 1: Calculate the Tolerance Unit (i) in micrometers (µm)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{40} + 0.001 \cdot 40 \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot (3.4199) + 0.040 \\
i &= 1.5390 + 0.040 \\
i &= 1.579 \ \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 2: Calculate IT Grades**

For IT7 (Hole):
$$
\begin{aligned}
IT7 &= 16i \\
IT7 &= 16 \times 1.579 \\
IT7 &= 25.26 \ \mu m = 0.02526 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

For IT6 (Shaft):
$$
\begin{aligned}
IT6 &= 10i \\
IT6 &= 10 \times 1.579 \\
IT6 &= 15.79 \ \mu m = 0.01579 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 3: Calculate Limits of Hole (H7)**

For **Hole H**, the lower deviation is **0** (zero line is the lower limit).
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= D + 0 + IT7 = 40 + 0 + 0.02526 = 40.02526 \ mm \\
H_{min} &= D + 0 = 40.000 \ mm \\
\text{Hole tolerance, } T_h &= IT7 = 0.02526 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 4: Calculate Limits of Shaft (h6)**

For **Shaft h**, the upper deviation is **0**.
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= D + 0 = 40.000 \ mm \\
S_{min} &= D - IT6 = 40 - 0.01579 = 39.98421 \ mm \\
\text{Shaft tolerance, } T_s &= IT6 = 0.01579 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 5: Calculate Clearances**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= H_{max} - S_{min} \\
C_{max} &= 40.02526 - 39.98421 \\
C_{max} &= 0.04105 \ mm \\
\\
C_{min} &= H_{min} - S_{max} \\
C_{min} &= 40.000 - 40.000 \\
C_{min} &= 0 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 6: State the Type of Fit**

Since $C_{max} > 0$ and $C_{min} = 0$, this is a **Snug (Sliding) Fit** — a clearance fit where minimum clearance is zero, used for parts requiring accurate location with occasional sliding motion (e.g., clutch hubs).

---

### 3.2 PROBLEM 2: Interference Fit (Press Fit) — KTU Model Problem

**Problem Statement:**
A shaft of **Ø60 mm** basic size has a tolerance designation of **s7** and the hole is **H7**. Calculate the maximum and minimum interference and tolerance for both.

**Given Data:**
- Basic size $D = 60$ mm
- Designation: **Ø60 H7/s7**

**STEP 1: Calculate Tolerance Unit (i)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{60} + 0.001 \cdot 60 \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot 3.9148 + 0.060 \\
i &= 1.7617 + 0.060 = 1.8217 \ \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 2: Calculate IT7**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IT7 &= 16i = 16 \times 1.8217 = 29.147 \ \mu m = 0.02915 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 3: Hole H7 Limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= 60.02915 \ mm \\
H_{min} &= 60.000 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 4: Shaft s7 — Lower Deviation (Fundamental)**

For shaft **'s'**, the lower deviation is given by the IS 919 standard. For nominal size 60 mm, fundamental deviation 's' (lower deviation) ≈ **+43 µm = +0.043 mm** (standard tables give this value for size 50–80 mm range).

**Lower deviation of shaft s7** = +0.043 mm
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{min} &= D + 0.043 = 60.043 \ mm \\
S_{max} &= S_{min} + IT7 = 60.043 + 0.02915 = 60.07215 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 5: Calculate Interferences**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{max} &= S_{max} - H_{min} = 60.07215 - 60.000 = 0.07215 \ mm \\
I_{min} &= S_{min} - H_{max} = 60.043 - 60.02915 = 0.01385 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 6: Type of Fit**

Since the shaft is always larger than the hole ($S > H$), this is an **Interference Fit (Force Fit / Press Fit)** — used for permanent assembly, e.g., bushing in gear blanks.

---

### 3.3 PROBLEM 3: Transition Fit Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
A **Ø25 H7/k6** fit is to be analyzed. Calculate limits, maximum clearance, and maximum interference.

**STEP 1: Calculate Tolerance Unit (i) for D = 25 mm**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{25} + 0.001 \times 25 \\
i &= 0.45 \times 2.9240 + 0.025 \\
i &= 1.3158 + 0.025 = 1.3408 \ \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 2: Calculate IT6 and IT7**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IT6 &= 10i = 10 \times 1.3408 = 13.408 \ \mu m = 0.01341 \ mm \\
IT7 &= 16i = 16 \times 1.3408 = 21.453 \ \mu m = 0.02145 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 3: Hole H7 Limits**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= 25 + 0 + 0.02145 = 25.02145 \ mm \\
H_{min} &= 25.000 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 4: Shaft k6 — Fundamental Deviation**

For shaft 'k' (size range 18–30 mm), the lower fundamental deviation is approximately **+9 µm = +0.009 mm**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{min} &= 25 + 0.009 = 25.009 \ mm \\
S_{max} &= 25.009 + 0.01341 = 25.02241 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 5: Calculate Clearance and Interference**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= H_{max} - S_{min} = 25.02145 - 25.009 = 0.01245 \ mm \quad (\text{Clearance}) \\
I_{max} &= S_{max} - H_{min} = 25.02241 - 25.000 = 0.02241 \ mm \quad (\text{Interference})
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 6: Type of Fit**

Since clearance and interference both exist, this is a **Transition Fit** — used for parts requiring accurate location with occasional removal (e.g., gear drives, coupling hubs).

---

### 3.4 PROBLEM 4: Numerical on Tolerance and Allowance (Unilateral & Bilateral)

**Problem Statement:**
A shaft of nominal diameter **50 mm** is manufactured with:
- Unilateral tolerance of **+0.030 mm**, and
- A hole with bilateral tolerance of **±0.020 mm**.

Calculate: (a) Maximum clearance, (b) Minimum clearance, (c) Type of fit, (d) Allowance.

**STEP 1: Determine the Limits**

Shaft (Unilateral Tolerance, all positive — typical of "below" the basic line, but here all upper):
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= 50 + 0.030 = 50.030 \ mm \\
S_{min} &= 50 + 0.000 = 50.000 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

Hole (Bilateral Tolerance):
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= 50 + 0.020 = 50.020 \ mm \\
H_{min} &= 50 - 0.020 = 49.980 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 2: Calculate Clearances**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= H_{max} - S_{min} = 50.020 - 50.000 = 0.020 \ mm \\
C_{min} &= H_{min} - S_{max} = 49.980 - 50.030 = -0.050 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**STEP 3: Interpret the Result**

Since $C_{min}$ is negative ($−0.050$ mm), it indicates **interference**, not clearance. So this is actually a **Transition Fit** with:
- Maximum interference = **0.050 mm**
- Maximum clearance = **0.020 mm**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** When $C_{min}$ comes out negative, students often forget to convert it into a corresponding interference. Always interpret the sign of the result carefully — negative clearance = interference.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Flow of Solving Limits & Fits Numerical Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read Problem Statement] --> B[Identify Basic Size D in mm]
    B --> C[Identify Fit Designation e.g. H7/g6]
    C --> D{Identify Fit Type?}
    D -- H followed by h to h or H/h --> E[Clearance Fit Region]
    D -- H followed by j to zc --> F[Interference or Transition Fit]
    E --> G[Compute Tolerance Unit i]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute IT6 IT7 IT8 using Multipliers]
    H --> I[Set Hole Lower Deviation = 0]
    I --> J[Compute Hole Max and Min Limits]
    J --> K[Set Shaft Deviation per Fundamental Table]
    K --> L[Compute Shaft Max and Min Limits]
    L --> M[Calculate Clearance or Interference]
    M --> N[State Final Fit Type and Allowance]
    N --> O[End]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Hole-Shaft Tolerance Zone Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Basic Size D Zero Line] --> B[Hole H Lower Deviation = 0]
    A --> C[Shaft h Upper Deviation = 0]
    B --> D[Hole Max Limit = D + IT Hole]
    C --> E[Shaft Min Limit = D - IT Shaft]
    D --> F[Clearance Zone]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Calculate Cmax and Cmin]
    G --> H{Cmin greater than 0?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Clearance Fit]
    H -- No --> J{Smax greater than Hmin?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Transition Fit]
    J -- No --> L[Interference Fit]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Mapping of IT Grade Tolerance Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Basic Size Line] --> B[IT5 = 7i Tightest]
    A --> C[IT6 = 10i]
    A --> D[IT7 = 16i General Engineering]
    A --> E[IT8 = 25i]
    A --> F[IT9 = 40i Coarse]
    A --> G[IT11 onwards Rough Machining]
    B --> H[Used in Bearings and Gauges]
    C --> H
    D --> I[Most Common Grade for Holes]
    E --> J[Used in Sheet Metal and Castings]
    F --> J
    G --> K[Sand Castings and Forgings]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the terms: (i) Allowance, (ii) Tolerance, (iii) Fundamental Deviation.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Allowance**: The minimum clearance (in clearance fits) or maximum interference (in interference fits) between the mating parts. It is the intentional difference prescribed between maximum material conditions.
- **Tolerance**: The total permissible variation in the size of a part. Mathematically, **Tolerance = High Limit − Low Limit**. It represents the precision grade (IT01 to IT18).
- **Fundamental Deviation**: The deviation (upper or lower) of the tolerance zone that is closest to the zero (basic size) line. It defines the position of the tolerance zone relative to the basic size.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is the difference between a unilateral and a bilateral tolerance system? Give one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Unilateral Tolerance**: The tolerance is allowed in only **one direction** from the basic size. Example: $50^{+0.030}_{0}$ mm (tolerance is only positive). Used in precision engineering to maintain a specific functional fit.
- **Bilateral Tolerance**: The tolerance is distributed on **both sides** of the basic size. Example: $50^{\pm 0.020}$ mm. Used in general engineering where some deviation in either direction is acceptable.
- **Asymmetric (Preference) Bilateral**: A special case where most tolerance lies on one side. Example: $50^{+0.025}_{-0.005}$ mm. This is the **most commonly preferred** in industry.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Full Solutions Provided)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**

**Question:**
A hole-shaft assembly has a basic size of **80 mm** with designation **H7/g6**.
(a) Calculate the maximum and minimum clearance and the tolerance for both hole and shaft. State the type of fit. (7 marks)
(b) If the fit is changed to **H7/s7**, calculate the maximum and minimum interference. (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Basic size $D = 80$ mm

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Tolerance Unit (i)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{80} + 0.001 \times 80 \\
i &= 0.45 \times 4.3089 + 0.080 = 2.0190 \ \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: IT6 and IT7**
$$
\begin{aligned}
IT6 &= 10i = 10 \times 2.0190 = 20.19 \ \mu m = 0.02019 \ mm \\
IT7 &= 16i = 16 \times 2.0190 = 32.30 \ \mu m = 0.03230 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Hole H7 Limits**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= 80.000 + 0.03230 = 80.03230 \ mm \\
H_{min} &= 80.000 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Shaft g6 Limits**

For shaft 'g' (size range 65–80 mm), fundamental deviation (upper) ≈ **−12 µm = −0.012 mm**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= 80 - 0.012 = 79.988 \ mm \\
S_{min} &= S_{max} - IT6 = 79.988 - 0.02019 = 79.96781 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Clearance**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= H_{max} - S_{min} = 80.03230 - 79.96781 = 0.06449 \ mm \\
C_{min} &= H_{min} - S_{max} = 80.000 - 79.988 = 0.012 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Type of Fit**
$C_{min} = 0.012 \ mm > 0$, hence this is a **Clearance Fit — Sliding Fit** (used where parts must slide freely with small clearance, e.g., pulleys on shafts).

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Designation: 80 H7/s7**

For shaft 's' (size range 65–80 mm), fundamental deviation (lower) ≈ **+53 µm = +0.053 mm**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{min} &= 80 + 0.053 = 80.053 \ mm \\
S_{max} &= S_{min} + IT7 = 80.053 + 0.03230 = 80.08530 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interference Calculations:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{max} &= S_{max} - H_{min} = 80.08530 - 80.000 = 0.08530 \ mm \\
I_{min} &= S_{min} - H_{max} = 80.053 - 80.03230 = 0.02070 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Type of Fit: Interference Fit (Heavy Press Fit)** — used for permanent joints subjected to high torque, e.g., railway wheel hubs.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**

**Question:**
A nominal diameter of **45 mm** is designated as **Ø45 H8/h7**.
(a) Determine the maximum and minimum limits of both hole and shaft, tolerance, and allowance. State the type of fit. (7 marks)
(b) For the same nominal size, if a transition fit **H7/k6** is to be analyzed, determine the maximum clearance and maximum interference. (7 marks)

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Step 1: Tolerance Unit (i) for D = 45 mm**
$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{45} + 0.001 \times 45 \\
i &= 0.45 \times 3.5569 + 0.045 = 1.6456 \ \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: IT7 and IT8**
$$
\begin{aligned}
IT7 &= 16i = 16 \times 1.6456 = 26.330 \ \mu m = 0.02633 \ mm \\
IT8 &= 25i = 25 \times 1.6456 = 41.140 \ \mu m = 0.04114 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Hole H8 Limits**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= 45 + 0 + 0.04114 = 45.04114 \ mm \\
H_{min} &= 45.000 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Shaft h7 Limits**

For shaft 'h', upper deviation = 0.
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{max} &= 45.000 \ mm \\
S_{min} &= 45.000 - 0.02633 = 44.97367 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Allowance (Min Clearance)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= H_{max} - S_{min} = 45.04114 - 44.97367 = 0.06747 \ mm \\
C_{min} &= H_{min} - S_{max} = 45.000 - 45.000 = 0 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Allowance = $C_{min} = 0$ mm, Type: Snug Fit / Sliding Fit**

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Designation: 45 H7/k6**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IT6 &= 10i = 10 \times 1.6456 = 16.456 \ \mu m = 0.01646 \ mm \\
IT7 &= 0.02633 \ mm \text{ (already calculated)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hole H7:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= 45.02633 \ mm \\
H_{min} &= 45.000 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Shaft k6:**

For shaft 'k' (size range 30–50 mm), fundamental deviation (lower) ≈ **+13 µm = +0.013 mm**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{min} &= 45 + 0.013 = 45.013 \ mm \\
S_{max} &= 45.013 + 0.01646 = 45.02946 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Results:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= H_{max} - S_{min} = 45.02633 - 45.013 = 0.01333 \ mm \\
I_{max} &= S_{max} - H_{min} = 45.02946 - 45.000 = 0.02946 \ mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Type of Fit: Transition Fit (Locational Transition Fit).**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (–2 marks)**: Tolerance unit $i$ is in **micrometers (µm)**, but final limits are reported in **millimeters (mm)**. Always convert: divide µm by 1000.
> 2. **Sign of Deviation (–2 marks)**: For shafts 'a' to 'h', the fundamental deviation is **upper** (negative). For shafts 'j' to 'zc', it is **lower** (positive). Mixing this up gives a totally wrong fit.
> 3. **IT Grade Reference Table (–3 marks)**: In KTU exams, you may be allowed to use a standard table for IT multipliers. Memorize: IT5=7i, IT6=10i, IT7=16i, IT8=25i, IT9=40i. Other grades (e.g., IT10=64i) are rarely asked.
> 4. **Forgot the Allowance (–1 mark)**: After computing clearance, ALWAYS explicitly state the "Allowance" as the minimum clearance (or maximum interference for interference fits). KTU evaluators check this separately.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Tolerance Unit Formula**: $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$ (valid for $D$ from 0 to 500 mm), answer in **µm**.
- **IT Grade Multipliers to Memorize**: IT5 = 7i, IT6 = 10i, IT7 = 16i, IT8 = 25i, IT9 = 40i.
- **Hole H is the Anchor**: In a hole-basis system, the hole's lower deviation is always **0**; this is your reference zero line.
- **Shaft h is the Anchor**: In a shaft-basis system, the shaft's upper deviation is always **0**.
- **Direction of Fundamental Deviation**:
  - Shafts **a–h** → Upper deviation is the fundamental (and is negative).
  - Shafts **j–zc** → Lower deviation is the fundamental (and is positive).
- **Fit Classification Rule**:
  - $C_{min} \geq 0$ → **Clearance Fit**.
  - $C_{min} < 0$ and $C_{max} > 0$ → **Transition Fit**.
  - $C_{max} < 0$ → **Interference Fit**.
- **Three Magic Steps**: (1) Calculate $i$, (2) Multiply to get IT grade, (3) Apply deviations → Limits.
- **Final Check**: Always verify that Hole limits > Shaft limits for clearance fits, and Shaft limits > Hole limits for interference fits.
- **Industry Note**: H7/g6 is the most common clearance fit in mechanical assemblies. H7/s7 is a standard press fit. H7/k6 is the textbook transition fit.
- **KTU 2024 Trend**: The examiner often gives a two-part problem where Part (a) is a clearance fit and Part (b) asks to change the fit type and re-compute. Practice this switch confidently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limit Gauging - Classification of Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET402 - MACHINE TOOLS AND METROLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Limit Gauging - Classification of Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Limit Gauging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limit Gauging (per IS 3455:1971 & KTU 2024 Syllabus)**
> Limit gauging is an **indirect method of dimensional inspection** in which a workpiece is accepted or rejected based on whether it falls within the prescribed limits of size (tolerance zone). It is performed using *limit gauges* that have fixed GO and NO-GO members, rather than measuring the actual dimension.

**Limit Gauge:** A gauging member that has two functional sides — a **GO side** (which must pass) and a **NO-GO side** (which must not pass) — to verify whether a dimension lies within its tolerance limits.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **door frame** that must be wide enough for a wheelchair to pass. The builder doesn't measure with a tape every time. Instead, he makes a **GO template** (the wheelchair itself, which *must* pass through) and a **NO-GO template** (a slightly larger dummy, which *must not* pass). If the GO template goes through and the NO-GO does not, the doorway is correct.

In manufacturing:
- **GO gauge** = the smallest acceptable hole / largest acceptable shaft (Maximum Material Condition — MMC).
- **NO-GO gauge** = the largest acceptable hole / smallest acceptable shaft (Least Material Condition — LMC).

## 1.3 Taylor's Principle of Limit Gauging

> [!NOTE]
> **Taylor's Principle (1905)** — The foundation of all KTU limit-gauge design.
> *"The GO gauge should be designed to check the **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)** and the NO-GO gauge should check the **Least Material Condition (LMC)**. The GO and NO-GO members should never be combined on a single end, except as a progressive or trilock gauge under specific design rules."*

### Functional Implications
| Feature | GO Gauge | NO-GO Gauge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Checks | MMC (most material) | LMC (least material) |
| For a Hole (Plug) | Must **enter** the hole | Must **not enter** |
| For a Shaft (Ring) | Must **pass over** the shaft | Must **not pass** over |
| Wear Allowance | Provided | **Not** provided |
| Form | Full cylindrical/ring | Cylindrical (short, stubby) |

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standards
- **Standard Wear Allowance (per IS 3455):** *W* ranges from **1% to 5% of the tolerance band**, depending on the nominal size.
- **Maximum Permissible Wear (MPW)** on a GO plug gauge: limited to **5% of the tolerance band** under IS 3455.
- **Gauge Tolerance:** typically **10% of the workpiece tolerance (IT)** for the GO gauge, and the same for the NO-GO gauge on the rejection side only.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logic Flow of Limit Gauging

1. **Identify the feature type** — internal (hole) or external (shaft).
2. **Determine the tolerance limits** from the drawing:
   - Hole: $D_{max} = D + \text{upper deviation}$, $D_{min} = D - \text{lower deviation}$
   - Shaft: $d_{max} = d + \text{upper deviation}$, $d_{min} = d - \text{lower deviation}$
3. **Apply Taylor's Principle:**
   - For a hole → GO plug diameter = $D_{min}$ ; NO-GO plug diameter = $D_{max}$
   - For a shaft → GO ring diameter = $d_{max}$ ; NO-GO ring diameter = $d_{min}$
4. **Apply gauge tolerance (g)** to the GO gauge only (in modern practice, to both, on the appropriate sides).
5. **Add Wear Allowance (W)** to the GO gauge in a way that does not change its functional diameter.
6. **Choose the physical form** of the gauge (plug, ring, snap) per the part geometry and production volume.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notes / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | GO Plug (hole) diameter | $D_{GO} = D_{min}$ | Diameter in mm |
| 2 | NO-GO Plug (hole) diameter | $D_{NG} = D_{max}$ | Diameter in mm |
| 3 | GO Ring (shaft) diameter | $d_{GO} = d_{max}$ | Diameter in mm |
| 4 | NO-GO Ring (shaft) diameter | $d_{NG} = d_{min}$ | Diameter in mm |
| 5 | Workpiece Tolerance | $IT = D_{max} - D_{min}$ | mm |
| 6 | Gauge Maker's Tolerance | $g \approx 0.10 \cdot IT$ | mm (IS 3455) |
| 7 | Standard Wear Allowance (Plug) | $W = 0.05 \cdot IT$ (approx.) | mm; added as **axial length** |
| 8 | GO Plug actual length | $L_{GO} = W + (L - W)$ | Includes wear skirt |
| 9 | GO Ring wear allowance (radial) | $\Delta d = W$ (added outward) | mm |
| 10 | Acceptance rate after wear | $P_{acc} = \dfrac{IT - W}{IT} \times 100\%$ | % of tolerance still usable |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic — "MIN GOES IN, MAX GOES OVER"** → for a *hole* the *minimum* diameter is the GO; for a *shaft* the *maximum* diameter is the GO.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mass production** (automotive, bearing, fasteners): Limit gauging reduces inspection time by ~80% compared to micrometer-based checking.
- **ISO 2175 & IS 3455** compliance: All gauges in Indian manufacturing must be designed using Taylor's Principle.
- **Interchangeability**: Ensures parts from different machines fit together (the historical foundation of the assembly line).
- **Quality Cost Reduction**: Limit gauges cost 5–10× less than CMMs and require no operator skill in reading values.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of GO / NO-GO Diameters from a Drawing

### Problem Statement
A hole is specified as **$\phi 50^{+0.039}_{+0.000}$ mm**. Design the GO and NO-GO plug gauges per Taylor's Principle (IS 3455). Take gauge tolerance $g = 0.005$ mm and wear allowance $W = 0.002$ mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the limits of the hole:**
$$
D_{max} = 50.000 + 0.039 = 50.039 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
D_{min} = 50.000 + 0.000 = 50.000 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Workpiece tolerance:**
$$
IT = D_{max} - D_{min} = 50.039 - 50.000 = 0.039 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Taylor's Principle for the GO plug (must enter the smallest hole = $D_{min}$):**
$$
D_{GO} = D_{min} = 50.000 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — NO-GO plug (must not enter the largest hole = $D_{max}$):**
$$
D_{NG} = D_{max} = 50.039 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply gauge maker's tolerance $g = 0.005$ mm (worn side for GO, rejection side for NO-GO):**
- The GO plug is made slightly **smaller** so that even at the worn-out state, it doesn't reject a good part:
$$
D_{GO,\text{maker}} = D_{GO} - g = 50.000 - 0.005 = 49.995 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6 — NO-GO plug tolerance is applied on the *plus* side (so it doesn't accept oversize holes):**
$$
D_{NG,\text{maker}} = D_{NG} + 0 = 50.039 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{strict — no tolerance on rejection side})
$$

**Step 7 — Wear Allowance in the GO plug (applied as length, not diameter):**
If the original GO plug length needed is $L = 20$ mm:
$$
L_{GO,\text{new}} = W + (L - W) = W + L_{active} = 0.002 + (20 - 0.002) \approx 20.000 \text{ mm}
$$
The wear skirt of **0.002 mm** protrudes at the entry side so that as the gauge wears, the diameter is maintained for the active gauging length.

## 3.2 Derivation for a Shaft (Ring Gauge)

### Problem
A shaft is specified as **$\phi 40^{-0.025}_{-0.050}$ mm** (so $d_{max}=39.975$, $d_{min}=39.950$). Design the GO/NO-GO ring gauges with wear allowance $W=0.002$ mm.

### Solution
$$
d_{max} = 40.000 - 0.025 = 39.975 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
d_{min} = 40.000 - 0.050 = 39.950 \text{ mm}
$$

**GO ring** must pass over the largest acceptable shaft:
$$
d_{GO} = d_{max} = 39.975 \text{ mm}
$$
**NO-GO ring** must not pass over the smallest acceptable shaft:
$$
d_{NG} = d_{min} = 39.950 \text{ mm}
$$

**Wear Allowance on GO ring (radial, outward):**
$$
d_{GO,\text{worn}} = d_{GO} + W = 39.975 + 0.002 = 39.977 \text{ mm}
$$
The diameter is intentionally *over-sized* by $W$ so that when wear of $W$ occurs, the gauge is still at exactly $d_{max}$.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Plug vs Ring vs Snap Gauges

| Criterion | Plug Gauge | Ring Gauge | Snap Gauge |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feature checked | Internal (Hole) | External (Shaft) | External (Shaft) |
| Production rate | Slow (insertion) | Slow (sliding) | Fast (pass-through) |
| Wear location | Outer cylindrical surface | Inner bore | Two parallel jaws |
| Suitable size | Small to medium | Small to large | All sizes |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Low–High (adjustable) |
| Accuracy achievable | $\pm 2 \text{ \mu m}$ | $\pm 2 \text{ \mu m}$ | $\pm 5 \text{ \mu m}$ |
